paragraph_text: The year ahead is one of many challenges, and we are building a significant portion of our future. For our rural areas, we must build access to mobile and digital technology, to transportation, and foster greater economic and social innovation. For our disadvantaged neighborhoods, we must enable economic and social mobility and fight against discrimination. For our farmers, we must ensure they can earn a dignified living from the prices they receive. For our overseas territories, which have suffered greatly in recent months, I want to extend a special greeting. We must adapt our regulations and build strong economic sectors that promote greater energy independence and job creation. For equality between men and women, we will also need legal changes and a renewed commitment from our entire society. For entrepreneurs and self-employed individuals, we will simplify regulations, finally realizing a right to make mistakes. For employees, we will enable lifelong learning and provide new forms of security. And for our civil servants, we will clarify their roles and our expectations, and reward their efforts. As you can see, all of these projects will shape the year 2018, and will require a full and unwavering commitment from the Prime Minister and his government.

paragraph_text: French women and men, my dear compatriots from mainland France and overseas territories, faithful to a tradition that is dear to us, I am pleased to offer you all my best wishes for the year ahead. The year 2018 was not without its share of intense emotions of all kinds. France experienced moments of great significance: sporting victories, major cultural events, and the celebration of the centenary of the Armistice of 1918, and on that occasion, I gave voice to France’s commitment to peace. The Prime Minister, his Government, and Parliament, in 2018, did a great deal for the country. I will not list everything here, but many transformations, previously considered impossible, such as those concerning labor laws and the railways, were successfully implemented. They launched a strong initiative for our education system, our universities, vocational training, and attracting talent to our country. They laid the foundations for an ambitious strategy to improve the organization of our hospitals, clinics, and medical professionals, to combat climate change, eradicate extreme poverty, and to enable our citizens with disabilities to find their place in society. The results cannot be immediate, and impatience – which I share – must not lead to any renunciation. In the coming months, the Government must continue this work to anchor many of these reforms in our daily lives, as well as to fundamentally change the rules governing unemployment benefits to encourage a return to work, to reorganize the public sector to make it more efficient, and to reform our pension system to make it fairer. Ultimately, to build the new safeguards of the 21st century. But we also experienced significant divisions, and anger erupted, stemming from deep-seated causes: anger against injustice, against the pace of globalization, which is sometimes difficult to understand; anger against an administrative system that has become too complex and lacks empathy; and anger, too, against profound changes that challenge our society’s identity and purpose. This anger, whatever its excesses and excesses may be, told me one thing: we are not resigned; our country wants to build a better future, based on our ability to invent new ways of working together and being together. That, in my view, is the lesson of 2018: we want to change things to live better, to defend our ideals, and we want to innovate in terms of democracy, social policy, economics, and the environment.

paragraph_text: First, a wish for truth. Yes, in 2019, we should wish to never forget that nothing is built on lies or ambiguities. No, we must, on these issues, look at ourselves honestly and accept reality. We live in one of the world’s largest economies, our infrastructure is among the best in the world, we do not pay, or almost do not pay, for our children’s education, we receive healthcare at some of the lowest costs in developed countries, with access to excellent doctors. We spend more than half of what we produce each year on our public sector, both in operating and investment costs. So, let us stop… let us stop deceiving ourselves or pretending that France is a country where solidarity does not exist and where we must always spend more! We can do better, and we must do better: we must ensure that our public services remain accessible everywhere they are needed, that doctors settle in areas where they are lacking – in some rural areas or in cities and neighborhoods where there are none left – that we can have mobile phone and internet access wherever we live and work. And, above all, that we can live in safety and tranquility everywhere. I will personally ensure that, every day. The national debate that is about to begin must allow us to speak the truth, and I will write to you in a few days to outline its objectives.

paragraph_text: Another reason for hope is that, despite the health crisis and the fatigue, our country continues to move forward. We have not stopped working to attract businesses and investments, open factories, and create jobs. Never in the last fifteen years has unemployment been so low. The reindustrialization of our country is a reality. We have protected workers, helped those least well-off, and invested to defend the dignity of our fellow citizens with disabilities. We have taken clear measures to better protect our children and support our elderly. We have trained our young people. Indeed, who would have thought that we would be able, in just five years, to double the number of our apprentices: nearly 700, 000 apprentices in the last twelve months? Who would have thought we would be able to create a program, "One Young Person, One Solution, " which, in just a year and a half, has supported more than 3 million French people towards employment or training? Where we might have postponed things, we have never abandoned our collective ambition. Through reforms such as the unemployment insurance system and the "Youth Commitment" program, which will be implemented at the beginning of March, we have addressed purchasing power with the inflation compensation, the energy check, and the increase in salaries for the lowest-paid civil servants. We have tackled the ecological and climate emergency with the renewal of our car fleet, the development of renewable energy sources, the thermal renovation of housing, and, as of January 1st, the end of plastic packaging, as well as new, unprecedented measures for the well-being of our animals. Our agriculture, with the crop insurance program; the minimum pension at 1000 euros; the deployment of 2000 "France Services" centers; the automatic payment of child support, particularly benefiting single mothers; the free contraception for women up to 25 years old; and the reform of the state, of our senior civil service, including the creation of the National Institute of Public Service – just in recent weeks and in the coming months, decisions that have been discussed for decades have been taken and will be taken, decisions that will change lives. France, despite the challenges, is stronger today than it was two years ago. All of this is thanks to you, thanks to all of us, thanks to our resilience, our solidarity, our civic engagement, our commitment, and our entrepreneurial spirit.

paragraph_text: 2022 must be a year of a turning point for Europe. Our continent has been so often criticized in recent years. Without Europe, we could not have built, across our continent, some of the most ambitious economic recovery plans in the world, and we would not be seeing the economic results – growth and job creation – that we are currently experiencing. Therefore, from midnight, France will assume the Presidency of the European Union, and you can count on my full commitment to make this moment, which occurs only once every 13 years, a time of progress for you. Yes, the values that our Union embodies – democracy, the balance between freedom and solidarity, a particular vision of humanity – I am convinced that these are the values that will enable us to meet our contemporary challenges. paragraph_text: Now, let’s look to the future. First, if I may, let me share an old thought of mine, one I had when I was still Minister of the Interior: let’s look at the Mediterranean. Today, in this sea, how much bloodshed, how much tragedy, how much! I know that when this topic is raised, various states, or perhaps a particular state, fear that someone will be poking their nose into their affairs. No, no! But I must say, with great clarity, that Europe, the European Community, cannot stand by and watch, it cannot, while people are being massacred, hundreds, thousands. It is not civilized, it is not humane, it is unthinkable. We need to find a way that is not about intruding where we have no right to, but also about preventing ourselves from standing by and watching. This sea, just the other day, served as a route for Kurds to arrive; and how many times have people of color from various parts arrived? And these criminals – a very mild term – these mercenaries, who prey on these people, then sink the ship and flee with the money. This is incredible criminality. Why can’t the countries find a common ground to find a way to punish this criminality, this exploitation of vulnerable people? Italy extends its hand. I know that there are many sensitive issues. It seems to me that the Ministry of the Interior has found a point of equilibrium. It is always difficult, without a doubt, but if people are coming because they are experiencing persecution, then the doors must be opened wide. Those who fought against the dictatorship also found open doors. It would be wrong to close the doors to those who are persecuted, to those who flee in order to survive. It is not humane! But, let me be clear, let me be clear about a certain type of politics that, in an attempt to punish states that are accused of not respecting human rights, ultimately proposes isolation. Be careful! I don’t believe in that policy. It must not happen anywhere! I don’t believe in it. I don’t think it does any good to the European Union if, at a certain moment, with that motivation. . . We must never break off dialogue. We must never! And so, let’s look at the problems from a broader perspective. It is not just a problem for the countries that border it. No. I feel it is my duty to express my gratitude to President Herzog, President of Germany. paragraph_text: We are confronted with the world’s major problems. Science has reached the forefront of understanding life, both animal and plant. paragraph_text: The 20th century can be clearly divided into two parts. The first was marked by two terrible wars, which erupted in Europe and engulfed and shook the entire world. paragraph_text: For a man of my generation, the word "war" evokes many memories. Starting with the summer of 1939: the outbreak of the Second World War. I experienced that summer, together with young people from many European countries, at the University of Bonn in Germany. We were studying German. For me – I was nineteen years old – it was meant to deepen my knowledge of classical philology, the discipline I had chosen. In the mornings, we, along with our peers from France, Italy, Belgium, and England, attended university lectures. In the afternoons, we would meet on the banks of the Rhine, sometimes with German friends. We talked, with a mixture of disbelief and unease, with the carefree spirit of youth, about the storm that was about to break upon our heads, a storm that could lead us to fight each other. And the unthinkable happened. Those who were fortunate enough to survive – and we must never forget the faces of our companions who were lost in their youth – made a vow in their hearts: never again war between us. In our hearts, a passion was ignited, a passion that has never been extinguished. It is the passion that gave rise to the European Union. At the heart of its success lies the principle that inspired its first creation, the European Coal and Steel Community: to pool resources, rather than divide them. Initially, coal and steel were pooled; now, it is the currency, the euro.

paragraph_text: In recent European and American, and Russian years, we have worked together in the Balkans, where senseless hatreds had sparked conflicts, creating a very serious threat to everyone. paragraph_text: Europe proposes to the world the principle of dialogue: starting with dialogue with the Islamic world, which is so close to us, on the other side of the Mediterranean. " I send my heartfelt greetings and best wishes to the Holy Father for the new year. Together with him, we reject any idea of a religious war. Such a war does not exist, and it will not exist; it is repugnant to our consciences and contradicts the fundamental principle of respecting the rights of every human being.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for international dialogue, peace in the Middle East, and the rejection of religious conflict in alignment with the Pope's message.
reason: The paragraph addresses international dialogue, Middle East relations, and religious conflict.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the promotion of peace and dialogue as a normative goal rather than framing the current state of affairs as an urgent, existential threat requiring immediate defensive action.

paragraph_text: We must not forget that we have, throughout the centuries, had a fruitful exchange of ideas with those lands, with those nations. Our cultures have also been protagonists in historic conflicts, but for long periods, we have built together the edifice of civilization. We share common roots in the great monotheistic religions, among which a new dialogue is now emerging. We say no to conflicts between civilizations. No to the challenge of terrorism, which can justify nothing: in God's name, we do not kill. Terrorism must be combatted through the concerted action of the international community, and by working to improve the living conditions of disadvantaged populations.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for international cooperation, interfaith dialogue, and collective action to combat terrorism while rejecting the concept of a conflict between civilizations.
reason: Addresses international relations, the dialogue between civilizations, and the global fight against terrorism.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames terrorism as a serious threat requiring collective action and international cooperation, which constitutes an active threat-focused framing.

paragraph_text: Finally, politics must look beyond Italy today, and consider the world and its future. We have built and consolidated peace in the heart of Europe, but peace has not yet been achieved beyond our borders. Tragically, we continue to receive disturbing news from Iraq. We feel a shared concern about the wars that destabilize the Middle East, and the challenges facing Africa – most recently, once again, in Somalia – as well as other regions. The international community, and particularly Europe and Italy, cannot stand idly by in the face of these conflicts, or the risk of nuclear proliferation. We must actively promote peace, stability, disarmament, and development, and support human rights everywhere. Therefore, it is right to intensify international cooperation initiatives and participate in United Nations and European Union missions in crisis areas, such as the one recently launched in Lebanon. And it is important to do so with the professionalism and humanity that characterize our armed forces, to whom I express my sincere gratitude this evening. Decisions, such as the one regarding the most recent mission, have been made in Parliament with overwhelming majorities – a positive example of consensus between opposing political forces.
paragraph_summary: The President calls for Italy to actively promote international peace, stability, and human rights through cooperation and participation in global peacekeeping missions.
reason: The paragraph discusses international conflicts, foreign policy, and Italy's participation in global missions.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the promotion of positive international values and routine diplomatic cooperation rather than framing the current state of affairs as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: The public interest demands full support for Italy’s international efforts, and its commitment, first and foremost, within the European Union, to revitalize it and to foster a common approach to the crucial issues of international peace and security. Just a few weeks ago, when I brought a unified voice from our country to the United States, confirming Italy’s widely shared international standing, I was able to see how our closest historical ally appreciates the contributions and efforts of Italy and Europe in a world that, even today, is dramatically affected by the aggression of terrorism and by a multitude of global changes, challenges, and serious tensions. At this moment, we are more than ever close and grateful to the thousands of our military personnel who face extreme daily risks – I pay heartfelt tribute to those who have recently sacrificed their lives in these missions – and who, together with them, are tackling the demanding work of humanitarian aid in some of the most critical areas of the world. They are doing so, we are doing so, in the spirit of our Republican Constitution.
paragraph_summary: The President emphasizes Italy's commitment to European integration, international security, and the vital contributions of Italian military personnel serving in global missions.
reason: The text focuses on Italy's international standing, European Union cooperation, and military missions abroad.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on routine diplomatic commitments, international cooperation, and expressions of gratitude for military service, lacking any framing of these topics as urgent threats or dangers.

paragraph_text: This New Year's Eve is marked, in each of our hearts, by a sense of unease stemming from the news and images reaching us from the heart of the Middle East. A tragic cycle of violence and war has been reignited in that region, a cycle that must be stopped. This is the call from Italy, the European Union, the United Nations, and the Pope: today, as I speak to you, I believe our first duty is to reopen the path to peace in a region so long plagued by conflict.
paragraph_summary: The President addresses the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and emphasizes the urgent international necessity to restore peace in the region.
reason: The text addresses the international conflict in the Middle East and the call for global peace.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the conflict as an urgent international necessity, indicating a serious threat to regional stability that demands immediate attention and action.

paragraph_text: This is because the world has changed. The center of economic – and even demographic – growth has shifted away from Europe, and not only our country, but our entire continent is seeing its weight and resources diminish, and must revise the way it conceives and distributes its own well-being, and concentrate its efforts in gaining new positions and opportunities in the global competition. Without compromising the social dimension of the European model, or respecting the dignity and rights of workers.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses Europe's declining global economic and demographic influence, urging a strategic shift to maintain competitiveness while preserving social protections and workers' rights.
reason: The paragraph analyzes the shifting global economic landscape and Europe's position within it.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the decline in economic and demographic influence as an urgent threat to the nation's competitiveness and social model, necessitating a strategic shift to avoid negative consequences.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, even in times like these, there are things we can rely on. Things that make us strong: We have friends – in Europe and around the globe. Partners with whom I coordinate daily to ensure our security in Germany and Europe. The European Union makes us strong. When the EU acts in unison, it represents more than 400 million citizens. In a world with eight, and soon ten billion people, that is a significant advantage. That is why it is so important that Europe emerges united and strengthened from the European elections next year. After all, Russia’s war in eastern Europe is not over yet! The conflict in the Middle East is also ongoing. And in the United States, presidential elections will be held next year, potentially with far-reaching consequences – also for us here in Europe.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of European unity and international partnerships in response to global security challenges, including the wars in Ukraine and the Middle East.
reason: The paragraph discusses international alliances, the European Union, and foreign election impacts.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the wars in Ukraine and the Middle East as active global security challenges that necessitate a specific, urgent response through unity and partnership, indicating an ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: The brutal Russian invasion of Ukraine, the return of war to Europe, the terrible suffering of Ukrainians, and the fear of a wider conflict – all of this is unsettling and frightening to so many people in our country. Even the primary school children in Freiberg and their families in Ukraine wish for peace, perhaps even more urgently than we do. But that peace is not yet within reach. And it must be a just peace, one that does not reward land grabs or leave the people of Ukraine at the mercy of their occupiers. Until peace can truly prevail, it is our duty to stand by those who have been attacked, those who are threatened, and those who are suffering. In doing so, we offer a light of hope in the darkness of injustice.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the Russian invasion of Ukraine, emphasizing the necessity of a just peace and Germany's commitment to supporting the Ukrainian people.
reason: The paragraph focuses on the Russian invasion of Ukraine and the international geopolitical situation.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the Russian invasion of Ukraine as an ongoing threat and emphasizes the urgent necessity of a just peace and continued support, which constitutes an active threat-focused framing.

paragraph_text: It is in our own best interest to resolve all of these challenges, and we can do so most effectively by considering the interests of others. This is the lesson we learned from the two World Wars of the previous century. However, this conviction is not universally shared today, and the principles of international cooperation are under pressure. In such a situation, we must stand even more firmly behind our beliefs, argue, and fight for them. And we must take on greater responsibility, in our own interest. Germany will serve as a member of the UN Security Council for the next two years, and we will actively advocate for global solutions. We are increasing our funding for humanitarian and development aid, as well as our defense spending. We are committed to making the European Union more robust and more capable of making decisions. And, despite Brexit, we want to maintain a close partnership with Great Britain. In May, you will have the opportunity to contribute to ensuring that the European Union remains a project of peace, prosperity, and security through your participation in the European elections.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for strengthening international cooperation, European unity, and Germany's global responsibility in response to challenges facing the international order.
reason: Focuses on international cooperation, UN Security Council membership, and foreign policy objectives.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the international order as facing 'challenges' that require urgent action through unity and responsibility, treating the situation as a threat to global stability.

paragraph_text: I mention the financial crisis and the growing differences within the European Union, and the intense debates about the future of Greece. I also think of Ukraine, Syria, Afghanistan, and the regions of Africa threatened by terror. And today, on Christmas, I think especially of those people who are persecuted because of their Christian faith. I am grateful for the civilian aid workers who tirelessly work amidst hunger, hardship, and civil war. I also extend my thanks to the men and women serving in the armed forces, who are deployed in the dangerous fight against the roots of terror, which recently struck, among other places, in Paris.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses international conflicts, the European financial crisis, the persecution of Christians, and expresses gratitude toward humanitarian workers and military personnel.
reason: The text focuses on international conflicts, foreign crises, and overseas military deployments.
crisis_reason: The paragraph explicitly identifies international conflicts, a financial crisis, and the persecution of Christians as urgent, threatening situations requiring attention and response.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, 2014 will be remembered as a year that unfolded in ways we could scarcely have imagined at the turn of the year. It was a year in which we experienced, with unprecedented severity, what it means when the fundamental principles of our European peace order – namely, the free self-determination of nations – are challenged. This is precisely what Russia is doing to Ukraine. It goes without saying that we want security in Europe, not against Russia, but together with Russia. However, it also goes without saying that Europe cannot and will not accept the notion of a stronger power asserting its dominance while disregarding international law. Therefore, 2014 was also the year in which Europe recognized and embraced this challenge, working together with its transatlantic partners. Europe decided not to be divided, but to act more united than ever before, in order to defend its peace order and its values – values that underpin Europe’s future, and that of its member states, both politically and economically. This unity in Europe is not an end in itself, but it is the key to overcoming the crisis in Ukraine and upholding the rule of law.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses Russia's violation of Ukraine's sovereignty and emphasizes the necessity of European unity to uphold international law and regional peace.
reason: It discusses European security, the conflict in Ukraine, and international law.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the violation of sovereignty as a direct threat to international law and regional peace, necessitating urgent collective action and unity.

paragraph_text: 2014 was also the year in which the devastating disease Ebola struck people in West Africa on a scale never before seen. I would like to thank everyone who is contributing to containing this disease, which remains a significant challenge: the doctors, the nurses, the volunteers of the German Red Cross, and, not least, the soldiers who are dedicating their lives, here and around the world, to this effort.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude to medical professionals, volunteers, and soldiers for their efforts in containing the 2014 Ebola outbreak in West Africa.
reason: It addresses the international response to the Ebola outbreak in West Africa.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on expressing gratitude for past efforts in containing an outbreak, which frames the situation as a recovery or retrospective acknowledgment rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: A few days ago, I returned from Afghanistan. I was deeply impressed by how German soldiers are preventing terror and protecting the civilian population, often risking their own lives. My thanks go to them, as well as to the civilian aid workers there. Such a journey brings home just how precious the peace that has prevailed in Europe for over 60 years truly is. That peace has been secured by the European ideal. The European Union rightly received the Nobel Peace Prize.
paragraph_summary: The speaker honors German military and civilian efforts in Afghanistan while emphasizing the importance of European peace and the European Union's role.
reason: It discusses German military involvement in Afghanistan and the broader European peace project.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on honoring past efforts and affirming the importance of peace and the European Union, rather than framing a current or ongoing threat that requires urgent action.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, At the beginning of this year, people in North Africa and the Middle East began to fundamentally change the political landscape in their regions. In March, Japan was struck by a devastating earthquake, a horrific tsunami, and subsequently, a catastrophic nuclear accident. In the fall, the world’s seventh billionth person was born – these are just a few highlights from the past year. 2011 was, without a doubt, a year of profound change.
model_actions: Dropped count of 'earthquake' | Dropped count of 'tsunami'

crisis_reason: The paragraph provides a retrospective summary of events without framing them as active, ongoing threats requiring urgent response or sacrifice from the nation.

paragraph_text: Many of our fellow citizens are serving as soldiers, police officers, or civilian aid workers abroad, to promote development, secure peace in the world, and combat terrorism. We are thinking of them and their partners, their children, and their parents, who especially miss them during this time.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude and support for German personnel serving abroad in military, police, and aid roles, as well as their families.
reason: The focus is on the role of German personnel in international peacekeeping and security missions.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude and support for personnel, which is a ceremonial and affirmative act rather than a description of an active threat or urgent danger.

paragraph_text: I see in this crisis an opportunity as well. An opportunity for better cooperation between nations. An opportunity for a better organization of the economy and finance, where capital serves everyone, and no one feels dominated by it. If we work towards that, this crisis will make us stronger.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for international cooperation and economic reform to transform the current crisis into an opportunity for collective strength.
reason: The paragraph focuses on international cooperation and global economic reform.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly identifies the current situation as a 'crisis' and frames it as a threat that requires international cooperation and economic reform to overcome.

paragraph_text: That is my greatest wish for the new year: that Germany can continue to live in friendship with its neighbors and partners, in peace and freedom. Europe has great expectations of our country. A strong, economically and socially vibrant Germany is essential for Europe to move forward. And without a strong Europe, Germany itself will be weak. We have resolved the financial issues at the last EU summit. But because Europe as a whole must become more effective, and because we must also be aware of our shared values, it is time for concrete results to emerge from the pause in the European constitutional process.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the necessity of a strong, unified Europe and Germany's role in advancing European integration and shared values.
reason: The text focuses on Germany's role in European politics and the necessity of a strong EU.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive goal of European integration and the promotion of shared values, which is framed as an achievement or a standard diplomatic objective rather than a response to an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: On the Horn of Africa, I visited our soldiers and sailors. They told me how they celebrate Christmas on the frigate. I sent them all your greetings and thanked them for their service. Because while we are spending these days at home, they and their comrades are serving for peace and freedom in other parts of the world – far from their families and often risking their lives. Our soldiers and sailors deserve our thanks and recognition.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude and recognition for German soldiers and sailors serving abroad to maintain peace and freedom during the holiday season.
reason: The paragraph focuses on German military personnel serving in international peacekeeping missions abroad.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude and recognition for service members during the holidays, which is a ceremonial and affirmative act rather than a focus on an active threat or crisis.

paragraph_text: My fellow citizens, The images of unimaginable horror that we see every day tell us that what happens far away affects us all. Victims are people from another part of the world – and victims are also Germans, our own countrymen and women. In this shared suffering, we feel the indivisibility of our one world. This world has been struck by the worst natural disaster in living memory. Tens of thousands have died, and millions have been left homeless, losing their livelihoods completely. Our collective response must be one of global solidarity. Political divisions, religious differences, and ideological viewpoints are not important now. It is about solidarity based on a shared responsibility – for those affected who need help, and for the children who need protection. It is about alleviating suffering – now. But it is also about the future of the region. Globalization affects us all. This is a lesson we must not forget. Sadly, German tourists are among the dead. More than 1, 000 German citizens are still missing. Given the devastating destruction, we must fear that several hundred Germans will be among the dead.
model_reasons: natural disaster: refers to a specific humanitarian catastrophe, not climate change or environmental policy
paragraph_summary: The speaker calls for global solidarity and humanitarian aid following the devastating 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami that claimed the lives of many German citizens.
reason: The paragraph discusses a major international natural disaster and the need for global solidarity.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a devastating natural disaster that caused significant loss of life, framing it as an urgent situation requiring immediate humanitarian response and global solidarity.

paragraph_text: In a situation like this, international solidarity must be demonstrated. We must not leave the countries most affected by the tsunami, or the people there, alone. Not now, and not in the future. The German government has allocated 20 million euros for immediate humanitarian assistance. This will also fund the efforts of our major aid organizations, for all those affected, regardless of their country of origin. The staff of all aid organizations – including those from Germany – are performing vital and life-saving work under extremely difficult conditions. They will return home changed by their experiences. We extend our deepest gratitude and admiration to them all.
model_reasons: tsunami: refers to a specific humanitarian disaster event, not climate or environmental policy
paragraph_summary: The German government is providing humanitarian aid and expressing gratitude to relief workers following the tsunami.
reason: The focus is on international humanitarian aid and the role of German organizations in disaster relief.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the provision of aid and the expression of gratitude for relief efforts, which frames the situation as a recovery and response phase rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: In the disaster areas, it is particularly important to ensure medical care and provide safe drinking water, given the risk of epidemics. The German government has therefore decided that, in addition to the efforts of aid organizations, the German Air Force will establish field hospitals and water treatment facilities in the affected countries, and will continue to operate them for an extended period.
paragraph_summary: The German government is deploying the Air Force to provide medical care and water treatment facilities in disaster-stricken regions to prevent disease outbreaks.
reason: The paragraph details the deployment of German military resources to provide medical and water aid in foreign disaster zones.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a disaster-stricken region and the urgent need to prevent disease outbreaks, framing the situation as an acute threat to health and safety requiring immediate intervention.

paragraph_text: Given the immense damage in the region, we must also help the affected countries rebuild. The people there have not only lost their personal belongings, but the economic foundations of these countries have also been severely damaged. Therefore, we must provide German and international assistance to rebuild infrastructure, construct housing, roads, and schools. Germany will work with the European Union, the United Nations, and the World Bank to ensure that these countries receive effective and streamlined assistance. For me, this also includes continuing to forgive the debts of these countries – both bilaterally and multilaterally. They need all the resources they can access to accelerate their reconstruction.
paragraph_summary: The speaker outlines Germany's commitment to providing international financial aid, infrastructure reconstruction, and debt relief to countries affected by severe regional damage.
reason: It outlines international reconstruction efforts, debt relief, and cooperation with global institutions.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the provision of aid and reconstruction efforts, which frames the situation as a recovery or support initiative rather than an active, urgent threat requiring immediate defensive action by the speaker's state.

paragraph_text: Ultimately, we must strengthen international efforts to expand existing early warning systems for earthquakes and tsunamis, so that we can achieve effective global prevention and early warning. Germany will work towards this goal within the United Nations and with the major industrial nations.
model_reasons: tsunamis: refers to geological natural disasters, not climate change or environmental protection
paragraph_summary: Germany commits to collaborating with the United Nations and industrial nations to enhance global early warning systems for earthquakes and tsunamis.
reason: The speaker advocates for international cooperation to establish global early warning systems for natural disasters.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a proactive, routine international cooperation effort to improve disaster preparedness rather than framing a specific, urgent threat that requires immediate crisis response.

paragraph_text: When the Iraq war began in March, many of us were deeply shocked and disturbed. We are pleased with the end of a terrible dictatorship, but we recognize, once again, that weapons and military superiority alone cannot solve the world’s problems. Peace does not come through violence. Of course, there are situations where we must protect freedom and justice, even with force. Therefore, I thank all the soldiers, police officers, and the many civilian helpers who, often far from home, dedicate themselves to peace, human rights, and the protection against terror and violence. We think of them and their families and friends. My thoughts are especially with the families of those who lost their lives in the line of duty, and with those who are suffering from serious injuries.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the Iraq war, the limitations of military force, and expresses gratitude toward personnel serving in international peace and security missions.
reason: The paragraph discusses the Iraq war, international military deployments, and global peace efforts.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on reflection and gratitude for past service rather than framing an active or urgent threat that requires immediate response.

paragraph_text: We can be grateful that we live in peace in Germany. In order that people elsewhere in the world may also live in peace, soldiers of the Bundeswehr and civilian aid workers are deployed in many countries. I would like to express my gratitude to them today, and to their families, my special greetings.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude to German soldiers and civilian aid workers deployed abroad for their contributions to international peace.
reason: It acknowledges the international deployment of German soldiers and aid workers.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude and recognition for service, which is explicitly listed as a non-crisis category.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, people around the world are also looking to us Germans for another reason. Together with our partners, we have recently succeeded in definitively overcoming the division of Europe. By welcoming ten new member states, including our neighbors Poland and the Czech Republic, into the European Union, we have finally put an end to the era of wars and conflicts on our continent. What our ancestors could only dream of – building a united Europe – has now become a tangible reality. This benefits us politically and creates new opportunities for our economy. We can all contribute to making Europe a continent of peace, freedom, and prosperity.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights the expansion of the European Union as a historic achievement that fosters peace, unity, and economic opportunity across the continent.
reason: The focus is on the expansion of the European Union and the geopolitical implications of European integration.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the expansion of the European Union as a positive achievement and a source of unity and opportunity, rather than as a threat or an urgent problem requiring defensive action.

paragraph_text: I also want to thank the many thousands of civilian helpers who are working in Afghanistan, in the former Yugoslavia, and in many other parts of the world. I think of 'Doctors Without Borders, ' the men and women of the Technical Relief Organization and the Red Cross, and all those who are building homes and wells, laying roads, and rebuilding schools. Without their work, there can be no real chance of peace. And we know that peace and security cannot exist without justice.
paragraph_summary: The speaker acknowledges the contributions of civilian aid workers in international conflict zones and emphasizes the link between humanitarian efforts, justice, and global peace.
reason: The focus is on international humanitarian aid and reconstruction efforts in foreign countries.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive contributions of aid workers and the abstract ideals of justice and peace rather than framing the conflict zones as an urgent, active threat requiring immediate defensive action or sacrifice.

paragraph_text: In the months ahead, Europe will be looking to us. Germany will assume the presidency of the European Union for six months, starting today. During those six months, we want to launch important reforms in finance, agriculture, but above all, in creating jobs. And we want to ensure that Germany receives fairer funding for Europe.
paragraph_summary: Germany plans to prioritize financial, agricultural, and employment reforms while seeking fairer funding arrangements during its upcoming six-month presidency of the European Union.
reason: Discusses Germany's upcoming presidency of the European Union and international policy goals.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes routine policy priorities and administrative goals for an upcoming presidency, lacking any language framing these issues as urgent threats or dangers.

paragraph_text: We Germans know very well what Europe has given us. We enjoy open borders, open markets, and a vibrant exchange with the cultures of our neighboring countries. Europe has given us the opportunity for unprecedented development in peace and prosperity, and it has made possible the unity of our country.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights the benefits of European integration, including open borders, economic prosperity, and the role of Europe in German reunification.
reason: Reflects on the benefits of European integration and open borders.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive achievements and benefits of European integration, such as prosperity and reunification, rather than framing any situation as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, the impressions I gained a few days ago in Sarajevo have reinforced my conviction: Nothing is more important than a united Europe, in which peace and freedom are guaranteed. Anyone who knows history understands that peace and freedom are not guaranteed. The best safeguard against war and irresponsible power struggles is European unity. That is why I am passionately committed to it. I urge you to complete the project of a united Europe. It must become a home for all the peoples of our continent – for all who wish to live in peace and freedom. At the European Council meeting in Luxembourg, we made significant progress and set the course for the eastward expansion of the European Union. At the end of a century marked by catastrophes and human suffering, Europe is coming together, overcoming all former divisions. A vision is becoming reality. A new Europe is emerging. This Europe will be a continent of peace, where future generations can live in freedom.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for European unity and eastward expansion of the European Union to ensure lasting peace and freedom across the continent.
reason: The focus is on European integration, the expansion of the EU, and international peace.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive goal of unity and expansion to ensure peace and freedom, rather than framing the current state as an urgent threat or an active danger.

paragraph_text: This is quite simply a government in a hurry. And there’s a reason for that: Britain is in a global race to succeed today. It’s a race with countries like China, India, and Indonesia—a race for the jobs and opportunities of the future.
paragraph_summary: The government is prioritizing rapid economic development to remain competitive in the global race for future jobs and opportunities against emerging economies.
reason: Discusses the UK's competitive position in the global economy against other nations.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames economic development as a routine policy goal and a competitive ambition rather than a response to an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: We have taken some very tough decisions in the past few years, especially in the last 12 months. They were necessary and I believe they are beginning to pay off. The decision to go to war in Iraq was the most difficult of all. Our superb service men and women have spent Christmas in Iraq, working day and night to help rebuild the country. As ever, we are hugely proud of them. The recent capture of Saddam Hussein was a vital milestone on the road to a stable Iraq. Meanwhile constant progress on essential services like electricity and water are sure signs that life in Iraq is slowly going in the right direction. In 2004 we must stick to the task. There will be no better signal for the Middle East or the world than a democratic, prosperous Iraq replacing a tyrannical, brutal dictatorship. Libya's courageous decision to dismantle its weapons of mass destruction will also make the world a safer place. And it shows that the problems of these weapons, can, with determination and good faith, be tackled through discussion and engagement.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses the progress of the Iraq war, the capture of Saddam Hussein, and Libya's decision to dismantle its weapons of mass destruction.
reason: The paragraph discusses foreign policy, the war in Iraq, and international relations with Libya.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the progress of a war and the neutralization of security threats, which are presented as achievements and successful outcomes rather than active, ongoing threats requiring urgent response.

paragraph_text: I cannot recall a time when Britain was confronted, simultaneously, by such a range of difficult and, in some cases, dangerous problems. Iraq, and the prospect of committing UK troops to action if Saddam Hussein continues to flout international law and fails properly to disarm; the mass of intelligence flowing across my desk that points to a continuing threat of attack by al-Qaida; the lack of progress on the Middle East peace process which has the potential to wreak havoc well beyond that region; and more recently the disturbing developments over North Korea's nuclear programme. In the era of globalisation the world is more interdependent than ever. Whilst that has brought many benefits, particularly economic and cultural, it has brought risks too. Alongside security concerns, there are economic concerns arising from lack of confidence in key parts of the world economy.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister outlines significant global security threats, including potential military action in Iraq, terrorism, and nuclear proliferation, alongside international economic instability.
reason: The paragraph focuses on international security threats, foreign conflicts, and global economic interdependence.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly identifies multiple severe threats to security and economic stability, framing them as urgent dangers that require attention and response.

paragraph_text: In the meantime, the government will continue to address the other international challenges, on the environment, on the poverty, war and famine that mark out Africa as a special problem for our world. We have to reach out to the Arab and Muslim world, and show that the hand of friendship is sincerely extended. We must understand too the anger they feel, that progress in the Middle East peace process has been so slow, so painful, so deadly. We must focus on moving the process forward: on security, on political reform, on the only viable solution the whole world now supports - an Israeli state, recognised by all, and a viable Palestinian state. And we have to do it quickly which is why the UK will host a conference on Palestinian reform early in the New Year. Until we give a sense of hope and progress this issue will continue to cast a dark shadow over our world.
model_reasons: environment: refers to general international challenges rather than the natural environment
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister outlines UK efforts to address global poverty, environmental challenges, and the Middle East peace process through diplomatic engagement and upcoming reform conferences.
reason: The text addresses international challenges including poverty, war, and the Middle East peace process.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes routine diplomatic engagement and policy initiatives rather than framing these issues as urgent, immediate threats requiring emergency response or sacrifice.

paragraph_text: Last year global economic growth slowed to its lowest level since the first oil price shock in the 1970s. G7 industrial production actually fell and world trade stagnated following years of steady growth. This followed the world's three largest economies, the US, Japan and Germany, all experiencing recession in 2001. Growth picked up during 2002, led by recovery in the US. But growth in the eurozone remains weak, particularly in Germany, and there is little sign of sustained recovery in Japan. Stock markets have fallen sharply during the course of the year, weakening hopes of a rapid investment-led recovery.
paragraph_summary: The speech outlines the global economic slowdown, recessionary trends in major economies, and the lack of a sustained recovery in international markets.
reason: The paragraph discusses global economic trends and the performance of major international economies.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the global economic slowdown and lack of recovery as a persistent, damaging threat to international markets, which constitutes a chronic crisis framing.

paragraph_text: We must confront the issue of Iraq and WMD (weapons of mass destruction) because to fail to do so would make the world a very dangerous place in the future, with countries free to flout the will of the United Nations. We must push on with the Middle East peace process, whatever the problems, because otherwise we are guilty of the very double standards we are accused of. We must continue to take a leading role in the fight against terror. Doing so doesn't make us a target. We are a target anyway, as is every country in the world in the eyes of today's breed of terrorist, and the only way to stop being a target is to stop the terrorists. We must continue to play our full part in Europe because though difficult, there is no future in Britain marginalising itself in Europe where almost 50% of our trade and many of our key alliances lie.

paragraph_text: On Iraq, the choice is Saddam's. No one wants a military confrontation with Iraq. But Iraq must be disarmed of WMD. By going down the UN route, the international community has given Saddam the chance for peaceful disarmament. If he does not seize it, he will have to be disarmed by force. Faced with his continual defiance of the UN, and knowing as we do the risk to the region and the world, we will be failing in our duty to make the world a safer place if we allow him to continue to develop WMD unhindered. Uniquely, he has used them before. He has to be stopped before he does so again. And to rogue states developing and trading in WMD, and terrorist groups, who would acquire and use them if they could, the message must go out; they can not and will not be allowed to.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister asserts that Iraq must disarm its weapons of mass destruction peacefully or face military intervention to ensure global security.
reason: The paragraph discusses international security policy regarding Iraq and weapons of mass destruction.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the possession of weapons of mass destruction as an urgent threat to global security that necessitates a response, including the potential for military intervention.

paragraph_text: As the international community looks back on one year, and forward to the next, it can be immensely proud of the way it responded to the attack upon humanity that took place on 11 September, and the UK can be proud of the role we are playing on all fronts, military, diplomatic, humanitarian. A huge amount has been achieved. The case against bin Laden and the al Qaida network was carefully and patiently put together. Their guilt established, an ultimatum was issued to the Taliban. Once it was ignored, the US-led coalition put together a targeted bombing campaign, first destroying the terrorist training camps, then the Taliban's military infrastructure, then hitting their front-lines before, more swiftly than we had dared imagine, the Taliban fell. The joy with which their fall was greeted nailed the lie that ours is a war against Islam. It showed, on the contrary, that the long-suffering people of Afghanistan also wanted rid of the Taliban and the terrorists they harboured for so long. The formation of, and the transition to, an interim government has been swift and extraordinarily smooth given the complex and volatile reality of Afghan politics. On the humanitarian front, despite the obstructions and the difficulties, aid agencies and governments have done a remarkable job in getting help to millions of people who need it. And around the world governments, including our own, have been strengthening their laws in relation to the operation and financing of terrorism and have sent the most powerful possible signal to terror groups everywhere: the world will not tolerate terrorism as a factor in international affairs.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister reviews the successful international military, humanitarian, and diplomatic response to the September 11 attacks and the subsequent fall of the Taliban.
reason: The paragraph discusses international military and humanitarian efforts in Afghanistan and global counter-terrorism.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the successful international response and the aftermath of the events, framing them as a past achievement rather than an active or ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: So there has been huge progress but the job is not yet done. I have always been clear there will be two phases to this campaign. First, dealing with al Qaida and bin Laden in Afghanistan, and then the wider war on terrorism. Many thousands of terrorists have been trained in the terror camps of Afghanistan and have long since left there. They continue to pose a risk, and the international community must continue to be vigilant and determined in rooting them out and shutting down their networks. We will be. Equally, today I re-state our commitment to help Afghanistan in the long term and not to make the mistakes of the past and walk away. September 11 was an act of evil. In its response, the international community has shown that by acting together with a sense of shared values and shared mission, it can be a force for good.
paragraph_summary: The speaker outlines the ongoing international campaign against terrorism and reaffirms a long-term commitment to supporting Afghanistan's stability.
reason: The text focuses on the international community's ongoing commitment to the war on terrorism and the future of Afghanistan.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames terrorism as an ongoing international threat requiring a sustained campaign and commitment, which constitutes a chronic threat framed as an urgent security concern.

paragraph_text: Now is the time for a concerted effort to bring peace to the Middle East. In playing the role we have, I believe Britain has shown that in pursuit of a modern foreign policy we do not need to choose between Europe and America. We are stronger in Europe because of our closeness to the US. We are stronger with the US because of our growing strength in Europe.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for Middle East peace efforts while emphasizing the strategic benefits of Britain's dual engagement with Europe and the United States.
reason: The paragraph addresses foreign policy, specifically the UK's relationship with the Middle East, Europe, and the US.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on diplomatic strategy and the pursuit of peace as a positive, routine foreign policy objective rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat or an active crisis.

paragraph_text: Sometimes, it can help to look beyond ourselves and draw inspiration from others. Consider the Ukrainian people, who have now been standing against the brutal aggression of Russia for ten months, with incredible courage and unity. The promise of Christmas – the hope for peace – lives in the hearts of all Ukrainians, and in ours as well.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights the courage and unity of the Ukrainian people in their ongoing resistance against Russian aggression.
reason: It discusses the international conflict in Ukraine and the Russian aggression.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the ongoing Russian aggression as a direct threat to the Ukrainian people, emphasizing the necessity of resistance and unity in the face of this danger.

paragraph_text: Christmas is the festival celebrating the anticipation of "peace on earth, goodwill to men. " Singing those words, as an expression of personal faith, can simultaneously evoke a sense of unease. The world is so vast. The problems are so complex. Interests are so opposed. And the suffering that people endure is often deeply distressing. Natural disasters tear communities apart. We see images of people in makeshift shelters, fleeing hunger and terror. We hear words of hatred passed down through generations, making it difficult for voices of goodwill to be heard. The larger picture seems beyond our influence. That can create a feeling of powerlessness.
model_actions: Dropped count of 'natural disasters' | Dropped count of 'disasters'
model_reasons: natural disasters: refers to general humanitarian suffering, not explicitly linked to climate change | disasters: refers to general humanitarian crises, not environmental or climate events

paragraph_text: The European community has brought prosperity. But there is more than just money; above all, Europe offers us peace. Yet, it seems as if discontent overshadows everything. In the crisis that currently affects us, a climate has emerged in which distrust prevails. Breaking free from that negative mindset begins with the realization that Europe is not a foreign power, but our own community in this part of the world. Europe is us. With confidence, we can continue to build on European cooperation.

crisis_reason: The paragraph frames societal distrust as a problem to be overcome through renewed confidence and shared identity, treating the lack of cohesion as a threat to the European project.

paragraph_text: Many people live between sorrow and hope in this world. Violence is a daily reality for countless individuals, while peace remains a distant aspiration. A constant undercurrent of tension burdens existence. Fear can so easily escalate into aggression.
paragraph_summary: The speech addresses the global prevalence of violence, fear, and the persistent struggle to maintain hope amidst ongoing international tension.
reason: The paragraph discusses the general state of violence and fear in the world.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the global situation as a persistent struggle against violence and fear, characterizing these international tensions as an ongoing threat to hope and stability.

paragraph_text: For those living in poverty, freedom from want and poverty is paramount. We cannot disclaim our shared responsibility for their daily lives. Destitute conditions in vast parts of the world should never be considered "normal." Everyone is entitled to a dignified life, rising above mere survival. Inequality and injustice remain unacceptable. More than half a century after the Four Freedoms were proclaimed, the first challenge for the international community remains to champion social justice.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the international community's shared responsibility to combat global poverty and inequality to ensure social justice and a dignified life for all.
reason: The focus is on the international challenge of poverty and social justice.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames poverty and inequality as a moral imperative and a goal for social justice rather than an urgent, existential threat requiring immediate crisis response.

paragraph_text: After two world wars, a true victory could only be declared when the chaos gave rise to an international community dedicated to peace and justice. The organization that embodied these ideals found a home in America. In the midst of all the tensions in international relations today, the history of the United Nations can provide a guiding path. From the foundation of the Four Freedoms, we can still strive for peace and justice today. Together, they give shape to human dignity. God affirmed that dignity in each and every one of us through the birth of Jesus. That is what we celebrate at Christmas. I dedicate this speech to my mother, who has always lived according to this conviction. In everyone's life, meaning and purpose can be found in compassion for others. That is the encouragement and the mission of Christmas. As a poet once said, "Continue to dream those great words, never let go of hope." I wish you all a blessed Christmas.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the United Nations' ideals of peace and justice, connecting them to the Christmas message of compassion and human dignity.
reason: The paragraph discusses international relations, the United Nations, and global peace efforts.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on universal ideals and the spirit of a holiday, lacking any framing of a threat, danger, or urgent problem requiring action.

paragraph_text: As this century draws to a close, we are burdened by the awareness of humanity's 'dark side. ' Violence seems relentless, and intervention in conflicts is extremely difficult. However, it is now recognized that we cannot stand idly by in the face of cruelty and atrocities. This century, marked by wars, has also seen a growing awareness that the exercise of power – both between nations and within countries – must be measured against the framework of international law. While national sovereignty was initially considered an inviolable concept, internationally accepted norms are now gaining increasing weight. Those who commit crimes against humanity know that the international community wants to see such actions punished. This is one of the most hopeful developments of our time.
paragraph_summary: The speech discusses the shift toward prioritizing international law and accountability for crimes against humanity over the traditional concept of absolute national sovereignty.
reason: The text discusses international law, human rights, and the global community's response to atrocities.
crisis_reason: The paragraph discusses a shift in international legal norms and principles of sovereignty as a conceptual evolution rather than framing these issues as an urgent, active threat to the state or global order.

paragraph_text: I would like to send a special greeting tonight to all Norwegians who work abroad, both on land and at sea. Those of you who are constantly reminded that we are all interconnected across nationalities. And who daily experience the value of international cooperation.
paragraph_summary: The speaker extends a special greeting to Norwegians working abroad who contribute to and experience the value of international cooperation.
reason: It focuses on the experiences of citizens working abroad and the value of international cooperation.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is a ceremonial greeting and an expression of appreciation for international cooperation, which does not invoke any threat or urgent danger.

paragraph_text: “Solidarity and community have lost ground, ” says the poet Kjartan Fløgstad. These values have been fundamental in building Norway. But even on the international stage, we see how important it is to stand together. This autumn, we celebrated the 20th anniversary of the fall of the Berlin Wall. Many of us still remember the magical moment when the first pieces were chipped away, and neighbors, families, and an entire continent could reunite. One of the pictures from 1989 shows the world-renowned cellist Rostropovitch standing in front of the opening in the wall. "I played out of sheer joy. It was the wall in my own life that was being torn down, " he said. But "Berlin Walls" can be found everywhere. Walls of irreconcilable hatred and distrust contribute to war and conflict in many parts of the world. Inequalities and injustices also help build walls and create distance. Nevertheless, it offers a glimmer of hope that the remnants of the Berlin Wall are now a tourist attraction and a symbolic meeting point, both for a reunited Germany and a reunited Europe.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the fall of the Berlin Wall to discuss the importance of overcoming global divisions, hatred, and inequality through solidarity.
reason: The text discusses international historical events like the fall of the Berlin Wall and global geopolitical conflicts.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on historical reflection and the promotion of positive values like solidarity and unity rather than framing current conditions as an urgent, active threat.

paragraph_text: This Christmas, we have once again witnessed how quickly and dramatically life can change. The worst natural disaster of our time has struck many countries, particularly in Southeast Asia. Without warning, hundreds of thousands of people have been lost or severely injured, and millions have lost their homes, their livelihoods, and everything they owned. We have watched with horror at the recent, devastating news reports from the disaster that has impacted so many. Many Norwegians who were looking forward to their holidays and a time of joy have also been taken by the tsunami. Many have suffered great pain and grief, and I fear the number of Norwegians who have died or been injured may continue to rise. What has happened is incomprehensible, and it highlights how close we can be to the edge between joy and despair, between life and death, and between a normal everyday life and catastrophe. We, the Royal Family, share your pain and grief, and our thoughts are with those who have lost loved ones and with those who have been injured. Living in uncertainty when a loved one is missing is an incredibly difficult burden. It is our hope that this uncertainty can be replaced with reunions for as many people as possible.

paragraph_text: I am pleased by the great commitment shown by the Norwegian people in helping those affected by the floods. It is inspiring to see so many people willing to contribute. For many of us, this may involve donating money to rescue and reconstruction efforts. It is reassuring to know that both the international community, the United Nations, and our major aid organizations have professional teams that can be quickly deployed. The extensive damage will require sustained effort over a long period.

reason: The paragraph focuses on international aid efforts and the role of global organizations like the UN.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on relief and reconstruction efforts following a past event, framing the situation as an achievement of public commitment rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: We live in a world that is becoming increasingly interconnected, and we are becoming ever more dependent on one another. It is no longer enough to simply look out for ourselves. Through extensive international cooperation, we must develop forward-looking agreements that ensure the lives and well-being of future generations. In contrast to the Christmas tsunami, there are environmental problems that are caused by human actions. More than ever in history, we face a shared destiny. Our hope is that together, we can meet the challenges this presents. This year’s recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize, Wangari Maathai, expresses this beautifully: "Today, we face a challenge that requires us to change our thinking so that humanity stops threatening the system on which we all depend. It is our duty to help heal the Earth, and through this process, we will also heal ourselves – yes, embrace the entire creation with its diversity, its beauty, and its wonder. ”
model_reasons: tsunami: refers to a specific historical natural disaster event, not climate change
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the necessity of international cooperation and collective action to address human-caused environmental challenges and ensure a sustainable future.
reason: The paragraph discusses global interconnectedness, international cooperation, and worldwide environmental challenges.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames environmental challenges as a serious threat requiring urgent collective action and international cooperation to ensure a sustainable future.

paragraph_text: The world is different now than it was in 1972, when a young heir, Margrethe, was proclaimed Queen of Denmark. But some crises have, unfortunately, remained constant. I am delivering my New Year’s address tonight from Marienborg. It is here that alternating prime ministers throughout history have received foreign guests. The first to stay here was Israel’s first prime minister, Ben-Gurion. That was in 1962. Much has changed since then. But some people’s hatred of Israel, and the Palestinians’ difficult living conditions, remain unchanged. In recent months, horrific images have been burned into our collective memory: Young Israelis, one moment celebrating carefree in the desert, and the next fleeing for their lives. Entire families were found dead in the bedroom, in one of the most brutal terrorist attacks in recent times. The war in Gaza. Childhoods ending, almost before they begin. A humanitarian catastrophe. A lack of food, medicine, and water. The war in the Middle East casts long shadows, most recently with attacks on ships in the Red Sea. And the war is creating divisions, even here in Denmark. The entire world depends on peace in the Middle East. It has never been achieved. It must be now. Can we share this vision? That Jews have the right to live in peace, security, and freedom, in Israel, wherever they may live and reside, including here in Denmark. And that Palestinians have the same right, that they too have a claim to their own land. Two states, existing side by side. It may seem impossible right now, but it is the only way forward. It requires Palestinians to distance themselves from Hamas, from antisemitism, and to recognize Israel, to seek peace. It requires Israelis to halt the illegal settlements, to ensure that the most extreme voices do not define what Israel is. Israel has the right to defend itself, but must also seek peace. The Allies ended the Second World War. NATO stopped the war in the Balkans. I believe that it is the international community that must pave the way for peace in the Middle East, from Jerusalem to Ramallah.
paragraph_summary: The speech addresses the ongoing Israel-Palestine conflict, the humanitarian crisis in Gaza, and the necessity of a two-state solution for lasting peace.
reason: The paragraph focuses on the Middle East conflict, international relations, and the role of the international community.
crisis_reason: The paragraph explicitly identifies the situation as a 'humanitarian crisis' and frames the ongoing conflict as a threat requiring a specific political resolution for peace, indicating an active, urgent threat.

paragraph_text: It is a sobering thought that more than one billion people – a quarter of the world’s population – live on less than ten kroner a day. These are numbers that are difficult to grasp. It is important to remember that giving hope to even one person has value in itself. It is only when we encounter individuals that these statistics truly come to life. Personally, I have experienced powerful encounters with many people who live under difficult circumstances, but who still refuse to give up. In recent days, our thoughts have been particularly with the people of Iran, who have experienced the devastation of an earthquake that has claimed tens of thousands of lives and left even more homeless.
