paragraph_text: We need the right balance for our prosperity and our sense of community. We need a willingness to perform and social security for all. The importance of this balance is also demonstrated by the European sovereign debt crisis. The reforms we have implemented are beginning to take effect. Nevertheless, we still need a great deal of patience. The crisis is far from over. Furthermore, even internationally, more needs to be done to better monitor financial markets. The world has not yet fully learned the lessons of the devastating financial crisis of 2008. But we must never again allow the kind of irresponsibility that we saw back then to prevail. In a social market economy, the state must be the guarantor of order, and people must be able to trust that.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses balancing economic performance with social security while addressing the ongoing European debt crisis and the need for stricter financial market regulation.
reason: It links domestic economic stability to the European sovereign debt crisis and international financial regulation.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly frames the European debt crisis as an ongoing threat and identifies the need for stricter financial regulation as a necessary response to this danger.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, let us also remember, especially at this time, those who ensure our safety, both here at home and far from our country. Our soldiers, police officers, and civilian helpers are providing their service to us with great personal sacrifices. From my conversations with them, I know how much it means to them when we think of them at home. I would like to express my sincere thanks to them, especially this evening.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude and support for soldiers, police officers, and civilian helpers serving both domestically and abroad.
reason: It acknowledges the service of German personnel both at home and abroad.
crisis_reason: the paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude and support for service members, which is explicitly listed as a non-crisis category.

paragraph_text: Many people are concerned, not least about the future of their children: Can we overcome the European debt crisis? What will become of Europe, which for decades we have experienced as a guarantor of peace and prosperity? I am confident: In recent months, the government and opposition have, under immense pressure, made far-reaching decisions together. In this spirit of unity, we will, together with our friends in Europe and around the world, find a way out of the crisis. It is important to remember: Europe is our shared home and our precious heritage. It stands for the great values of freedom, human rights, and social security. All of this can only be preserved together. Those who say otherwise may find short-term applause, but they are mistaken. We Germans have always experienced European solidarity, and we will continue to stand in solidarity with Europe.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses confidence in overcoming the European debt crisis through national unity and continued solidarity with European partners.
reason: It addresses the European debt crisis and its impact on German prosperity and identity.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the recovery and the speaker's confidence in overcoming the situation through unity, rather than framing the debt crisis as an active, ongoing threat requiring urgent intervention.

paragraph_text: This begins in our everyday lives: It also depends on each of us what kind of intellectual climate prevails in our own families, in our religious communities, in our neighborhoods, or in our clubs. Openness to others and to those who are different begins with small steps – and especially with the younger generation. My three-and-a-half-year-old son is delighted when I read him the book "Somehow Different" at bedtime. He falls asleep peacefully, knowing that it is good that we are all different. We must grasp, as soon as possible, how foolish and harmful exclusion or thoughtless prejudices are. Let us work together to build an open society – a society that also takes responsibility for the well-being of people in other parts of the world.
model_reasons: climate: intellectual climate — refers to social atmosphere, not Earth's climate system
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for fostering an open, inclusive society by overcoming prejudice and embracing diversity in everyday life and global responsibility.
reason: It discusses building an open society at home while taking responsibility for the global community.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive societal values and aspirational goals rather than framing prejudice or social issues as an urgent, active threat requiring emergency response.

paragraph_text: Tonight, we also think of those who, far from home, are working for peace, security, and decent living conditions – especially our soldiers. They are making a contribution to making our world a better place. Germany enjoys a good reputation in the world – and that is partly because, more than almost anywhere else, there is such a willingness to help others – in times of disaster, in the face of misfortune, and in response to the regular appeals from major aid organizations. Many people abroad have expressed their gratitude to us, and I would like to pass that gratitude on to you all today. Because we can be proud of our country.
paragraph_summary: The speaker acknowledges the contributions of German soldiers serving abroad and expresses gratitude for the nation's widespread willingness to provide humanitarian aid.
reason: It highlights German international aid efforts and the role of soldiers abroad.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on gratitude and the acknowledgment of contributions, which are ceremonial and affirmative in nature rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: This also applies to us in Europe. Here, the ongoing debt crisis continues to weigh heavily on our minds. Despite all our efforts, we must never forget that the peaceful unification of our continent is a historical gift to us. It has brought us over half a century of peace, freedom, justice, human rights, and democracy. These values are ones that we must continue to cherish, especially now, as Europe faces its most difficult challenge in decades, and as I know, many of you are concerned about the stability of our currency. Just a few hours from now, it will be exactly ten years since many of us stood in line at ATMs in the middle of the night to withdraw the first euro banknotes. Since then, the euro has made our daily lives easier and strengthened our economy. It protected us from even greater difficulties during the financial and economic crisis of 2008. Today, I want you to know that I am committed to strengthening the euro. However, that will only be possible if Europe learns from past mistakes. One of those lessons is that a common currency can only truly succeed if we work together more closely within Europe. Europe is growing stronger through this crisis. The path to overcoming it will be long and will not be without setbacks, but at the end of that path, Europe will emerge from this crisis stronger than it entered it.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the European debt crisis, emphasizing the importance of the euro and the necessity of closer European cooperation to ensure future stability.
reason: The text links the European debt crisis and the euro to German domestic economic stability.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly identifies the situation as a 'debt crisis' and frames the need for closer cooperation as a necessary response to ensure future stability, indicating an urgent threat to the economic order.

paragraph_text: This is only possible if we can live in freedom and security. Our police officers and soldiers provide a vital service, often risking their lives, both at home and in many regions around the world. I thank them, as well as the many civilian and volunteer helpers in our country. They stand up for the values of our nation, values that are repeatedly challenged, and sometimes even attacked. We were once again horrified when, in the fall, a right-wing extremist terrorist and murder group was uncovered. Their actions, which they carried out unchecked for over a decade, revealed an appalling level of hatred and xenophobia. We know that we cannot fully compensate for the suffering of the victims’ families. But we owe it to them, and to all of us, to thoroughly investigate these crimes and to hold all those involved, including those who assisted them, accountable. It is our duty to resolutely defend the values of our open and liberal society – at all times and against any form of violence. This is an ongoing task, for politics and for all of us.
paragraph_summary: The speaker honors security forces and volunteers while calling for the defense of democratic values following the discovery of a right-wing extremist terrorist group.
reason: It addresses domestic security and terrorism while acknowledging the role of soldiers serving abroad.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the discovery of a right-wing extremist terrorist group as an active threat to democratic values, necessitating a defensive response and vigilance.

paragraph_text: Solidarity, understanding, and getting along: this also applies to our relationships with all our partners in the world. Our country is highly regarded. Our liberal and tolerant society, our reliability towards large and small countries, is valued. It is a truly rewarding experience to witness this time and again, in my meetings with guests here and on our trips abroad. We demonstrate solidarity and are ready to continue taking responsibility – also in Europe. We expect the same from our partners. Everyone must do their homework. We have confidence in European unity and in the strength of Europe.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes Germany's commitment to international solidarity, reliability, and the importance of collective responsibility and unity within the European Union.
reason: It connects domestic social values to Germany's international reputation and European responsibilities.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive values like solidarity, reliability, and unity, which are presented as foundational principles rather than responses to an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: But despite all our optimism, we must continue to prove our strengths in the future – and not just in football. Europe is currently facing a major test. We must strengthen the euro. This is not just about our money. The euro is far more than just a currency. We Europeans are united by our shared desire for prosperity. A united Europe is the guarantor of our peace and freedom. The euro is the foundation of our prosperity. Germany needs Europe and our common currency. For our own well-being and to address major challenges around the world.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of strengthening the euro and maintaining European unity to ensure long-term prosperity, peace, and freedom for Germany.
reason: It frames the European currency and unity as essential for German prosperity.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on long-term prosperity and the maintenance of peace and freedom as positive, aspirational goals rather than framing the current state as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: We Germans take responsibility – even when it is very difficult. Our soldiers in Afghanistan had to cope with the loss of nine comrades this year. Although no words of mine can truly alleviate the pain of the families and friends of those who have fallen, I want to say from the bottom of my heart: I will not forget them. I will also not forget those who have been physically and emotionally wounded. I sincerely hope that they can recover quickly. The soldiers in Afghanistan told me that many people, even complete strangers, sent them letters and packages at Christmas. They specifically asked me to thank everyone on their behalf, and I am very happy to do so.
paragraph_summary: The speaker honors German soldiers killed or wounded in Afghanistan and conveys their gratitude for public support received during the Christmas season.
reason: It addresses the domestic impact of German military involvement in Afghanistan.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on honoring past sacrifices and expressing gratitude for public support, which is a ceremonial and reflective act rather than a framing of an active or urgent threat.

paragraph_text: I also think of our soldiers serving in Afghanistan. Do we truly understand the significance of their service? My best wishes go out to them, and to all our fellow citizens who, far from home, are working for safety and peaceful development in the police force or in aid organizations. To you all, I send my warmest Christmas greetings.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude and Christmas greetings to German soldiers, police, and aid workers serving abroad in Afghanistan and other regions.
reason: It connects the service of German personnel abroad to domestic appreciation and holiday greetings.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as a ceremonial expression of gratitude and holiday greetings, which is explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: Living attentively also means working towards a just order, both here and around the world. There is still so much to do. We have just experienced how recklessness among financial actors and shortcomings in government oversight plunged the world into a deep crisis. We need greater integrity and better regulations in the financial sector. We need to understand that money should serve people, and not the other way around. I call for introspection from those in positions of responsibility, just as I demand it from myself and from all of us.
paragraph_summary: The speaker calls for greater integrity and stricter regulation in the financial sector to ensure money serves the public interest following a global crisis.
reason: The paragraph links domestic financial regulation to a global crisis and international order.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the aftermath and recovery from a past crisis rather than an active, ongoing threat, framing the call for regulation as a policy response to ensure future stability.

paragraph_text: It will particularly support us in the tasks that lie ahead in the new year. I would like to express my sincere gratitude, first and foremost, to the many civilian helpers, the police officers, and our soldiers, who are away from their loved ones, performing their duty. They are serving in many places around the world, often risking their lives, particularly in Afghanistan. The German government is aware of the hardship and danger involved in their mission. However, the task of our soldiers, police officers, and civilian aid workers in Afghanistan – to create security and stability in Afghanistan, so that it never again poses a threat to our security and well-being – remains a vital and important one for us all. That is the mission. Politically, we must and will create the conditions to ensure that responsibility can be gradually transferred to the Afghans in the coming years. The Afghanistan conference at the end of January in London serves precisely that purpose.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude to German personnel serving in Afghanistan and outlines the goal of transitioning security responsibilities to Afghan authorities.
reason: It discusses German military deployment in Afghanistan as a matter of national security.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on gratitude for service and the routine transition of security responsibilities, which is framed as a policy goal rather than an active, urgent threat.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, yes, this is not just any New Year's Eve. A new decade is beginning, a decade in which many important decisions will be made for our country. Decisions will be made regarding: * How we can protect justice and humanity in a world that cannot completely eradicate injustice, violence, and war; * How we can overcome the most severe economic crisis in the history of the Federal Republic of Germany and, with a sense of responsibility for future generations, stabilize our public finances; * How we, as a society, can remain open in spirit, even as we age and our population grows smaller; * How we can maintain our prosperity by changing the way we live and conduct our economy.
paragraph_summary: The Chancellor outlines key challenges for the coming decade, including global justice, economic recovery, demographic shifts, and sustainable prosperity.
reason: The paragraph outlines national policy goals in the context of global challenges like war and economic crises.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the future as a set of challenges to be managed rather than an urgent, existential threat requiring immediate defensive action or sacrifice. It focuses on long-term policy goals and prosperity rather than an acute or chronic danger.

paragraph_text: Tonight, I also think of our soldiers, serving far from home, working to ensure security and reconstruction. They serve peace, risking their lives and well-being. We want to express our gratitude to them.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude to German soldiers serving abroad for their contributions to international security, reconstruction, and peace.
reason: It addresses the deployment of national soldiers in international missions.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude for service and contributions to peace, rather than focusing on an active threat or an urgent danger requiring immediate response.

paragraph_text: We are all concerned by the global financial crisis and its consequences. An unimaginable amount of money has been lost. Many are worried about their savings. And many fear for their jobs.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses public anxiety regarding the global financial crisis, specifically concerning the loss of savings and the threat to job security.
reason: It links the global financial crisis to domestic concerns about jobs and savings.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly frames the financial situation as a source of public anxiety and a direct threat to personal savings and job security, which constitutes a crisis framing.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, The end of the year is a time to distinguish between what is truly important and what is not. What was important to you this year? A long-held wish? Concern for a loved one? The birth of your own child? Your professional future? Time with family and friends? Or simply a moment to appreciate the immense good fortune we have in living in peace and security – a privilege many others, such as those in the Middle East, would give anything to experience. We are seeing this with renewed urgency in these days. We must not forget the causes and consequences of the current conflicts. The terror perpetrated by Hamas cannot be accepted. However, we must also not forget that there is no reasonable alternative to peaceful coexistence between Israelis and Palestinians in two states, an outcome that serves the best interests of the people on both sides. The German government will provide whatever support it can to achieve this.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on personal values and the necessity of a two-state solution to resolve the conflict between Israel and Hamas.
reason: It contrasts domestic peace with international conflicts like the Middle East crisis.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the conflict as a situation requiring a specific political resolution, but it focuses on the necessity of a solution rather than presenting the conflict as an urgent, active threat to the speaker's state or society.

paragraph_text: Because the global crisis also affects Germany. The reckless financial behavior and lack of social responsibility displayed by some bankers and managers – though certainly not all – have led the world into this crisis. The world has been living beyond its means. Only by identifying these causes can we lead the world out of this crisis. To do so, we need clear principles: The state must be the guardian of economic and social order. Competition requires both prudence and social responsibility. These are the principles of the social market economy. They apply here, but that is not enough. These principles must be observed worldwide. Only then can we lead the world out of this crisis. The world is beginning to learn this lesson, and that is the opportunity that this crisis presents – the opportunity for international rules based on the principles of the social market economy. I will not rest until we achieve such rules.
paragraph_summary: The speaker attributes the global financial crisis to irresponsible economic behavior and advocates for establishing international rules based on social market economy principles.
reason: It argues for applying national economic principles to global financial regulation.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly identifies the global financial crisis as a serious threat caused by irresponsible behavior, framing the need for new international rules as an urgent response to this danger.

paragraph_text: My fellow citizens, I would like to thank everyone who is working during the holiday season. And I send a grateful greeting from our homeland to our soldiers, our police officers, and our development aid workers, who are working abroad to promote peace in the world. My thoughts are also with the families of those who have lost their lives on overseas missions.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude to domestic workers and personnel serving abroad in military, police, and development roles while honoring those who died on missions.
reason: It acknowledges domestic service members and aid workers operating in international contexts.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude and honor for service members and personnel, which is a ceremonial and reflective act rather than a framing of an active or urgent threat.

paragraph_text: We must consolidate these gains. Despite all our successes, we must not become complacent. The risks to our economy and economic growth, particularly due to global influences, are too great. It is also understandable that many of you are concerned, for example, about the high prices for energy and food. And it is a sobering thought that we still have 3. 5 million unemployed people in our country. Therefore, my goal remains unchanged: to continue fighting unemployment. This means preserving existing jobs and creating the conditions for new ones to be created. That, and only that, will be the standard for our actions in the future. All the political measures we take must serve this purpose – whether it is the reform of inheritance tax, the introduction of minimum wage levels, the impact of additional costs for employers, or a robust debt brake for the years to come. I thank everyone in our country who shares this goal, our businesses – both large and small – as well as our workers.
paragraph_summary: The speaker outlines a commitment to economic stability and growth by prioritizing the reduction of unemployment through targeted policy measures and job creation.
reason: It discusses domestic economic policy while acknowledging the impact of global market influences.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on routine economic governance and positive policy goals like growth and job creation rather than framing unemployment as an urgent, existential threat or a crisis.

paragraph_text: A culture of attentiveness – we could also adopt this as a motto for the coming year. For example, let us look at the more than 23 million people in Germany who are actively involved in voluntary work: in soup kitchens, at the volunteer fire department, in sports clubs, or in reading circles. Or let us look at the more than half a million carol singers who, in the coming days, will collect donations for children's aid projects around the world. Other countries are closely watching what is happening in Germany. They see our potential and our increased economic strength. And they are carefully observing how we consistently and responsibly pursue our economic interests and political goals on a global scale. Both of these factors have significantly enhanced Germany’s reputation in the world over the past two years. Both of these factors also bring greater responsibility for Germany.

paragraph_text: Germany is not an island. We are participating in an international competition of quality that affects all aspects of our lives: Which nation is best at unlocking the creative potential of its people? How open is a society to new ideas? What does it offer young families? Which country has the best schools and universities? How well do native-born citizens and immigrants coexist? All of this also depends on political decisions. Therefore, this competition is also about the quality of politics. Good politics means, first and foremost, honesty in assessing our strengths and weaknesses. It means having the courage to set goals and being held accountable for achieving them. And it means consistency and coherence in our actions. That is what we should expect.

paragraph_text: Today, I also think of the men and women who cannot celebrate Christmas with their loved ones because they are working tirelessly for others, even on this day. My heartfelt Christmas greetings go especially to the soldiers of the Bundeswehr, who are working far from home to ensure our security and freedom. I thank you all sincerely!

paragraph_text: II. Germany has also gained prestige in foreign policy. We are a respected and reliable partner in Europe and the world. "Europe succeeds together" – that is the motto of our European Council presidency, which begins tomorrow. And I would add: Europe only succeeds together. Only a united Europe can meet the challenges of globalization, of global trade, but also of violence, terror, and war. A divided Europe is doomed to fail. Taking responsibility – at home, in Europe, and in the world – ultimately always comes down to very concrete actions. I would therefore like to thank all those who help, I thank the police officers, the medical personnel, and I thank our soldiers, who are currently helping to secure peace and stability far from home, even at the risk of their own lives.

paragraph_text: Much has moved the world this year: we said goodbye to Pope John Paul II, and we rejoiced with people around the world at the election of his successor, our fellow countryman, Pope Benedict XVI. We have the Frauenkirche in Dresden again, thanks to citizens from all over the world who set their minds to it. And nature has taught us humility, in the southern United States, in Central America, in Pakistan, and especially in the Indian Ocean. The tsunami brought before our eyes how fragile the world is and how vulnerable we humans are. The survivors and those left behind are grateful for the help they received. And we should be grateful to those who are not celebrating with their loved ones tonight, because they are there for others – in hospitals, in the police force, as bus and train drivers, or as soldiers, ensuring security and freedom far from home. Many of our fellow citizens are providing assistance abroad, in the name of Germany, to those who are less fortunate than us. That is also good for Germany, because we are all dependent on one another, whether we are Europeans or Americans, Asians or Africans. Jens Weißflog, the ski jumper, once said, "You can only jump as far as you think in your head. " A thought that inspires us in our efforts for those who live in poverty and hardship, here and everywhere in the world. We want to be a force for good.

paragraph_text: Next year, we, as a nation, have a great opportunity! The world will be watching Germany, as it did 16 years ago with the fall of the Berlin Wall. Of course, the situations are not comparable in terms of their significance, but nonetheless: billions of people around the world will be watching the FIFA World Cup on television, and millions of people will be coming to visit us. Naturally, we are cheering on our national team, and I believe we have a good chance. After all, our women's national team is already a World Cup champion, and I see no reason why the men cannot achieve the same. For me, the winner of the World Cup is already clear: it is us, the people of this country, because we can celebrate a festival with the whole world. The World Cup has a wonderful motto: "The world is coming to celebrate with friends. " Become a friend! Let us all become friends with our guests.

paragraph_text: There are also great expectations of our country outside of Europe – in negotiations on free world trade, in the deployment of German soldiers and police abroad, and in the reform of the United Nations. Germans have a habit of demonstrating what they are capable of. We show that we stand together, that we do not rest until those in need are safe again – as was the case with the kidnapping of Jürgen Chrobog, his wife, and their three sons in Yemen. We also think of others during major natural disasters. I thank you very much for your extraordinary generosity in donating after the tsunami. At the same time, I would like to ask you to also remember those who suffer in silence – for example, those affected by famine in Africa, the victims of the devastating earthquake in Pakistan, and those affected by tragedies in various regions of our world, caused by wars, civil wars, migration flows, and diseases. Let us not forget them, let us open our hearts to them – just as we do for those in need here at home.

paragraph_text: A few days ago, I returned from Africa. For most people there, life is truly difficult. Many are hungry day after day. The plight of children is particularly heartbreaking. But you know what? Even amidst that hardship, I also sensed a great deal of strength, courage, and joy of life. I was asked to send greetings from Fatuma, a woman from the highlands of Ethiopia. She asked me to tell the Germans how grateful we are! Thanks to donations from Germany, it was possible to cure Fatuma of leprosy. Now, with continued support from Germany, she is working with other women to build a small farm. Fatuma showed me how wisely she manages the donations from Germany. It was truly impressive.

reason: It discusses international aid and development in Africa through the lens of German donations and personal connection.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive impact and achievements of humanitarian aid rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: We are also indebted to those who are working here at home during the holidays. Some are caring for the sick or rescuing accident victims. Others are making sure that everyday life continues smoothly, even over Christmas. For us, it’s often taken for granted. The police officer, the nurse, the bus driver – today, in particular, we should remember those who are working for us. Gratitude is also due to those who volunteer and dedicate themselves to helping others, both at home and around the world.

reason: It acknowledges domestic workers and volunteers who contribute to both local and international causes.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude and recognition for ongoing service rather than an urgent warning about a threat or a call to action against a danger.

paragraph_text: My fellow citizens, what can we do now, and what must we do in the future? Of course, you and I are particularly concerned about how quickly we can determine the fate of German tourists and bring survivors home. German Air Force aircraft – so-called "flying hospitals" – are being deployed to transport the injured. We are not making any distinctions based on nationality – only the severity of the injuries matters. Additional German Air Force aircraft and charter flights are also available to transport German tourists back home. German travel agencies are demonstrating exemplary responsibility towards their customers.

reason: It addresses the domestic concern of rescuing German citizens abroad using national military assets.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the government's response and recovery efforts, specifically repatriation and medical assistance, rather than the threat itself.

paragraph_text: All of this is certainly necessary, and often difficult to implement. But can we truly do all of this? Are we delegating responsibility to an international level, thereby distancing ourselves from it? I have spoken about the scale of suffering that we must address, and to which we must respond, each of us in our own way: the governments, the regions, the economy, and the entire global community. I want to provide sustainable assistance to the region. I want us to feel a long-term sense of responsibility. All wealthy nations should form partnerships for the reconstruction of specific regions. I envision the major industrial nations taking responsibility for particular countries – and Germany, our federal states for corresponding districts, our cities for cities, and our villages for villages. Our economy can help. Assistance would become visible and concrete in this way. German schools and their students could form partnerships with schools there – supported by their parents. This would demonstrate that we want to go far beyond simply donating money – which is certainly important – and that we understand responsibility as something lasting. The German government will propose such a strategy of sustainable partnership to our friends in the European Union. Every country on our prosperous continent can demonstrate responsibility and concretely prove their humanity.

paragraph_text: This disaster has shaken us all. But amidst all the suffering, there are also moments of profound human kindness. People, even those who are poor, come together in times of need, helping each other, standing up for others, and caring for their neighbors despite their own immense suffering. We have all seen the gestures of kindness in the countries where our tourists have been visiting, and we must not fall short. You, my fellow citizens, have generously and compassionately donated to help those in greatest need. You have understood what is at stake. We Germans know what solidarity can achieve. We have learned to stand together in difficult times, and we stand by others when they need our support. We do not close our eyes – or our hearts – to the suffering of others. For that, I want to thank you all from the heart.

crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the public's positive response and solidarity following a disaster, which frames the situation as a recovery or an act of gratitude rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: It is true: tonight, on New Year's Eve, it is not appropriate to discuss the details of German domestic politics. However, I will say that last year, we implemented important reforms for the future of our country. These reforms will ensure that Germany remains on a path to success. We will continue our reform agenda with determination in the new year. Because it strengthens us to face the challenges within, and because it strengthens us to meet the greater responsibility we have in the world. Especially at this time of year, it is once again clear that we Germans possess the strength for solidarity, both within our own community and beyond. We can face the new year with confidence and hope – and be grateful that we live in one of the most peaceful and stable regions of the world. I wish you and your families happiness, success, and above all, good health in 2005.

crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on national reforms, solidarity, and confidence in future stability, which are framed as positive developments and routine governance rather than urgent threats.

paragraph_text: Decisively modernizing Germany We have ambitious plans for the New Year. Some of these will only be achievable with the support of other political parties. And I hope that these plans will be realized, despite the upcoming election campaigns. If Germany modernizes decisively and unitedly, it will benefit not only our country. The entire continent of Europe is watching and hoping for our efforts. And we, in turn, are looking to Europe, because together we are committed to balancing interests, fostering strong international partnerships, and supporting the policies of the United Nations. I believe that the events of the past few weeks also provide international grounds for optimism as we look ahead to the New Year: Iran and Libya have agreed to allow international weapons controls. Free elections are imminent in Afghanistan. German soldiers and civilians are working to make that possible. Because we in Germany have learned: we are part of a single world. Let us face the New Year with confidence and optimism. I wish you and your families a happy and healthy, a peaceful and successful New Year.

reason: It links domestic modernization to international expectations and global diplomatic commitments.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on modernization, cooperation, and optimism, which are framed as positive developments and routine governance rather than urgent threats or dangers.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, today, on New Year’s Eve, we inaugurated the "Transrapid" in Shanghai – a future technology developed here in Germany that offers an excellent solution to mobility challenges. This, too, clearly demonstrates that we in Germany have everything necessary for success. However, we must genuinely want it. No one should block or hinder progress. Everyone should use their abilities to move forward and contribute to the greater good. Around the world – not only in the emerging markets of Asia, which are so important for our future – people are counting on Germany. They are placing their faith in our economic strength and our innovative spirit. And they trust in our contribution to global stability and peaceful development.

crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive economic potential, innovation, and a call to contribute to stability, which frames the situation as an achievement and a forward-looking opportunity rather than an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, after the tragic helicopter accident over Kabul, I received expressions of sympathy from all over the world. My deepest condolences also go to the families of the German soldiers during this difficult time. These soldiers gave their lives in pursuit of a peace that the world’s people desire. They provide security, which is essential for rebuilding a humane and democratic society. Sustainable security requires a successful economic recovery, and a recovery requires security. Our goal is not war, but its prevention through all necessary means. This combination of security and recovery has already significantly stabilized the Balkans, which at times was more volatile than the Middle East. Germany has a responsibility, given its history, to emphasize alternatives to war. We Germans know from our own experience that sometimes, dictators can only be stopped by force. However, we also know what bombs, destruction, and loss of homeland mean to people. Therefore, my policy remains committed to achieving the UN resolution without resorting to war. I am grateful for the support from the center of our society, for example, from churches and trade unions, for this policy.

paragraph_text: The realities of our world often stand in stark contrast to this Christmas peace. We have been particularly acutely aware of this in recent months. We were all horrified by the terrible terrorist attacks in America. We have all been following with concern and anxious questions, trying to understand the consequences – not just for America and Afghanistan, but also for us.

reason: It connects the international impact of terrorist attacks to the domestic concerns and anxieties of the German public.

paragraph_text: The German government and the Bundestag made the difficult decision for Germany to participate in military action against terrorism. No one took that decision lightly. I believe that is almost as important as the decision itself. Recent months have clearly demonstrated one thing: even as we decide to defend freedom and security, even through military means, that does not change the fact that our people have a deep-rooted love for peace and a profound aversion to war.

reason: It addresses the domestic political decision-making process regarding participation in international military actions.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames military action against terrorism as a necessary response to a security threat, which constitutes an acute crisis framing.

paragraph_text: I am grateful that German soldiers in Macedonia have helped to prevent another civil war. I also thank the soldiers who are protecting peace and freedom in Kosovo, Bosnia-Herzegovina, and elsewhere. Tonight, my thoughts also turn to the soldiers who are preparing for deployment, and to their wives and families. I want all our soldiers to know that they have the support not only of the parliament and the government, but also of the vast majority of our people.

paragraph_text: We want to nurture good relations with our neighbors in Europe. A long period of peace has brought us prosperity and great opportunities for personal fulfillment. We want to preserve peace within our own country. It will increasingly depend on how we shape peaceful coexistence between people of different backgrounds. Integration is a task that requires the contribution of everyone – both native-born citizens and immigrants. I believe that we can achieve this.

reason: It discusses European relations alongside the domestic challenge of integrating immigrants into German society.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, we are all about to witness history being made tomorrow. We are entering a new era, a dream that people across Europe have held for centuries: borderless travel and a single currency – the euro and cent. Many of you – as my family has – have purchased the so-called starter kits, and your children and grandchildren are excitedly examining the new money. You are, like us, carefully storing these first new coins, believing they are worth more than the twenty marks we paid for them. For many, this has also evoked a touch of nostalgia. The Deutsche Mark held great significance for us. It represents cherished memories of a prosperous era for the Federal Republic. But rest assured, even better times lie ahead. Disputes will, at most, occur between bureaucracies, but no longer between citizens within our European Union. This dream has become tangible with the introduction of a single currency. What we have all created together within the European Union can serve as a model for other regions around the world.

crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive significance of European integration and the transition to the euro as a means for future prosperity and peace, rather than framing these topics as threats or urgent dangers.

paragraph_text: After devastating wars, our neighbors extended their hands in reconciliation, allowing for cooperation, from which friendship blossomed. Our gratitude for this development is a key reason why we are helping to enable peace in other regions as well. We live in a world – that is why we are involved, for example, in the Balkans and in Afghanistan. We are helping with what we have best: our people. With diplomats and doctors, with paramedics and soldiers, with many other civilian helpers and volunteers. I am deeply grateful to everyone who is undertaking this dangerous reconstruction effort. I would also like to thank everyone who has shown their compassion through donations. And I hope that you will continue to generously support this reconstruction in the months and years to come. In recent years, we have learned that the international community expects more from Germany than it previously did: that we will use our economic and political strength to resolve conflicts beyond our own borders. We can no longer afford to stand apart. Even when diplomacy is no longer sufficient to resolve a conflict, and military action must be taken to end oppression and restore peace. Those who have experienced solidarity – and that is us, the Germans – must, in turn, offer solidarity when it is requested. Decency demands it. This applies to international politics as well.

reason: It links Germany's historical experience of reconciliation to its current international responsibilities and military involvement.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the proactive role of the state in international conflict resolution and reconstruction, which is framed as a policy objective rather than a response to an urgent, immediate threat to the nation's own safety or integrity.

paragraph_text: Dear fellow citizens, I stood before the ruins of the World Trade Center in New York. Like all of you, I watched the terrorist leaders celebrate on television. And I tell you: we will fight this terrorism with all our strength and with the appropriate means, wherever it may be. My government has responded comprehensively to this new challenge: with solidarity abroad and with increased vigilance at home. You can rely on this: your safety, the protection against terrorism and crime, is one of the most important tasks of the state.

reason: It addresses the international threat of terrorism and the corresponding domestic security measures taken by the state.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames international terrorism as a serious threat to security that requires urgent action through both global cooperation and domestic measures.

paragraph_text: The expansion of the European Union is our next major task. It is politically, economically, and morally necessary. And it will prove to be a success story in which everyone will ultimately be a winner. But even those who support the eastward expansion must take the existing concerns seriously and create transitional solutions, for example, for the gradual opening of labor markets. We will manage these transitions in an economically sound and fair manner towards our neighbors. A larger Europe is in our national interest. It is the politically and economically correct response to the challenges of globalization. Such a Europe will not tolerate racism, xenophobia, or, above all, antisemitism. Those who are irredeemable, whose foreign hatred now targets anyone who even looks foreign, these groups have no place in our state and in our society. As the federal government, we want to ensure that they face the full force of the law. But we need continued support from citizens who demonstrate civic courage and stand up against xenophobia, racism, and antisemitism. I thank you for your commitment and for standing up for these values.

reason: It addresses the European Union expansion and globalization while linking them to domestic social values and national interests.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on policy goals and societal values rather than framing these issues as urgent, existential threats requiring immediate defensive action.

paragraph_text: We also have a responsibility to history. I am therefore pleased that we were able to reach an agreement at the end of the old century regarding compensation for forced laborers. We Germans had a particular moral responsibility in this matter. And without an awareness of our past, we cannot forge a good path into the future. Germany will host the first World Expo of the new century in Hanover next year. It gives us an opportunity to demonstrate our openness and hospitality to the world. Let us also make the year 2000 a year of good neighborliness, a rejection of intolerance and xenophobia.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses historical responsibility for forced labor compensation, Germany's role in the upcoming World Expo, and the promotion of tolerance and neighborliness.
reason: It discusses historical compensation for forced laborers and hosting an international expo, blending national history with global engagement.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on historical reflection, international cooperation, and the promotion of positive social values, none of which are framed as an urgent threat or a situation requiring emergency response.

paragraph_text: Our country will continue to grow closer together in the new century. The better we manage this process of internal unity within the heart of Europe, the more of an example we set for the further integration of Europe. Only when we stand together can we succeed in this great task. In the new century, we will need to rethink our approach. We will need to re-evaluate our expectations and take on greater responsibility. We cannot simply leave our well-being to the state alone. I am convinced that we Germans have the strength and courage to embark on this new beginning. I am equally convinced that the fascinating opportunities of the new century will reward that courage. And our children and grandchildren will thank us if we provide them with prospects for a peaceful and fulfilling future. For the first time in our history, we Germans have the opportunity to begin a new century in a united country, living in peace and freedom. Before us lies the bright prospect of building a future of prosperity and democracy for all people in Europe. Let us seize these opportunities together. I wish you all the best for the year 2000.

reason: The text discusses internal German unity as a model for European integration.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on a positive vision for the future, emphasizing unity and prosperity rather than framing the current situation as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: There is also a risk of a divided society, not just between young and old. Think of the millions of people of foreign origin who live among us. Do we truly understand what drives them? Especially in times of global upheaval, it is important to know what unites us. We must therefore talk to one another across these boundaries: East with West, young with old, native-born with those who have come from elsewhere. For many years, I have been committed to fostering dialogue between cultures around the world, because I believe that is the only way to secure a lasting peaceful coexistence between people on this planet. But we also need this dialogue within our own country. It is challenging, but it is essential.

reason: It addresses the integration of foreign-born residents and the need for dialogue between cultures both domestically and globally.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on fostering unity and peaceful coexistence as a positive, aspirational goal rather than framing social diversity as an urgent threat or a crisis requiring emergency intervention.

paragraph_text: But starting tomorrow, dear fellow citizens, more will change. You will need to indicate on your bank transfers whether you want to pay in euros or, for a transitional period, still in Deutsche Mark. The common European currency is now a reality. And I am convinced that, together, we will make that currency a success.

reason: The adoption of the Euro is a national implementation of an international currency agreement.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a positive policy transition and expresses confidence in its success, which is framed as an achievement rather than a threat or urgent danger.

paragraph_text: This year, Germany will also chair the summit of the world's leading industrial nations in Cologne. We will focus particularly on developing an international financial architecture that prevents entire economies from collapsing due to speculation. Because we must remember this: when Indonesia, Thailand, or Brazil fall into crisis due to currency speculation, it is not just about numbers. It is about the lives of thousands and tens of thousands of people who have lost their livelihoods and whose hopes have been cruelly dashed. It is also about us – about our opportunities, about our ability to sell our products in those countries.

reason: Discusses international financial architecture and its direct impact on the domestic economy.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames speculative economic instability as an urgent threat to global livelihoods and trade, necessitating proactive reform to prevent future damage.

paragraph_text: There are many inspiring examples of community spirit and civic courage in our country, especially among young people. I am thinking of our soldiers and the many volunteers who worked tirelessly in the Oderbruch region. I am thinking of the soldiers who, at the risk of their own lives, are helping to secure peace in Bosnia-Herzegovina. These images are before our eyes. They represent our Bundeswehr (German Armed Forces). We will not tolerate, and will not allow, those who follow radical slogans.

paragraph_text: At the same time, Germany, our fatherland, is growing ever closer together. Despite all the challenges that still lie ahead, we are moving step by step towards the inner unity of our nation. Next year, we will decide on the common European currency. The euro will not only be a strong currency; it will also strengthen the European and German economies, benefiting all. Dear fellow citizens, let us act with courage and determination, in the spirit of freedom, responsibility, and charity. I wish you happiness, health, and success in 1998. God bless our German fatherland.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses the progress of German national unity and the upcoming adoption of the euro to strengthen the European and German economies.
reason: It links domestic national unity with the adoption of the European common currency.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive developments, specifically national unity and economic strengthening through the adoption of the euro, rather than framing these as responses to an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: Hi everyone, I hope you had a happy Christmas. Before we enter the new year, I want to take a moment to reflect. 2022 was tough. Just as we recovered from an unprecedented global pandemic, Russia launched a barbaric and illegal invasion across Ukraine. This has had a profound economic impact around the world, which the UK is not immune to. Now I know many of you have felt that impact at home.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the economic challenges facing the UK following the global pandemic and the Russian invasion of Ukraine.
reason: It links the global impact of the war in Ukraine to domestic economic consequences for the UK.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the economic situation as a set of ongoing challenges resulting from external shocks, focusing on the persistent negative impact rather than recovery or achievement.

paragraph_text: I'm not going to pretend that all our problems will go away in the new year, for 2023 will give us an opportunity to showcase the very best of Britain on the world stage, continuing to stand with our Ukrainian friends against Putin's brutality and defending freedom and democracy wherever we find it under threat. In this historic year of His Majesty the King's coronation, we will come together with pride in everything that makes this country great. Yes, 2023 will have its challenges, for the government I lead is putting your priorities first. I wish everyone a very Happy New Year.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister outlines priorities for 2023, including supporting Ukraine against Russian aggression and celebrating the coronation of King Charles III.
reason: It balances domestic priorities with the UK's international role in supporting Ukraine and global democracy.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the support for Ukraine against Russian aggression as a priority, which constitutes an active threat to international security and stability, while the mention of the coronation is a ceremonial event.

paragraph_text: This country has the fastest economic growth in the G7. We have more people in work now than there were before the pandemic began. And if you want a sign of future growth – look at the massive global investment in British high tech. Almost £30 billion into the UK tech sector this year - twice as much tech investment as Germany, three times as much as France.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights the United Kingdom's strong economic growth, high employment levels, and significant international investment in the domestic technology sector.
reason: It compares domestic economic growth and international investment in the UK against other nations.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive economic indicators, growth, and investment, which are framed as achievements rather than threats or urgent problems.

paragraph_text: In 2020 we have seen British scientists not only produce the world’s first effective treatment of the disease, but just in the last few days a beacon of hope has been lit in the laboratories of Oxford. A new room temperature vaccine that can be produced cheaply and at scale, and that offers literally a new lease of life to people in this country and around the world. And with every jab that goes into the arm of every elderly or vulnerable person, we are changing the odds, in favour of humanity and against Covid. And we know that we have a hard struggle still ahead of us for weeks and months, because we face a new variant of the disease that requires a new vigilance.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights the development of the Oxford COVID-19 vaccine while acknowledging the ongoing challenges posed by a new variant of the disease.
reason: It discusses both domestic vaccine development and the global impact of the pandemic and scientific progress.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly frames the new variant as an ongoing challenge, indicating an active threat that requires continued attention and vigilance.

paragraph_text: Of course the first item on my agenda is to fulfil the will of the electorate and take us out of the European Union. This should have happened already, but we were thwarted by a Parliament determined to use every trick in the book to stop us leaving the EU. Now we have a new Parliament, elected by the people to deliver the people’s priorities, which will finally respect the referendum and deliver Brexit. So we’ll get Brexit done before the end of this month. That oven-ready deal I talked about so much during the election campaign has already had its plastic covering pierced and been placed in the microwave. We got down to work immediately after the election. The necessary legislation has already begun its passage through Parliament and, once MPs return to Westminster, we’ll waste no time in finishing the job. At long last we will take back control of our laws, borders, money and trade.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister outlines the government's commitment to finalizing the United Kingdom's withdrawal from the European Union following the recent general election.
reason: Brexit involves international negotiations and trade, which directly impacts domestic laws and governance.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes the implementation of a political decision and the fulfillment of a government commitment, which is framed as a routine policy objective rather than an urgent threat or emergency.

paragraph_text: New Year is a time to look ahead and in 2019 the UK will start a new chapter. The Brexit deal I have negotiated delivers on the vote of the British people. It gives us contral over our borders, our money and our laws. It is good for jobs, protects our security, and works for our whole United Kingdom. In the next few weeks, MPs will have an important decision to make. If Parliament backs the deal, Britain can turn a corner. The referendum in 2016 was divisive, but we all want the best for our country. 2019 can be the year we put our differences aside and move forward together into a strong new relationship with our European neighbours and out into the world as a globally trading nation.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister urges Parliament to approve the negotiated Brexit deal to foster national unity and establish a new global trading relationship.
reason: It discusses the Brexit deal, which involves international relations and domestic trade policy.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the political process of approving a deal and establishing future relationships, framing the situation as a constructive step forward rather than an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: 2017 has been a year of progress for the United Kingdom. In January, I set out our objectives for the Brexit negotiations, and in the months since we have pursued them with steady purpose. In March we triggered Article 50, putting the decision of the British people into action. In December we reached agreement on the first phase of negotiations with our EU partners. Next year we will move on to the vital issues of trade and security, and I am determined that we will keep up our progress in 2018. Because whichever way you voted in the referendum, most people just want the government to get on and deliver a good Brexit, and that’s exactly what we are doing. Making a success of Brexit is crucial, but it will not be the limit of our ambitions.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister reviews the progress of Brexit negotiations throughout 2017 and outlines the government's commitment to future trade and security discussions.
reason: It covers the progress of Brexit negotiations, which are international in nature but domestic in political consequence.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the progress of negotiations and future commitments, which frames the situation as a routine matter of governance and diplomatic process rather than an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: We all want to see a Britain that is stronger than it is today. We all want a country that is fairer so that everyone has the chance to succeed. We all want a nation that is safe and secure for our children and grandchildren. These ambitions unite us, so that we are no longer the 52% who voted Leave and the 48% who voted Remain, but one great union of people and nations with a proud history and a bright future. So when I sit around the negotiating table in Europe this year, it will be with that in mind. The knowledge that I am there to get the right deal, not just for those who voted to Leave, but for every single person in this country. Of course, the referendum laid bare some further divisions in our country; between those who are prospering, and those who are not. Those who can easily buy their own home, send their children to a great school, find a secure job, and those who cannot. In short, those for whom our country works well, and those for whom it does not.
paragraph_summary: The speaker calls for national unity following the Brexit referendum while addressing socioeconomic inequalities and the government's commitment to securing a deal for all citizens.
reason: It links international Brexit negotiations with domestic social and economic inequality.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the post-referendum period as a situation requiring urgent national unity and government intervention to address socioeconomic inequalities, treating the political and social instability as a threat to the country's cohesion.

paragraph_text: We also promised something else in our manifesto: giving you a say on Europe, and now we’re delivering on that promise. There will be an in-out referendum by the end of 2017; it is written into the law of the land. I’m negotiating hard to fix the things that most annoy British people about our relationship with the EU. There’s just one thing that drives me: what is best for the national interest of our country. But in the end, it will be for you to decide: is our economic and national security in a dangerous world better protected by being in or out?
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister discusses the upcoming referendum on the United Kingdom's membership in the European Union.
reason: Concerns the UK's relationship with the EU and the impact of international membership on national security.
crisis_reason: The summary describes a political process and a scheduled referendum without using language that frames the situation as an urgent threat, danger, or emergency.

paragraph_text: And we will take on another social problem too: when our national security is threatened by a seething hatred of the West—one that turns people against their country and can even turn them into murderous extremists—I want us to be very clear: you will not defeat us. And we will not just confront the violence and the terror; we will take on the underlying poisonous narrative of grievance and resentment. We will come down hard on those who create the conditions for that narrative to flourish, and we will have greater confidence in—and indeed we will revel in—our way of life. Because if you walk our streets, learn in our schools, and benefit from our society, you sign up to our values: freedom, tolerance, responsibility, loyalty.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the threat of domestic extremism and the necessity of upholding national values to counter anti-Western narratives.
reason: Discusses national security threats posed by international extremist ideologies.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames domestic extremism and anti-Western narratives as an active, urgent threat to national values and stability, which requires a defensive response.

paragraph_text: These are the big challenges of our age—some of the biggest our nation has ever faced—and this year is a test of our mettle: whether we put up with poverty or put an end to it; ignore the glass ceiling or smash it; abandon the tenant or help make them a homeowner; appease the extremist or take apart the ideology piece by piece. We’ll get Britain a better deal in Europe, give families the peace of mind they crave, and make our country even more secure. That’s what this year—this turnaround decade—is all about. So let me wish everyone the very best and a very Happy New Year.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister outlines national priorities for the coming year, including addressing poverty, social inequality, housing, extremism, and the country's relationship with Europe.
reason: Covers a mix of domestic social reforms and the international relationship with Europe.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames poverty, inequality, housing, and extremism as urgent national priorities, which implies a threat to social stability and the integrity of the state.

paragraph_text: We've got clear and strong plans to bring down our deficit, which gives us some protection from the worst of the debt storms now battering the Eurozone. We have gained security for now -- and because of that, we must be bold, confident and decisive about building the future. I know much needs to change. We've got to do more to bring our economy back to health. So we've set out big plans for the transformation of our infrastructure, starting now -- with better roads and railways, superfast broadband and new homes. And while much of Europe's economy is struggling, other parts of the world are growing. There are huge opportunities for our businesses all over the planet. I'm determined to get out there and seize them.
model_reasons: storms: metaphorical reference to economic instability, not meteorological or climate-related
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister outlines plans to reduce the national deficit, invest in infrastructure, and pursue global trade opportunities to strengthen the economy.
reason: Connects domestic infrastructure and economic plans to the global Eurozone crisis and international trade.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes routine economic governance and future-oriented policy planning rather than framing the current economic situation as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: The British people have got what it takes -- and the government has got the ideas and policies we need. As we welcome the world to the best Olympics ever -- and as in the 60th year of her reign we honour our Queen as the finest and most famous example of British dedication, British duty, British steadiness, British tradition -- let's use these things as a mirror of ourselves too, a mirror of the nation. Resilient. Realistic. Intelligent. Curious. Enterprising. Inventive. Unswerving. It's the spirit that has made our universities among the best in the world, our scientists Nobel prize winners, our athletes gold medal winners, our culture, our music and our television famous everywhere, and our armed forces respected for their dedication and professionalism -- as they showed last year in Libya, and as they continue to show in Afghanistan.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights British national achievements, cultural strengths, and military service while celebrating the Queen's Diamond Jubilee and the London Olympics.
reason: Uses national identity to frame both domestic achievements and international military engagements.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on national achievements, cultural celebrations, and positive milestones, which are explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: Eight months ago we inherited an economy in deep trouble. The previous government had racked up the biggest budget deficit in our peacetime history. We only have to look at what’s been happening in Greece or Ireland to see the kind of danger we were in. Rising interest rates. Falling confidence. Others questioning whether you are still credit-worthy as a country. And, remember, the deficit we inherited back in May was actually forecast to be bigger than that of Ireland or Greece - or any other developed country for that matter. But we have pulled Britain out of that danger zone. Through the Budget and the Spending Review we’ve taken some really tough decisions to rescue our public finances and fundamentally change the direction of our economy. The new independent Office for Budget Responsibility forecasts the economy will grow continuing into 2011 and growth will rise further in 2012. So we have a credible plan for restoring confidence in our economy. But we have to see it through. A lot of the heavy lifting will happen in 2011. Each and every Minister in this Government is acutely aware that the plans we have in place are tough, in fact incredibly difficult, but we are clear that the alternative - indecision and delay - would mean taking unacceptable risks with our economy, our country and our people.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister outlines the government's fiscal strategy to reduce the national deficit and restore economic growth following the previous administration's financial challenges.
reason: Compares domestic economic performance and deficit management to other countries like Greece and Ireland.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the government's strategy for economic recovery and growth, framing the previous administration's challenges as a past event rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: Fourth and finally, I want to say something about our national security. For many years now we have been aware of the threat we face from international terrorism. Recent arrests show that that threat is still very much with us. And it is as serious today as it ever has been. As we enter the New Year our police officers, together with their colleagues in the security and intelligence agencies, are working around the clock to foil plots that would do terrible harm to our people and our economy. This government will be unstinting in the support it gives them. But they also depend on the support of the public as they go about their work: together we will defend our values and way of life and defeat those who threaten them. But we must ask ourselves as a country how we are allowing the radicalisation and poisoning of the minds of some young British Muslims who then contemplate and sometimes carry out acts of sickening barbarity. And the overwhelming majority of British Muslims who detest this extremism must help us to find the answers together.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the ongoing threat of international terrorism and the need for public cooperation to combat the radicalization of young British Muslims.
reason: Addresses the domestic threat of international terrorism and the need for community cooperation.
crisis_reason: The speaker explicitly frames international terrorism and radicalization as an ongoing threat that requires active public cooperation and intervention, which constitutes a chronic threat framed as an urgent danger.

paragraph_text: But in the fight against terrorism we cannot just protect ourselves at home. We also need to take action with our international partners abroad. Just before Christmas, the Prince of Wales and I visited service personnel being treated at the Royal Centre for Defence Medicine in Birmingham. It was a stark reminder of the incredible bravery and sacrifice being made by all our servicemen and women who put their lives on the line to keep us safe. For those serving in Afghanistan, 2011 is a crucial year in which we will start to transfer security responsibility for districts and provinces to Afghan control. As the Afghans become steadily more capable of looking after their own security, so we will be able to start to bring our own forces home. Enterprise, aspiration, public service reform and national security: these are the things that will determine whether in 2011 we take the steps towards the better, stronger, safer Britain that is within our grasp. I am determined that we will. That together, we have the right plan to pull through the tough times ahead. And that if 2010 was the year we stopped the rot, we can make 2011 the year that Britain gets back on her feet.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses international counter-terrorism efforts, the transition of security responsibilities in Afghanistan, and national recovery goals for 2011.
reason: Discusses international military operations in Afghanistan and their link to domestic security.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the transition of security responsibilities and national recovery goals, which frames the situation as a post-crisis phase rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: In 2009 we said goodbye to Harry Patch, the last survivor of the generation of soldiers who risked their lives for Britain in the Great War. As we mourned him, we remembered too all those who have laid down their lives in the defence of this country we love. Last year was particularly tough for our forces in Afghanistan, and we face some more difficult months ahead. In late December we were reminded at this decade’s end, just as we were at its beginning, that there is a terrorist threat which puts our safety and security at risk and which requires us to take on al Qaeda and the Taliban at the epicentre of global terrorism – which is the mountains of Afghanistan and Pakistan. And while progress is difficult, I am working every day and will continue to step up our efforts with no let-up to disrupt the ability of terrorists to mount attacks on Britain, and to work with other countries to prevent terrorism on our streets. This will have my relentless focus in 2010, just as Afghanistan will have. We will steadily be giving more control of Afghanistan to Afghan troops and police.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister discusses the ongoing military commitment in Afghanistan, the threat of international terrorism, and the transition of security responsibilities to Afghan forces.
reason: Links the domestic terrorist threat to international military operations in Afghanistan and Pakistan.
crisis_reason: The paragraph explicitly identifies international terrorism as a threat and frames the military commitment in Afghanistan as a security challenge requiring ongoing attention and strategic transition.

paragraph_text: So let me talk to you about my key priorities for the coming year – about how we will secure the recovery and make the new decisions of this new decade. My first priority is securing that recovery. The second is radical improvement and reform of our public services, protecting frontline spending on schools, hospitals and the police. The third is a new, cleaned up politics. And the fourth is maintaining Britain’s global strength and fulfilling our responsibilities against the terrorist threat in Afghanistan and across the wider world.
paragraph_summary: The speaker outlines four key priorities for the coming year: economic recovery, public service reform, political integrity, and maintaining global security and counter-terrorism efforts.
reason: The paragraph combines domestic public service reform with international security and counter-terrorism efforts.
crisis_reason: The paragraph outlines routine governance priorities and policy goals for the upcoming year rather than framing these areas as urgent, active threats or existential dangers.

paragraph_text: That is why we are fighting so hard to secure and sustain Britain’s recovery. The last 18 months have been difficult, and too many people were hit by a crisis that originated in the US housing market and then came rolling across the Atlantic towards us – a crisis that hit Britain hard because the financial sector is such an important part of our economy.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses the government's efforts to sustain Britain's economic recovery following the impact of the global financial crisis.
reason: It discusses the domestic impact of a financial crisis that originated in the United States.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the government's efforts to sustain economic recovery, which frames the situation as a post-crisis achievement rather than an active or ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: This season is above all a time to pay tribute to those who serve and sacrifice for our country, often in places far away. And we pledge that the men and women on the frontlines of our security, at home and overseas, will have all the resources they need for our defence and their own safety.
paragraph_summary: The speaker honors military personnel serving at home and abroad while pledging continued support and resources for national defense and their personal safety.
reason: It addresses national security and military support for operations both at home and abroad.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on honoring personnel and routine support for defense, which is framed as a standard duty of the state rather than an urgent response to an active threat.

paragraph_text: And we will continue to work with our international partners to counter the ongoing threat of global terrorism, most recently witnessed in the atrocities in Pakistan. So we will not shirk but see through changes and reforms in the vital areafor our future - secure energy, pensions, transport, welfare, education, health and national security. We will strengthen the democracy and unity of our country. Our priority at all times, our guiding purpose: One Britain of security and opportunity for all the British people.
