The King and Queen's ability to recognize and acknowledge the sorrow, joys, laughter, and tears of individuals has been remarkable. Through closeness, genuine interest, and their presence, the King and Queen have left their own unique mark on the Norwegian monarchy. We wish the entire royal family a happy new year.
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses appreciation for the King and Queen's personal connection to the Norwegian people and their unique impact on the monarchy.
reason: It expresses gratitude toward the Norwegian royal family for their domestic service.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on expressing gratitude and reflecting on the symbolic role of the monarchy, which is a ceremonial and affirmative framing rather than a threat-based one.

paragraph_text: Last November, we learned the story of Odin, a young man who, after years of bullying, took his own life at just 13 years old. It's a deeply moving story. Odin said, "It's no use reporting it, because they won't do anything anyway. " Exclusion and bullying can rob children and young people of precious years. Both children, young people, and adults must contribute to stopping bullying and exclusion. Adults have a particular responsibility. It must be worthwhile to report incidents to an adult. We must care, and not dismiss children who are struggling. This also applies to us, as parents. We must take responsibility for how our own children behave. In that way, we can all make a difference.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the tragedy of youth bullying and emphasizes the collective responsibility of adults and parents to prevent exclusion and support struggling children.
reason: It addresses the domestic issue of bullying in schools and the responsibility of parents and adults.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames youth bullying as a serious social threat that requires urgent collective intervention and responsibility, characterizing it as a damaging issue rather than a routine matter.

paragraph_text: Some people also did something extra for others this Christmas. Healthcare workers. Volunteers who organized Christmas events for those who might otherwise have been alone. Most of us have enjoyed a good and peaceful Christmas with family and friends. Some may have felt a sense of sadness and longing. Perhaps someone was missing. Someone we loved dearly. Some of those we lost in 2014 died from cancer. While it offers no comfort to those who have lost loved ones, cancer treatment is constantly improving, thankfully. More and more people are surviving the disease. In the future, even more will survive. Healthcare professionals make a difference every day that they work.
paragraph_summary: The speaker acknowledges the contributions of healthcare workers and volunteers while reflecting on personal loss and advancements in cancer treatment.
reason: It discusses domestic healthcare and the impact of cancer on Norwegian families.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on gratitude for past service and personal reflection on medical advancements, which frames the content as an acknowledgement of achievement and recovery rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: However, some people are diagnosed with cancer too late. Others have to wait too long during treatment. It is the responsibility of politicians to organize healthcare better. At the beginning of the new year, we have established diagnostic centers throughout the country. These centers will quickly assess patients when there is suspicion of cancer. We have also created what is called a "care pathway" for cancer patients. It's not the patient who is the package; the patient should receive the package. A care pathway means that treatment is tailored to a standardized approach based on the best available knowledge of what provides effective treatment. Patients will receive a dedicated coordinator. These changes are designed to adapt the system to the needs of the individual. Often, patients have experienced the opposite. It won't be perfect from day one, but we are underway. We are creating a healthcare system that puts patients first.
paragraph_summary: The government is implementing diagnostic centers and standardized care pathways to improve the efficiency and patient-centered quality of cancer treatment.
reason: It outlines domestic policy changes and improvements to the national healthcare system.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes routine healthcare policy improvements and administrative efficiency measures rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: From today, people with significant needs will have a legal right to user-directed personal assistance. Many people with disabilities will no longer have to adapt their lives to fit the system. They will be able to decide how the assistance they are allocated will be provided and who will provide it. It's about opportunities for everyone. It's about equality. We are strengthening our welfare programs for cancer patients and people with disabilities. We can do this because we have both the human and economic resources to do so.
paragraph_summary: The government is granting people with disabilities a legal right to user-directed personal assistance to improve equality and welfare services.
reason: It discusses domestic welfare programs and legal rights for people with disabilities in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a routine policy improvement aimed at enhancing welfare and equality, rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat or a crisis requiring immediate defensive action.

paragraph_text: For decades, the oil industry has been a driving force behind our economic growth. It has created significant revenues, secure jobs, and made it possible to create one of the world's best welfare systems. We have long known that the oil industry would eventually peak. There is growing evidence that this has now happened. New industries must contribute more to supporting our welfare programs and driving our country forward. We must make it more profitable to invest in new Norwegian jobs. We must become better at creating new opportunities. Therefore, this year, we will be making a historic investment in research, innovation, and knowledge. This will give us the strength to adapt. Some families may experience greater uncertainty next year. That's why it's so important to create more new jobs. Therefore, we must prepare Norway for the future.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses transitioning Norway's economy from oil dependency toward new industries through increased investment in research and innovation to ensure future job security.
reason: It addresses the national economic transition away from oil and the need for domestic innovation.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a strategic economic transition and investment in innovation as a proactive measure for future prosperity rather than framing the current state as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: Dear everyone, After the holidays, many are looking forward to returning to work. We are excited about the opportunities and challenges that the new year will bring. Some have taken the opportunity to set new, optimistic goals. Others are more uncertain about the future. For some, finding a job can be difficult enough. For those who are employed, it can be a daily struggle to manage everyday life.
paragraph_summary: The speaker acknowledges the diverse experiences of citizens regarding employment, daily life challenges, and varying levels of optimism for the coming year.
reason: It reflects on the domestic labor market and the personal challenges of everyday life in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a general reflection on the diverse experiences and sentiments of the population without framing these conditions as urgent threats, dangers, or systemic failures requiring immediate intervention.

paragraph_text: I am thinking of those affected by mental health issues. Half of us will experience them at some point in our lives. This is one of our major public health challenges. Most of us probably know someone who is struggling. But you may not know who they are. Mental health issues can be difficult to talk about. However, there is much we can do. The call on World Mental Health Day was: "See each other – make a difference. " Giving compliments is a good way to prevent problems. It’s also very pleasant! It’s easy to think that we need to be cautious when approaching someone who is struggling. But often, that is when they need us the most. It can be difficult to admit that you need treatment. It is important that we help those who need it to access treatment. We can also take responsibility for our own mental health and seek help early on.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the prevalence of mental health challenges and encourages social support, early intervention, and open communication to improve public well-being.
reason: Addresses domestic public health challenges and mental health support within Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames mental health challenges as a significant public well-being issue requiring active intervention and social support, which constitutes a chronic threat to social health.

paragraph_text: There is more we can do. We, as adults, must address bullying among children and young people. A damaged childhood can leave deep scars. A good childhood lasts a lifetime. In the years to come, the government will work to strengthen mental health services. Currently, they are not good enough. It must be easier to get help. The challenges faced by children and young people must be identified early on. This will help us prevent small problems from growing into larger ones. Fortunately, it is possible to recover fully.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for addressing childhood bullying and improving mental health services to ensure better support and early intervention for young people.
reason: Focuses on domestic policy regarding bullying and mental health services for Norwegian youth.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames childhood bullying and mental health issues as urgent problems requiring intervention and support, treating them as threats to the well-being of young people.

paragraph_text: Many will return to work. It’s not always easy. Some find that doors close when they have been out of work for a period. They have a gap in their CV. This creates a double burden. I would like to encourage employers to hire more people who have a gap in their CV. There are many competent and hardworking people who are eager for a new opportunity. Those who have taken such a chance often report that the work environment has improved. The company Stormberg employs as much as 25 percent of its staff who previously had difficulty finding work. This is an example worth following. We want to create a working life that includes everyone.
model_reasons: environment: refers to the workplace atmosphere, not the natural environment
paragraph_summary: The speaker encourages employers to hire individuals with employment gaps to foster a more inclusive working life.
reason: Discusses domestic labor market policies and encouraging employment for those with gaps in their CVs.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as a positive, aspirational call for social inclusion and workplace improvement rather than a response to an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: Last autumn, I visited Lillehammer Car Damage Centre. The company is working to strengthen employees’ skills in reading, writing, and IT. Mats Lien himself told me that when he obtained his vocational certificate 16 years ago, they never even touched a computer in school. Today, they cannot do their job without one. Therefore, it is crucial to improve the skills of adults so that they do not fall behind. The requirements for knowledge are increasing.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the necessity of improving adult education and digital skills to keep pace with evolving workplace requirements.
reason: Highlights domestic vocational training and the need for adult skill development in the Norwegian workforce.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the need for education and digital skills as a routine policy goal for workplace adaptation rather than an urgent threat to national stability or safety.

paragraph_text: It is a challenge that Norwegian students are not learning enough in school, particularly in mathematics and science. Almost one in four 15-year-olds are performing at a critically low level in mathematics. This is a serious issue, both for those students and for society as a whole. Many of them are unable to complete upper secondary school and risk being without a job. It is also a serious issue for our society. It is expensive to produce goods in Norway. We will never be the cheapest. Therefore, we must be smart. That is why the government is so focused on creating Norway’s knowledge society. Knowledge is the oil of the future for Norway.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses declining student performance in mathematics and science, emphasizing the necessity of a knowledge-based economy for Norway's future competitiveness.
reason: Addresses domestic educational performance and the importance of knowledge for the Norwegian economy.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames declining educational performance as a direct threat to the nation's future competitiveness and economic viability, which constitutes a chronic issue framed as an urgent danger.

paragraph_text: I have met students who managed to complete their education thanks to an engaged teacher. I have met students who found joy in mathematics and foreign languages because they had a fantastic teacher. In April, I visited the students in the science education program in Trondheim. It is almost as difficult to get into that program as it is to get into an engineering program. They told me that they wanted to become teachers because they themselves had been inspired by a skilled teacher. We will build on all the good things in our schools. The government will invest heavily in making good teachers even better. More students must have the opportunity to meet their dream teacher. You, as teachers, should know that you are doing Norway’s most important job. The future of many young people lies in your hands.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of teachers in the Norwegian education system and outlines government plans to invest in their professional development.
reason: Focuses on domestic teacher quality and the importance of the education system in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a routine government policy announcement regarding professional development and investment in education, which is framed as a positive development rather than a response to an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: We, as parents, also have a significant responsibility. The most important thing is to talk to our children about how important school is. We must expect effort from our children. It can be boring to solve math problems for a small child who would rather play. But it means so much when a mother or father tells their child how proud they are when the homework is finally finished. We entrust our six-year-olds to the school on their first day. One day, they will let go of our hand and go to school on their own. They need all the knowledge that school can provide. “Education is the most powerful weapon we can use to change the world, ” said Nelson Mandela. He was absolutely right. Education has also helped to transform Norway. In the early 1800s, our predecessors fought for Norway to have its own university. Only then could Norway create its own future. The Danes were skeptical, but eventually had to give in. In 1813, the University of Oslo opened. It was an important step towards Norwegian independence.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of parental support for children's education and highlights the role of academic institutions in Norway's historical development and independence.
reason: Discusses the role of parents and the history of the Norwegian education system and independence.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive role of education and parental support in national development and history, which are presented as routine and affirmative values rather than urgent threats.

paragraph_text: This year, on May 17th, we will celebrate the 200th anniversary of our constitution. The new year will be marked by celebrations. It is rare that we are so proud, as when we celebrate our Constitution on May 17th. We celebrate the values that are important to us: democracy, community, and freedom. The way we celebrate also says a lot. Children, the future, and optimism are at the heart of it all. I hope many will participate in this year’s celebrations. I look forward to celebrating our Constitution with you. The Royal Family has played a significant role in Norway’s independence. Therefore, the Royal Family has a particularly important role to play in this jubilee year. Tonight, I would like to extend my greetings to our beloved Royal Family and thank them for their important contribution to our country.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses the upcoming bicentennial celebration of the Norwegian Constitution, emphasizing national values and the significant role of the Royal Family.
reason: Covers the domestic celebration of the Norwegian Constitution and the role of the Royal Family.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on a national celebration, historical values, and the role of the Royal Family, which are ceremonial and affirmative topics rather than threats requiring urgent response.

paragraph_text: Dear everyone, This year marks exactly 100 years since Norwegian women gained full voting rights, equal to those of men. It was certainly long overdue, as we think today. But at the time, it was a milestone, and it marked the beginning of an adventure for an entire nation. Over the past 100 years, increased equality has sparked hope, fueled dreams, and given new strength to the belief in a better society. This is what we will celebrate this year! And we will remember the women who paved the way, those who demanded what was obvious – the right to decide for themselves.
paragraph_summary: The speech commemorates the centenary of Norwegian women gaining full voting rights and reflects on the impact of gender equality on society.
reason: Reflects on the domestic history of women's voting rights in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is a commemorative reflection on a historical milestone and the positive impact of gender equality, which does not frame the situation as an urgent threat or a crisis.

paragraph_text: Sami Eli Anne Nystad also showed courage. She was harassed and nearly set on fire by a group of young people. The 23-year-old refused to accept it. Instead of bowing her head, she publicly confronted the bullies. Eli Anne turned a personal humiliation into a campaign against bullying, and became a role model. Another role model is 15-year-old Juliane Asdal from Tromøy. She could no longer bear to see the internet flooded with blatant harassment. Therefore, she started the anti-bullying campaign "Stop it" on Facebook. The campaign is now followed by young people from north to south. I agree with Eli Anne and Juliane. No one deserves to be bullied, and no one should be allowed to threaten others without consequences. Therefore, the government wants to punish hateful online speech. Next spring, we will present a bill on this to the Storting (Parliament). But remember – we all have a responsibility to stand up against bullying.
model_reasons: fire: refers to a physical act of violence, not environmental fire
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights individual anti-bullying efforts and announces upcoming government legislation to criminalize hateful online speech.
reason: Discusses domestic anti-bullying initiatives and proposed national legislation regarding online speech.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames hateful online speech as a serious threat to social cohesion and safety, necessitating urgent government intervention through new criminal legislation.

paragraph_text: One of them was Fernanda Nissen, a prominent citizen who stood at the forefront of the match factory workers' strike in 1889, and who responded to those who claimed that women were not suited for politics. "Damned nonsense, " she retorted. Grete Waitz was another – a sporting legend who created magical moments, a person who broke down barriers. Grete Waitz showed young women around the world that no distance is too great. When Gro Harlem Brundtland formed her women-led government in 1986, she made history. Eight out of 18 government members were women. At a girls' school in Japan, the breakthrough was celebrated with the poem "The Day the Mountains Will Move. " However, the greatest mountain moved in silence. Because the most important thing is that women could finally make their own choices. Work at home, or enter the workforce? Within 40 years, 600, 000 women took the step into paid employment. Today, young women dominate fields like law and medicine. Education was and is the key to freedom for women, the key to the future.
paragraph_summary: The speech highlights the historical progress of women's rights, political participation, and workforce integration in Norway, emphasizing the transformative role of education.
reason: Details the domestic progress of women in the Norwegian workforce and political sphere.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on historical progress, achievements, and positive societal developments rather than presenting a threat or an urgent problem requiring intervention.

paragraph_text: Therefore, it is so encouraging that Norwegian schools – and our teachers – are now showing such progress in international assessments. This helps create one of the world's most equal countries. With courageous women as role models, we will dare to envision an ideal for our country: an inclusive, equal, and safe Norway. A society of five million individuals – woven together into a nation built on trust in one another. The contract is simple: everyone must contribute according to their ability, and everyone should receive according to their needs. Freedom and security – created by the community – that is the magic of our country. Americans have their dream. We have the Norwegian model. Our path may not seem as adventurous, but it is all the more secure. When we combine freedom and security, more people can realize their dreams. Therefore, my assertion is: it is easier to realize the American dream in Norway than in America. That is something we should be proud of, and something we should cherish. This is Norway. This is us.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights the success of the Norwegian model in fostering equality, social trust, and security to help citizens realize their personal dreams.
reason: Compares the Norwegian social model to the American dream to emphasize domestic values.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the success of a national model and positive social outcomes, which is an affirmative framing rather than a threat-based one.

paragraph_text: At the same time, I caution against believing that things will simply happen on their own, or believing that there are any shortcuts. The strength of Norwegian solidarity lies in the fact that everyone contributes. No one can simply demand. Everyone must ask themselves: what is my contribution? If we allow some to evade their responsibilities, for example, by evading taxes, defrauding social welfare programs, or giving themselves excessive salaries while others show restraint, then we undermine the glue that holds our society together and weaken trust in the community.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes that maintaining Norwegian social cohesion and trust requires individual responsibility and contributions while condemning tax evasion, welfare fraud, and excessive personal compensation.
reason: Addresses domestic social responsibility, tax compliance, and welfare integrity.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames social fragmentation and the erosion of trust as an urgent threat to the integrity of the state, demanding individual responsibility and condemning specific behaviors as damaging to the social fabric.

paragraph_text: Norway is not a perfect country, but it is a good country. Many European countries are currently experiencing crises. We are a country that is growing. We are conducting more research, developing more, and creating new businesses. Norway is moving in the right direction. It is all the more important that we stand up for those who are on the outside – those struggling to make ends meet, those who are lonely, or those who are struggling in other ways. It feels particularly difficult in a country where so many people are doing well.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights Norway's economic growth and development while emphasizing the need to support vulnerable citizens who are struggling amidst general national prosperity.
reason: Discusses the domestic economic situation and the need to support vulnerable groups within Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on national prosperity and economic growth as the primary context, framing the support for vulnerable citizens as a matter of social policy rather than an urgent threat to the state or society.

paragraph_text: Here at home, last year was marked by courageous people. Just a month ago, we honored 22 women and men with the Edel dåd medal for the courage they showed when terror struck Norway. Each and every one of them who arrived alone and first, had to make a decision. It was a matter of life and death – the lives of others, their own lives. Without helmets or protective gear, they crossed the line of fear. With their own hands, they saved lives. Honoring them is a tribute to humanity's deep-seated desire for goodness.
paragraph_summary: The speaker honors individuals who demonstrated exceptional courage and saved lives during a terrorist attack in Norway.
reason: Honors individuals for their courage during a domestic terrorist event.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on honoring individuals for their past actions during a terrorist attack, which frames the content as a reflection on a past event and a tribute to heroism rather than an active, ongoing threat requiring current response.

paragraph_text: Dear everyone, Two months ago, little Danica was born in Manila. She became the world’s seven billionth resident. In a few months, a new child will be born in Norway, becoming our five millionth citizen. What will the future hold for these two children? What future awaits them? That’s what parents ask. It’s what parents have always asked, throughout history, in every country, when first encountering a new life. In that magical moment, a contract for eternity is forged – a loving bond that can withstand anything. That’s why it’s so unbearably brutal when a young person dies. No mother should lose a son. No father should lose a daughter. On July 22nd last year, so many were affected. Fathers, mothers, siblings, and grandparents were forced to walk the difficult path to the graves. It was so heartbreaking. I have thought about the survivors and those left behind every day since. How are you doing? Why did this happen? Five months later, we still need answers to understand. 2012 will bring new answers. First, there will be the trial of the perpetrator. Then, the commission will present its honest and unvarnished account of July 22nd. That means we must confront evil. I am confident that we can do it.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the July 22nd attacks, expressing empathy for the victims' families and anticipating the upcoming trial and commission report.
reason: The paragraph focuses on national mourning and the domestic response to the July 22nd attacks in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the aftermath, empathy for victims, and the legal/administrative process of the trial and report, rather than framing the event as an active, ongoing threat requiring urgent response.

paragraph_text: Last summer, the Norwegian people made history. Initially, we were stunned by the bomb and the gunfire. Then, something remarkable happened. We rose up, shook off our fear, and stood tall for democracy. We felt it in the processions of roses and the torchlight parades. In the funerals, churches, mosques, and in front of television screens. We were one people. Faced with the worst that life could offer, we brought out the best within ourselves. It has strengthened my belief in the goodness of humanity, in hope, and in the power of a people’s will.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the national unity and resilience demonstrated by the Norwegian people following the 2011 terrorist attacks.
reason: The text reflects on the internal national unity and emotional response of the Norwegian people following a domestic tragedy.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the resilience and unity shown in the aftermath of a past event, framing it as a reflection on history rather than an active or ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: This year, I would like to send a particularly warm greeting to His Majesty King Harald and Her Majesty Queen Sonja. Perhaps never before in peacetime has the Royal Family been so close to its people as after July 22nd. With their presence and warmth, they offered comfort. The King, the Queen, and the Crown Prince, soothed pain with their wise words. This year, both the King and the Queen are celebrating their 75th birthday. Five million Norwegians will express their gratitude, offer congratulations, and wish the best to King Harald and Queen Sonja during this jubilee year.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister honors King Harald and Queen Sonja for their comforting role following the July 22nd attacks and celebrates their 75th birthdays.
reason: It addresses the domestic role and celebration of the Norwegian Royal Family.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on honoring the monarchs for their past role and celebrating their birthdays, which frames the content as ceremonial and commemorative rather than treating the attacks as an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: Dear everyone, on December 14th, I participated in a celebration commemorating Roald Amundsen and his men at the South Pole. It was a momentous occasion. People from all over the world honored the polar heroes and the Norwegian flag. The sound of "Ja vi elsker" became part of the vast, white silence. Norway felt so close, even though we were so far away. There, in that most distant place, I saw a Norway that we can all be proud of. A land with pioneers who have left a lasting mark on history. A people who were able to respond to adversity with kindness, as we experienced during the brutal attacks this summer. And everyday heroes who make this small country great. Let us shape the future with that spirit. Happy New Year!
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the legacy of Norwegian polar explorers and the resilience of the Norwegian people in the face of national adversity.
reason: The text reflects on national identity and pride through the lens of Norwegian history and domestic resilience.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on historical legacy and national resilience as a reflection on identity rather than framing a specific, ongoing, or acute threat that requires urgent action.

paragraph_text: Prime Minister Stefan Löfven's Christmas address in Visby, December 15, 2019. Spoken word prevails. Thank you. Many of us are looking forward to Christmas. We long to relax with our families and enjoy Janssons frestelse. But Christmas is also a yearly reminder of something larger. It’s a reminder of our country’s greatest strengths, our brightest lights: cohesion, security, and a strong, shared welfare system. Even though many of us can take some time off, nurses and police officers continue their vital work. They celebrate without their families so that we can feel safe doing so with ours. If the unthinkable were to happen, and Christmas celebrations were interrupted by emergency services or concerns for loved ones, they would be there to help us during heart attacks or after a break-in. And Christmas is a reminder that, regardless of the season, holiday, year, or day, we must never become indifferent to the well-being of our neighbors or the health of those around us. When newspapers offer tips on how families can get along during Christmas, some relatives start talking about politics, while others realize they don't have a single loved one to argue with or spend Christmas Eve with – or any other day, for that matter. The Salvation Army representatives I met earlier today are dedicating their time and energy to combating loneliness, collecting Christmas presents, and spreading joy. But ultimately, it’s a responsibility we all share. Christmas is also a reminder of our country’s most painful weaknesses, as poverty and vulnerability are starkly highlighted against the backdrop of advertising and filtered images on Facebook that portray an idealized version of Christmas. When colleagues chat about how stressful it is to buy Christmas presents, others are thinking about how they’re going to manage to afford even a single present to put under the tree for their children. This – illness, loneliness, poverty – can never be reduced to just individual problems. It’s our collective responsibility. It’s politics. Today, I want to talk about how we can build a strong Sweden where we take that collective responsibility for each other, on Christmas Eve and on Monday mornings, in large cities and rural areas, simply because we humans feel better when we do, and because our country becomes stronger.
paragraph_summary: Prime Minister Stefan Löfven emphasizes collective responsibility for addressing social issues like poverty, loneliness, and inequality to strengthen Swedish society.
reason: The paragraph discusses domestic welfare, social services, and the collective responsibility of Swedish citizens.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames poverty, loneliness, and inequality as urgent social issues that threaten the strength of society, requiring collective responsibility and action.

paragraph_text: In the Social Democrats’ party platform, it is stated that together, we can create a better future. It may sound obvious, but it’s worth remembering in a time when the future is often portrayed as an endless, dark November gloom, or even worse, as predetermined. Sweden has made an incredible journey over the past hundred years. Many of us live lives that our grandparents could scarcely have imagined. Convinced that the future can be better, we have built Sweden. Not individually, not as isolated individuals striving only for their own benefit, not as consumers in a market, but together. We have chosen to have healthcare and education for everyone. We believe in equality and parental leave. We may not be world champions at small talk with strangers on the bus, but we have a strong community life and a sense of belonging. The most important lesson from Sweden’s history is that society can be changed. Nothing is permanent; everything can be altered or rebuilt. But to make that possible, everyone must know that they are a part of society. That they have a role in building Sweden. Pessimism about the country’s future, and despair about one’s own tomorrow, are the worst poisons that can spread within a person and within a society. The Social Democrats’ party platform begins with a description of the kind of society we want to build: a democratic society based on the equal value and rights of all people, comprised of free and equal individuals in a solidary society. It may sound abstract, but it’s clear. We simply want to build a Sweden where every person is free to create a good life, regardless of their parents’ income or last name. A Sweden where people’s equal value is a constant. Guided by those principles, we collectively built our welfare state. It remains our mission today, tomorrow, and in a hundred years.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of collective action, social solidarity, and equal opportunity in shaping Sweden's future and maintaining its welfare state.
reason: It outlines the domestic political vision and historical development of the Swedish welfare state.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive societal values and the maintenance of the welfare state as a constructive goal rather than framing these as responses to an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: Social Democrats can hold internal meetings and wish that the Social Democrats’ party platform were implemented perfectly. But the election result doesn’t allow for that; reality doesn’t work that way. And of course, we could have acted like a disgruntled child, sitting in a corner and saying that we are right, refusing to compromise even a fraction, and happily studying our beautiful party platform while the Moderates, Christian Democrats, and Sweden Democrats dismantle the welfare system. But that’s a view of politics as a game, where the goal is to never get your hands dirty during the day, so that you can say at night that you haven’t compromised even a fraction – but at the same time, you haven’t improved the lives of even one person. That’s a perspective I can’t share. No, politics isn’t a game. Politics concerns people’s freedom, opportunities, and lives. For me, politics is about ensuring that parents can afford to buy a Christmas present for their children. Politics is about ensuring that nurses have more colleagues. Social democracy is about ensuring that no one feels truly alone when facing major challenges, because you need a strong society standing by your side. I am proud of Sweden’s history, which has often been built through broad solutions, where many contribute, take responsibility, and create stability. And I am equally proud that we are continuing to build that society through collaboration and compromise, for the benefit of the country.
paragraph_summary: The speaker defends the necessity of political compromise and collaboration to improve citizens' lives and maintain social stability over rigid adherence to party ideology.
reason: The text focuses on domestic political strategy, compromise, and the implementation of national welfare policies.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the value of political collaboration and compromise as a standard governance approach rather than framing the political situation as an urgent threat or emergency.

paragraph_text: To function, a political collaboration requires a shared goal and a willingness to sit down at the table with the conviction that it is for Sweden’s best interests. In several instances, the leader of the Moderate Party, Ulf Kristersson, made a promise: to never collaborate with the Sweden Democrats. Ulf Kristersson cited several strong reasons why such collaboration was not an option, pointing to the party’s questionable values, its dark history, and the presence of xenophobic elements – all contrary to what he stood for. However, that has apparently changed. Recently, Ulf Kristersson broke that promise when he and Jimmie Åkesson met and publicly announced their collaboration. It is a mystery how a Moderate Party leader could possibly believe that starting to collaborate with Åkesson is truly in Sweden’s best interest. I have been quite astonished over the past week, as the Moderate leader has attempted to explain his broken promise. Ulf describes that a condition of the Alliance agreement was not collaborating with the Sweden Democrats, but insists that it was not a demand he pushed. He claims it was a requirement from the Centre Party and the Liberals. That is truly astonishing. The promise to never collaborate with the Sweden Democrats is now presented as a strategic maneuver to hold the Alliance together, a tactic discarded the moment opinion polls demanded it. This kind of approach, where everything is a political strategy and nothing is sacred, risks completely eroding trust in political parties and in democracy itself.
paragraph_summary: The speaker criticizes Ulf Kristersson for breaking his promise not to collaborate with the Sweden Democrats, arguing this undermines trust in political institutions.
reason: It critiques domestic political alliances and the internal trust within the Swedish parliamentary system.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the breach of political promises as a direct threat to the integrity of democratic institutions and public trust, which constitutes a chronic threat framed as an urgent danger.

paragraph_text: However, Jimmie Åkesson’s meeting with Ulf Kristersson also sends a different message to voters. We are aware of the Sweden Democrats’ dark history, but I believe that many who might consider voting for the party are, above all, dissatisfied. Dissatisfied with social democracy, with the Moderates, with the healthcare system, or with politics in general. That is certainly a lesson for all of us. But the message now being sent to the Sweden Democrats’ voters concerns the direction the party will take in the future. They often try to portray themselves as representing ordinary people, yet they are aligning themselves with the Moderates in a conservative, right-wing bloc. Today, the Sweden Democrats advocate a policy that is hostile to trade unions, anti-wage earners, and fundamentally right-wing. It is impossible to be certain which leader is truly the candidate for prime minister within that conservative, right-wing bloc. But one thing is clear: their policies represent something entirely different from what we associate with Sweden – our security, our unity. I can promise that we social democrats, and the entire labor movement, will continue the debate, continue to scrutinize, examine, and question. But above all, we will show our alternative – a strong society where social welfare always comes first, and we will demonstrate what unity for all can achieve for a country and for its people. That is my promise.
paragraph_summary: The speaker critiques the Sweden Democrats' political alignment with the Moderates and contrasts their right-wing policies with the Social Democrats' focus on social welfare.
reason: The paragraph addresses domestic political competition and the ideological direction of Swedish political parties.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a routine political critique and a contrast between party platforms, which constitutes standard political discourse rather than the framing of a specific threat to the state or society.

paragraph_text: The dissatisfaction that exists today, the distrust that Sweden can overcome its challenges, I believe stems from the fact that society has, in some ways, withdrawn. Where society should have been strongest, it has been weakest. Therefore, our project for the next decade will be built on the foundation of a strong society. It is necessary. Since the 1980s, economic inequalities have grown. Some argue that the income gap itself is not a problem, but that the most important thing is the opportunity for upward mobility. They have fundamentally misunderstood. There is a strong correlation between small income gaps and social mobility. In simpler terms, when the difference between the rich and the poor becomes large, so does the risk that wealth and poverty will be passed down through generations. When upward mobility becomes more difficult, fewer people achieve it. If we are to build a strong society based on community, a mother’s part-time job should not determine her daughter’s future. It is probably already understood that money does not equal happiness, especially by someone who has seen an entire family budget collapse because their daughter needs new winter boots. The areas of Sweden that have fallen behind, where the paint is peeling from the houses and unemployment is rising, are the same areas where many newly arrived immigrants have come. It is often the only place where they can find housing, or at least a room with relatives. Therefore, our focus now is on rapid integration, on equality, and on restoring a strong society throughout Sweden. A few weeks ago, it was radical to simply state what social democrats have always said: that inequality, poverty, and class divisions are fertile ground where crime and despair take root. When society withdraws, criminal gangs step in to fill the void that teachers, social workers, and a sense of hope should occupy. It has nothing to do with skin color or religion; it has to do with class and community. Regardless of political affiliation, I believe everyone agrees that these criminal gangs, who believe they are above the law, must face the full force of society – in the form of police, the justice system, and oversight. But that is not enough. We could have full detention centers and life sentences for petty theft, but it won’t matter as long as there are ten teenagers ready to take the place of an older gang member who is sent to prison. To combat this, we need social workers, teachers, and youth leaders who also check in with families, and who will intervene when a young person starts associating with older criminals or falls behind in school. A strong police force, a strong civil society, a strong social welfare system, and a strong sense of hope – that is how we truly combat crime and its underlying causes.
paragraph_summary: The speaker advocates for strengthening society, reducing economic inequality, and improving integration to combat rising crime and restore social mobility across Sweden.
reason: It discusses domestic social issues such as inequality, integration, and crime prevention within Sweden.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames rising crime and the lack of social mobility as urgent threats to the integrity of society, necessitating active intervention to restore stability.

paragraph_text: The best way to build a strong Sweden is through a stable, reliable welfare system. None of us can say, "I will always manage on my own. " None of us can say, "I don't need anyone else. " None of us can say, "I will never get sick. " We never know. And in the uncertainty of life, we all feel safer, braver, and more confident when society is strong, when schools and healthcare are strong, and when you know that if you do your part for society, you will always be able to demand your rights. Last term, we made a historic investment of 35 billion kronor for social welfare. That was important. Now, 100, 000 more people are working in social welfare, including 15, 000 in healthcare. And this term, we will allocate at least an additional 20 billion kronor to social welfare. But that is clearly not enough. It is rewarding to expand social welfare, but it is not free. We social democrats will always prioritize social welfare first. It will cost money, and it will be worth every krona. We will not abandon any region, any municipality, or any part of the country. We need to stand together. That's why, at the beginning of the year, the government will be discussing solutions with the municipalities and regions facing the most challenging circumstances. And that’s why the government is now ensuring that more funds are redistributed to sparsely populated regions and municipalities, through the cost equalization system. In that regard, Gotland is one of the biggest beneficiaries, receiving approximately 70 million more next year. We will always choose to prioritize the collective welfare, because a strong society with a safety net, free education, and healthcare, allows every individual to reach their full potential. And when every individual reaches their full potential, the entire society becomes stronger.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of strengthening the national welfare system through increased funding and regional redistribution to ensure individual and societal stability.
reason: The text focuses on the funding and management of the domestic Swedish welfare and healthcare systems.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a routine policy goal of strengthening the welfare system to ensure stability, rather than framing the current state as an urgent threat or a crisis requiring emergency intervention.

paragraph_text: So, we can build a country where we stand together, and where everyone – from teenagers to single mothers or pensioners – knows that no one needs to feel despair or loneliness. There should be a whole community by your side, working to improve your future. Now, I’m going to wrap up. I suspect you all have preparations to make, or groceries to buy for Christmas. I do, at least. And it feels good to have warmed up for the holidays with something as engaging as the Social Democrats’ party platform. Regardless of Christmas presents, one thing is certain: we can create a better future. And I guarantee that social democracy will always remember its mission: to continue building a strong and equitable society that empowers people. That struggle is ongoing. Yes, it continues through compromises and collaborations, through membership meetings and door-to-door campaigns, through Christmas celebrations and election victories. Step by step. In different ways, in different forms. But the fight for equality always remains. And together, we have a shared responsibility. When others say that the country is divided, that the situation is hopeless, that everyone should look out for themselves, we remind each other that the future is ours to shape, and we can shape it into a Sweden where tomorrow is something to look forward to – for everyone. Thank you.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the Social Democratic commitment to building an equitable, unified society through collective responsibility and ongoing political engagement.
reason: It concludes with a domestic political message about building a stronger, more equitable Swedish society.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive, ongoing commitment to social values and political engagement rather than framing these as a response to an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: “The pioneering generation” will also be remembered for another achievement. There was much to celebrate in Norway during the liberation spring of 1945. We saw the results in the high birth rates of 1946. The large post-war generations are now becoming retirees, and we are living longer and longer. Historians consider 30 years to be a generation. In Norway, the life expectancy has increased by almost an entire generation in 100 years. The fact that so many of us are living longer is perhaps the finest and most wonderful expression of the progress we have made. A century ago, many 65-year-olds were cared for by their children. Today, many 65-year-olds care for their parents. We are talking about a completely new form of aging. Today, older people don't just hang pictures on the wall; they use computers. They meet at shopping centers, bridge clubs, golf courses, and senior universities. Society’s view of aging is changing with the times. And as I discovered when I turned fifty, the view of aging also changes with age. As a society, we must change our attitudes towards aging as we live longer and are healthier than ever before. This is why we are introducing a completely new pension system in Norway. When we introduced the National Insurance Scheme in 1967, the retirement age was 70. The expected life expectancy was 74. Many people received no pension or only a short period of retirement. Today, it is possible for everyone to retire from the age of 62, while the expected life expectancy is now 81. Now, most people can look forward to a long period of retirement. The new pension system ensures freedom of choice for those who cannot or do not want to work any longer. Many have worked hard their entire lives and would prefer to have a well-deserved opportunity to relax, pursue their interests, and fulfill their dreams. They truly deserve it. At the same time, there are many people with valuable experience who are healthy and have the desire to continue working. Therefore, it will be easier to combine retirement and work from now on, to the benefit of both the individual and society. And for those who might be skeptical about the contributions of older adults, look at Egil “Drillo” Olsen. Last year, he convincingly demonstrated that experience is a force that is difficult to beat.
paragraph_summary: The speech discusses the societal shift in aging, increased life expectancy, and the implementation of a new pension system in Norway.
reason: The paragraph discusses domestic pension reforms and changing societal attitudes toward aging in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a demographic shift and a policy implementation as a routine matter of governance rather than an urgent threat to the state or social order.

paragraph_text: Of course, aging also brings with it the reality that health may decline. For many decades, care for the elderly in Norway was largely a private responsibility. As recently as the 1960s, most elderly people had to be cared for by their families. One of the most important pillars of a modern welfare state is publicly funded, high-quality elderly care. We must provide the elderly with the care they need. Fortunately, most people are able to take care of themselves, but no one should be afraid that they won't receive a helping hand. For some, a few hours of home help per week are sufficient. Others need round-the-clock care. The vast majority receive the help they need. Every day, tens of thousands of employees are making an incredible effort. In municipal home help services, in care homes run by non-profit organizations, and in private care companies. They offer comfort and assistance, wash and dress people, shop for food, and administer medication. Thank you for the care and attention you provide every single day! But we must also address what isn't working. By working together, we achieved full coverage for childcare. Now, we are embarking on our next major task: to ensure that all elderly people receive the care they need. Therefore, we are expanding the availability of nursing home places and round-the-clock care for everyone who needs it.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses the importance of publicly funded elderly care and outlines plans to expand nursing home capacity and services for the aging population.
reason: It focuses on the domestic challenge of providing high-quality, publicly funded elderly care in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a routine policy expansion and investment in public services for an aging population without framing the situation as an urgent threat or a danger to social stability.

paragraph_text: Throughout history, women have taken on the greatest caregiving responsibilities, both for the next generation and for the family's elders. Therefore, it is entirely appropriate that we are rewarding women's caregiving efforts to a greater extent in the new pension system. Those women who have stayed home with their children will receive a larger supplement to their pension when they retire. Low incomes and part-time work will also result in a higher pension. This means that far fewer women will receive the minimum pension. Good childcare and good elderly care are important for children and the elderly, but they have also made it possible for more women to pursue education and enter the workforce. What makes Norway prosperous is that so many people are employed. And what particularly sets us apart from most other countries is that so many women choose to be professionally active. People often tell me that Norway is lucky to have oil. That is true, but much more importantly: Norway is lucky to have Norwegian women. And with that, I return to one of my favorite points: we thrive because of each other's work. The more people who are employed, the greater the welfare.
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses the economic contributions of women and the implementation of pension reforms to better support those with caregiving responsibilities.
reason: The text addresses domestic labor policies, gender equality, and the Norwegian welfare model.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes routine policy implementation and social support measures rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: History is full of examples of what can go wrong when, during prosperous times, we forget that times always change. From the 1500s, Spain brought vast quantities of gold from the Americas. This wealth was used to buy luxury goods for the upper class, to provide tax exemptions for the nobility, and to wage wars. Work was undervalued. Meanwhile, in countries like England, people worked hard, producing textiles and other goods that they sold to Spain. This led to the English quickly surpassing the Spanish in terms of prosperity and development. In Spain, it was too late when a royal advisor finally realized the discrepancy between the large gold reserves and the low level of production, discovering that: "Work is wealth. " In 1946, Norway’s finance minister, Erik Brofoss, put it this way: "We must free ourselves from the idea that money is the basis for a decent standard of living and prosperity. Let us always remember that we live off each other's work, and I emphasize, each other's work, not others'. " The answer to one of the questions I am most often asked also lies here: "What will we live off when the oil runs out?" The answer is that we will live off each other's work, just as we do today. The value of the Norwegian people’s skills and knowledge is many times greater than the value of the oil wealth. Simply put, if the unthinkable were to happen and we all stopped working and simply lived off the pension fund, the entire fund would be gone in just over a year. It is work that forms the foundation of our well-being.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes that Norway's long-term prosperity depends on the collective work and skills of its citizens rather than reliance on oil wealth.
reason: The focus is on domestic economic philosophy, the value of work, and the future of the Norwegian oil fund.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on long-term economic strategy and the value of labor rather than framing the reliance on oil as an urgent threat or an active danger to the state.

paragraph_text: Therefore, we must constantly ensure that as many people as possible are employed. By building bridges back to the workforce for those who are unable to work but want to contribute. By allowing many healthy and capable older people to combine work and retirement. By ensuring that those new to the country learn Norwegian, making it easier for them to find jobs. And, not least, by reducing sick leave. No employee should fear getting sick. Sick pay is a right we have fought for, and a right we must protect. But we must do everything we can to ensure that as few people as possible need to use these systems. Primarily out of consideration for those who are sick. We will defend the sick pay system, not the high rates of sick leave. We want a working life that offers opportunities for everyone and a work environment that takes care of each individual. Employers who care about their employees are our most important tool in combating sick leave.
model_reasons: environment: refers to the workplace environment, not the natural environment
paragraph_summary: The speaker discusses strategies to increase workforce participation and reduce sick leave rates while maintaining the national sick pay system.
reason: The text addresses domestic labor policies, sick leave, and employment strategies within Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes routine policy adjustments and administrative goals regarding workforce participation and sick leave, rather than framing these issues as an urgent threat to the state or society.

paragraph_text: It is those young people who started school this fall with great expectations and somewhat oversized school bags, who will build and carry our country forward in the future. We must take good care of that eagerness to learn so that it doesn't disappear. One of our biggest challenges is that too many people do not complete their education. Last fall, I visited Stovner Upper Secondary School in Oslo. For many years, they have struggled with high dropout rates. Now, they have tackled the problem. Every student is seen. If someone stops attending, they are closely followed up. The school contacts the student and their parents, and dedicated support teams are put in place – and the school's representatives even go to the student's home to pick them up if necessary. This, too, is about caring. Just as engaged employers can achieve impressive results by caring for employees who are on sick leave, Stovner Upper Secondary School has achieved impressive results by caring for students who are at risk of dropping out of school. These are our future. We know that the best thing we can equip them with is knowledge. Knowledge trumps everything. It is each other's work and each other's knowledge that we will live off in the future.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of education and highlights successful strategies for reducing dropout rates among students to ensure future national prosperity.
reason: The paragraph discusses domestic education policy and dropout prevention strategies in Norwegian schools.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive developments, successful strategies, and future prosperity rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: Therefore, the government will invite a broad public debate on these fundamental questions in the coming year. We invite collaboration on work, on how we can develop a forward-looking business sector and make it easier to start new businesses. On how we will develop the knowledge and technology we need. On how we can prevent young people from dropping out of school. And about how we can reduce sick leave and prevent people from leaving the workforce. In the coming year, I, along with the ministers most directly affected, will invite people from various sectors to a consultation on this issue. The entire government will travel across the country to gather input. We will also use the internet to invite everyone to participate in the debate. It's about the nation's most important resource: its people.
paragraph_summary: The government is initiating a nationwide public consultation to address workforce participation, business development, education, and labor market challenges.
reason: The government is initiating a domestic public debate and consultation process regarding national economic and social issues.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a routine government initiative to address policy areas through consultation, lacking any language framing these challenges as urgent threats, dangers, or crises.

paragraph_text: A greeting goes to all of you who are on duty tonight, on the first day of the year. It is easy to forget that many thousands of people have to work while others are on holiday or asleep; in hospitals and fire stations, at power plants, or in snowplows, and in many other essential jobs that keep society functioning while others have free time. I would also like to extend greetings to all of you who volunteer to support and help people in their daily lives.
model_reasons: fire: refers to fire stations as emergency services, not environmental fire
paragraph_summary: The speaker expresses gratitude to essential workers and volunteers who maintain societal functions during the New Year holiday.
reason: The speaker extends greetings to domestic workers performing essential services on New Year's Day.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as an expression of gratitude and recognition for societal contributions, which is explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: On the other hand, we must not paint an overly bleak picture or become unnecessarily anxious and pessimistic. Pessimism can easily become self-fulfilling. Therefore, it is important to hold onto all that is positive about the start of a new year. Looking around the world, we see that we are in a relatively good position. Norway remains a society of opportunity. The vast majority of us will have secure jobs and good incomes this year as well. A very large number of people receive secure pensions and benefits from the government. Most have received a pay raise. Interest rates are now falling sharply. This is already being felt. Therefore, the vast majority of Norwegians will have greater purchasing power and more money to spend in the year we are entering than they did last year. They must not become unnecessarily worried or overly cautious. For most of us, life will proceed as normal in the coming year. And when that is the case, we must also behave normally. Otherwise, we risk contributing to the problems.
paragraph_summary: The speaker encourages optimism regarding Norway's economic stability, citing secure employment, rising purchasing power, and falling interest rates as reasons to avoid unnecessary anxiety.
reason: The speaker provides an assessment of the domestic economic situation and encourages normal consumer behavior.
crisis_reason: The paragraph explicitly encourages optimism and advises against anxiety, framing the economic situation as stable and positive rather than a threat.

paragraph_text: At the same time, we must not forget that, regardless of the financial crisis, there are people every day who struggle to pay their bills and meet their most basic needs. It can be difficult for those who are financially secure to understand what it's like to be struggling financially. Therefore, it is important that we constantly strive to do more for those who have the least. One of the things we have been most successful at in Norway is keeping unemployment low. That is now under threat. Unemployment is an evil that affects both the individual and society. It strikes randomly and unfairly. It increases inequality. For those who become unemployed, it is little consolation that most others still have jobs. Unemployment affects our ability to create and our ability to share. Therefore, we have implemented measures to secure jobs and protect the Norwegian economy. I would like to thank the Parliament for its good cooperation in ensuring that the extensive package of measures for the banks was processed so quickly. That was absolutely crucial in a difficult situation. I will continue to emphasize the importance of maintaining a good dialogue with the parties in Parliament to discuss new measures to combat the crisis. This is a time to come together.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the impact of the financial crisis on poverty and unemployment, emphasizing the need for government measures and political cooperation to protect jobs.
reason: The focus is on domestic social welfare, unemployment, and political cooperation within the Norwegian Parliament.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the financial situation as a direct threat to livelihoods and social stability, explicitly calling for urgent government intervention and political cooperation to mitigate the damage.

paragraph_text: Norway's starting point is better than in most other countries. The Norwegian economy is strong. Never before have so many people been employed. Since 2005, unemployment has been halved. We have economic reserves that we can draw upon. We are using them now, because we have not used them before. We can use them now because we have held back in good times. It is a good Norwegian trait. And when interest rates are raised in good times, they can be lowered in bad times. That is what is happening now. As the saying goes, "It is in fair weather that you should repair the roof. " We have done that. And we are benefiting from it now. In addition, we have a strong tradition of pulling together in this country – a culture of working together. The word "dugnad" is quite unique to the Norwegian language. This ability to cooperate is valuable. And we need it now – between workers and employers, between citizens and the government, and between individuals and the community. A welfare society is also a safety net that is particularly valuable in times like these. We will not experiment with our security. These are the cornerstones of the Norwegian model.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights Norway's strong economic position, the importance of fiscal reserves, and the value of national cooperation through the Norwegian model.
reason: The paragraph highlights the strengths of the Norwegian economic model and the national culture of cooperation.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on economic strength, fiscal reserves, and the value of national cooperation, which are presented as achievements and stable foundations rather than urgent threats.

paragraph_text: Difficult times also create opportunities. It is up to us to see them and seize them. For many years, the problem in Norway has been that we have lacked manpower. It is a paradox, because it is good that so many people are employed. However, there have never been enough available hands to solve important tasks. Now, the situation is different. There may be many available hands. More people may become unemployed. And the unresolved tasks remain. If we act correctly now, we can achieve a double benefit: available hands will be put to work, and unresolved tasks will be completed. We will increase public efforts to make Norway even better. Starting today, municipalities will receive extra funds to do more of what we all see around us where we live: schools and nursing homes that need to be renovated, roads that need to be maintained, sports facilities that need to be opened. Scaffolding at public buildings and construction sites throughout the country will signal that we are well underway.
paragraph_summary: The speaker proposes using increased unemployment to address national infrastructure and public service maintenance needs through additional municipal funding.
reason: The government outlines domestic infrastructure and public works projects to stimulate the national economy.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a routine policy proposal for infrastructure and public service maintenance, framing the use of unemployment as a governance strategy rather than an urgent threat to the state or society.

paragraph_text: Dear everyone, It is evening on the first day of the year, and we are entering a new year. The newborns of this year are almost a full day old. In them, we see hope and expectation, as they gaze with their eyes towards us, towards the world, and towards the light, for the very first time. We do not know what awaits them. But we know that the vast majority will live longer than our generation. Every five years, life expectancy increases in Norway by one year. If this continues, when this year's newborns become elderly, it will be common to live to 100 years in this country. In that case, we will live twice as long as we did when my great-grandfather was born in the 1860s. Few expressions of progress are as striking as that. More people are experiencing the gift of life. Societies where people live longer do not simply happen; they are created by people.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on increasing life expectancy in Norway and the societal progress that enables longer lives for future generations.
reason: The paragraph discusses domestic life expectancy trends and societal progress in Norway.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames increasing life expectancy as a positive development and a sign of societal progress, rather than as a threat or a burden.

paragraph_text: In six years, this year's newborns will begin school. We all remember our first day of school. As adults, we have seen the excitement and wonder in the eyes of first-graders when the school gates are opened to them for the first time. They are looking into a universe of knowledge – without limits. Throughout history, we have valued knowledge. In the "Hávamál, " it is written: "Better burden you bear in the earth than much man-wit. " Fortunately, there is much that is good in Norwegian schools, with wonderful teachers whom I hold in great respect. In Norway, we are accustomed to being at the top in international comparisons. However, just under a month ago, we received an international report that showed that Norwegian schools are far from the top in several important areas. In fact, we are below average. This is a serious warning. The government has taken note of the message. We will thoroughly review the reforms that have been implemented in schools in recent years. Have we gone too far in dismantling the established framework for teaching? Have we placed too much responsibility on individual students and their parents? We will invite parents and school staff from across the country to participate in discussions. But above all, we will strengthen efforts in schools. We will improve reading instruction, ensure better teacher training, and provide more teachers with opportunities for further education. We will have more hours in school and more homework assistance. School should be a place for learning and for acquiring fundamental knowledge in reading, writing, and mathematics. Teachers will be given a clear responsibility for what students learn in school.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses declining academic performance in Norwegian schools and outlines government plans to improve fundamental learning through reforms and increased teacher support.
reason: The text addresses domestic education policy and reforms in response to international performance reports.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames declining academic performance as a problem to be addressed through routine policy reforms and support, rather than as an urgent threat to the integrity of the state or social order.

paragraph_text: We also see positive developments in our own country. More people than ever are employed, and fewer are unemployed. There are currently fewer than 50, 000 unemployed people – the lowest level in almost 20 years. Our businesses are thriving across the country. Significant efforts are being made in municipalities throughout the country to ensure full access to childcare. There are more people working in elderly care. And there are increasingly more people who appreciate the care that elderly parents receive. This provides a sense of security. And secure people are more likely to take risks. In this way, new opportunities – both large and small – are created. In Kirkenes, the entrepreneur Bente Fiskerstrand started a call center. In Kårhamn, in Finnmark, the young entrepreneur Lene Wiese, together with her husband Andreas, started Lean Fish, which delivers top-quality fresh fish to the international gourmet market. This is innovation and world-class quality from clean and abundant waters. This is how they are helping to create jobs and development in their region – and with that, a belief in the future. We must believe that things will work out. Without such optimism, we lose our ability to act. And we need that ability to meet the major challenges of our time.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights record-low unemployment, business growth, and regional innovation as foundations for national optimism and the ability to address future challenges.
reason: It highlights domestic economic performance, employment statistics, and local business success stories.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive economic indicators and achievements, framing them as foundations for optimism rather than as threats or urgent dangers.

paragraph_text: King Olav once said, "Incredible things can happen if enthusiasm is used as a driving force. " He was absolutely right. Enthusiasm, will, and initiative are powerful driving forces. We see examples of this every day in our vast country. Tonight, I would especially like to highlight all those who generously donate their time: from parents who volunteer for school music programs and sports clubs – to the enthusiasts behind the summer historical plays. We also see many initiatives aimed at making people proud of where they come from. The people of Steinkjer have decided to make their town an "open, bright, and happy community. " Municipalities in Nord-Østerdal and the Røros area have joined together in the campaign "Move Here. " With messages about a good quality of life, excellent skiing conditions, and freedom from traffic jams, they have attracted 25 families to the region. Strong local pride and good initiatives, volunteer work, and a sense of community make it a wonderful place to live in our country. There are twice as many members in Norwegian organizations as there are residents in this country. Norway needs its volunteers. Without volunteers, there is less warmth, less joy. None of us are so strong that we never need support from others. We can all find ourselves in situations where we need help. And we have all experienced setbacks, or when life hasn't turned out as we had hoped. There are times when we need support and comfort. Neither the public sector nor the private sector can fill all the gaps that arise between the market, public services, and the needs of individuals. That is where we need compassionate people who are willing to step up. Tarjei Vesaas wrote, "Every human being is an island, as everyone knows. So, we need bridges. " We want a society with many such bridges between people.
paragraph_summary: The speech emphasizes the importance of volunteerism, local community initiatives, and interpersonal support in strengthening Norwegian society.
reason: The paragraph focuses on domestic volunteerism, local community initiatives, and national social cohesion.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive social values and community building rather than identifying a specific threat or urgent danger to the state or society.

paragraph_text: Everyone deserves to win, ” writes Roy Jacobsen in a short story. It speaks about the profound joy of achieving something. As the year begins, it’s not just children who dream of becoming a princess, a star, or perhaps this year, an Olympic champion. We all have the opportunity to experience small or large victories, when our efforts are recognized and appreciated. We can overcome ourselves, or external obstacles – in our education or careers. This year, tens of thousands will transition from unemployment to employment, and another tens of thousands will find new jobs. For many, this will be a victory. The victory might also be recovering from illness, or working one’s way out of financial difficulties, or emerging from grief. Achieving success means so many different things.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the diverse personal and professional achievements that constitute individual success and victory in the lives of citizens.
reason: It discusses domestic employment, personal achievement, and overcoming individual challenges within the country.
crisis_reason: the paragraph focuses on celebrating individual achievements and successes, which is explicitly categorized as a non-crisis topic.
