crisis_reason: The paragraph explicitly identifies the ongoing threat of terrorism as a danger, which constitutes a framing of a serious security threat requiring vigilance.

paragraph_text: Allow me now to refer to something that affects me personally. Next year marks the 25th anniversary of my reign. As that milestone approaches, I wish to reiterate my commitment to service and my profound pride in having been able to contribute to Spain’s efforts, in which I see you, the people, as the key protagonists and driving forces, in achieving this long period of peace and progress. As in previous years, I would like to conclude by dedicating a special memory to all our compatriots living abroad, and I wish you all a very Merry Christmas and all the best for the year 2000. Good night.
paragraph_summary: King Juan Carlos I reflects on his twenty-five-year reign, expresses gratitude to the Spanish people for their progress, and offers holiday greetings.
reason: This is a personal reflection on a reign anniversary and a concluding holiday greeting.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is a ceremonial reflection on a reign and an expression of gratitude, which are explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: My dear compatriots, May my first words this evening, on the occasion when we gather to celebrate the birth of God's Son, be to wish you, along with my entire family, a happy, joyful, and peaceful Christmas. Just over a month ago, the twenty-fifth anniversary of my being proclaimed King of Spain was marked. On that occasion, I had the opportunity to share with you, through the Cortes and the media, some personal reflections on this period of our recent history. I spoke, of course, of my profound gratitude for the constant support and the countless expressions of affection that both my family and I have received from you, the true protagonists of these years. Please allow me to reiterate, with emotion, my heartfelt thanks to you this evening. I also spoke of my commitment to being a steadfast champion of the rights and freedoms of all Spaniards, and to serving you as your first servant. I assure you, that commitment remains as strong today as it was twenty-five years ago, if not even stronger. In my address to the Cortes on November 22nd, I also expressed my satisfaction, a sentiment I believe is shared by the vast majority of you, regarding what we have achieved in these years through the collective effort of us all.
paragraph_summary: The King expresses gratitude for public support and reaffirms his commitment to serving Spain on the twenty-fifth anniversary of his reign.
reason: The paragraph contains holiday greetings and personal reflections on a royal anniversary.
crisis_reason: the paragraph is focused on gratitude and the celebration of a milestone anniversary, which are explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: I wish the compatriots who live and work outside our borders, the people of Iberoamerica, and all our friends around the world, peace and prosperity for the coming year. And to all of you, Spaniards, immigrants who share with us your work, your efforts, and your hopes, and to all foreigners who have chosen to live among us, I wish you all the best, and may this new year bring you well-being and happiness. Good night.
paragraph_summary: The speaker extends New Year greetings and wishes for prosperity to Spanish citizens, expatriates, and foreign residents living in Spain.
reason: This is a concluding holiday greeting to citizens and international friends.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is a ceremonial greeting and expression of well-wishes, which falls under the category of holiday greetings and is explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: It is equally important to continue working towards economic progress as it is to maintain and consolidate a broad spirit of consensus on the democratic principles and values that govern our coexistence. To fulfill our civic duties, we must not forget the need to strive for justice and social progress as essential and inherent values of the democratic society we enjoy. Over the past decades, the Spanish people have achieved much for themselves and have also fostered a spirit of solidarity with others, which has always been present within us. With limitations, but also with an undeniable will to overcome them, we have, together, made Spain a society that is increasingly free, more equal and just, more balanced, tolerant, and generous. This is the path we must, and want, to continue along: a path that upholds the universal values of defending and promoting the fundamental rights of the individual; a path that seeks the common good, based on the conviction that individual well-being must be linked to collective well-being; a path that fosters respect and appreciation for others and their ideas, even if they differ from our own; a path that considers peace inextricably linked to freedom and justice; a path that shares in the suffering of others and works generously to alleviate it.
paragraph_summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of economic progress, democratic values, social justice, and collective solidarity in shaping Spain's future development.
reason: The paragraph consists of general platitudes about democratic values and social progress.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on positive aspirations and foundational values for future development rather than identifying an urgent threat or danger that requires immediate intervention.

paragraph_text: Over the past year, I have been pleased to join many Spaniards in commemorating the 25th anniversary of my reign. My family and I have received countless expressions of affection from our fellow citizens, from the media, and from a wide range of social sectors. To all of them, to all of you, I would like to express my sincere gratitude for your encouragement and support, which inspires me so much to continue serving Spain and the Spanish people with enthusiasm. My family and I thank you. May the new year that is about to begin bring your projects to fruition and fulfill your greatest hopes. Merry Christmas.
paragraph_summary: King Felipe VI expresses gratitude to the Spanish people for their support during the 25th anniversary of his reign and offers holiday well-wishes.
reason: The paragraph contains personal remarks, greetings, and holiday well-wishes.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is purely ceremonial, focusing on gratitude for an anniversary and offering holiday well-wishes, which are explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: Throughout this year, Spain has continued to undergo significant changes. The world is increasingly shaped by global forces. Therefore, we need to develop our ability to adapt and respond in order to successfully navigate these transformations. A country like ours, which has overcome difficult situations and achieved historic transformations, can and must have full confidence in the future.
paragraph_summary: The Spanish head of state emphasizes the need for national adaptability and confidence in the future amidst ongoing global changes and transformations.
reason: This is a vague introductory remark about global forces and national confidence.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on general adaptability and confidence in the future rather than identifying a specific, urgent threat or danger to the state.

paragraph_text: As this year draws to a close, I would like to reiterate that the Crown will continue to work tirelessly to unite and bring together efforts for the benefit of all Spaniards. The Crown has complete faith in the great potential of Spain and in each and every one of its citizens. To all Spaniards, with a special affection for those of you living beyond our borders, and to all foreign residents in Spain, I wish you a Merry Christmas, peace, and well-being for the coming year. Good night.
paragraph_summary: The King expresses his commitment to national unity, conveys faith in Spain's potential, and offers holiday greetings to citizens and residents.
reason: This is a procedural holiday greeting and closing remark.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on national unity, optimism, and holiday greetings, which are ceremonial and affirmative in nature rather than identifying a specific threat.

paragraph_text: As another year comes to a close, I approach your homes during this special time of Christmas Eve, to express my affection and to share a moment of reflection with all of you. My family joins me in wishing each and every one of you in Spain a very happy Christmas, surrounded by your loved ones. Our deepest affection goes out tonight to those who are experiencing pain or facing difficult personal, family, or professional challenges, and we hope that these difficulties will soon be overcome. We are deeply grateful to all those who, through their dedicated work, ensure our safety, health, and well-being, now and always. May we continue to advance together in the new year 2004, toward peace, stability, freedom, and prosperity, achievements that we have attained through so much effort.
paragraph_summary: The King of Spain extends Christmas greetings, expresses empathy for those facing hardships, acknowledges essential workers, and calls for national progress in 2004.
reason: This is a procedural holiday greeting and expression of well-wishes.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is framed as a ceremonial holiday greeting and an expression of empathy and gratitude, which are explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: This year, our family has had the great pleasure of announcing the engagement of the Prince of Asturias to Doña Letizia Ortiz Rocasolano. For the Queen and myself, this engagement is a source of great joy, both from a family and an institutional perspective. We are grateful for the numerous congratulations and expressions of affection we have received. I conclude by reiterating my most sincere affection and the firm commitment of the Crown to continue working with enthusiasm and dedication in the service of Spain and all Spaniards. With that spirit, my family and I wish to express once again to all Spaniards, with a special affection for those living beyond our borders, and also to the foreign residents in Spain, our best wishes for happiness during these holidays, and for peace and prosperity in the New Year. Good night.
paragraph_summary: The King announces the Prince of Asturias's engagement, expresses gratitude for public support, and conveys holiday wishes for peace and prosperity to all Spaniards.
reason: This is a personal family announcement and holiday closing remark.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is ceremonial and celebratory, focusing on a royal engagement and holiday greetings rather than any threat or urgent problem.

paragraph_text: I address all of you this Christmas Eve with this traditional message, wishing to briefly share some reflections and to express, together with the Queen and my entire family, our deepest affection and best wishes for happiness during this festive season. We dedicate our feelings of closeness and affection to all those who are suffering or facing serious difficulties. Tonight, I cannot and will not forget those who have passed away this year, dedicating themselves to the service of others; we dedicate our most heartfelt memory to them, while embracing their families in their grief. May the example of solidarity they have given us, and which we so value, serve as a guide for us all as we continue to build a better Spain.
paragraph_summary: The King offers Christmas greetings, expresses sympathy for those suffering or grieving, and honors the memory of individuals who served others with solidarity.
reason: This is a procedural holiday greeting and a general expression of sympathy for the bereaved.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is ceremonial and focused on holiday greetings and expressions of sympathy, which are explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: During this holiday season, together with the Queen and our entire family, we want to express our deepest affection and best wishes for these holidays and for the New Year 2007, a year we sincerely hope will be filled with peace, joy, and well-being for all of you. As we do every year, tonight we want to express our love, closeness, and understanding to those who are experiencing pain, suffering, or loneliness. As we prepare for the New Year, we must recognize how much progress we have made together in recent decades and the immense potential that Spain holds for our future, when we work together. As I said last year, Spain is a great nation of which we can all be proud. We have never before achieved such a long and intense period of peace, stability, progress, and well-being, within a democratic and free system. United, we have achieved an unprecedented modernization in our history. However, we must not and cannot take for granted the advances we have achieved together, with such great effort and sacrifice, even through moments of intense pain and suffering. The keys to our modernization have been reconciliation, harmony, generosity, and the shared commitment to build a democratic, modern, united, and respectful Spain, centered around a Constitution that belongs to all and is for all, a product of the broadest consensus among Spaniards. That spirit of consensus, which made the Transition possible, has also been the foundation of our major achievements. Therefore, once again, I appeal to the institutions and political parties, so that they contribute, through the broadest consensus and with the support of society as a whole, to finding the best solutions to the main concerns of citizens, always within the framework of our Constitution. It is time to redouble our efforts in that direction, with mutual respect and through sincere and responsible dialogue. This is what the vast majority of citizens expect from their institutions and their legitimate representatives. It is not about abandoning our own convictions, but about focusing on what unites us in order to reach agreement on essential matters, always working towards an even better future for Spain. A future that deserves our daily dedication. Let us calm political life and work with an inclusive spirit.
paragraph_summary: The King calls for political consensus, national unity, and adherence to the Constitution to ensure Spain's continued progress and stability.
reason: This is a holiday greeting and a general reflection on national unity and progress.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on national unity and adherence to the Constitution as a means to ensure progress and stability, which is framed as a positive, routine governance goal rather than a response to an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: Wishing you all a very good evening. As we do every year, on this Christmas Eve, I want to extend my best wishes to all the people of Spain and to share some reflections on matters that affect us all. I would like to begin by dedicating my deepest affection and support to those who are suffering the most, or who need it most. I am also deeply grateful to those who, at this hour, are working to ensure our safety, health, and well-being. These days encourage us to reflect with greater calm on the country we have built together, on the progress we have made, and on the challenges that remain unresolved. Thinking about Spain means thinking about the goals, values, and principles that, as a great family, we have set for ourselves, to make our country a better place every day. It has been three decades since the first democratic elections during the Transition. We have demonstrated our ability to overcome challenges, to overcome discouragement, and to undertake, with energy and enthusiasm, a modernization that is truly unique in our history. The Spain enshrined in our Constitution is designed to embrace all of us. It rejoices in our achievements and mourns the setbacks or tragedies that affect any of our towns, cities, provinces, or autonomous regions. Faced with the difficulties of each moment, and with all that remains to be resolved or improved, we must be confident that, together, we will find the necessary solutions, if we commit the will and resources to do so. We have achieved so much together, and we can achieve even more. That capacity for resilience, that dynamism, is what defines Spain today. The Spain whose shared heritage and diversity are protected by our Constitution. Unity and diversity are interwoven in our democracy, concepts that define us and that, when properly integrated within the constitutional framework we have created, have allowed and continue to allow us to progress as a great nation.
paragraph_summary: The King reflects on Spain's democratic progress, constitutional unity, and the nation's collective resilience in overcoming challenges.
reason: This is a holiday greeting and a general reflection on national identity and history.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on national achievements, democratic progress, and collective resilience, framing past challenges as successfully overcome rather than as an active or urgent threat.

paragraph_text: In addition to thanking the numerous expressions of personal and institutional affection towards the Crown, tonight I want to reaffirm my deep and enduring commitment to serving and dedicating myself to Spain and to all Spaniards. Spain today is, in essence, an inclusive and supportive project. It is a great European nation of which we can be proud, for its culture, economic strength, quality of life, social sensitivity, and international presence. It is a country with a diverse historical, artistic, and linguistic heritage, a well-developed structure, and a proven dynamism. Next year, we will celebrate the thirtieth anniversary of our Constitution. Three decades of progress that should underpin our full confidence in the future of Spain. Full confidence in our ability to continue building a modern, united, and diverse country, a just and supportive nation, with greater well-being for all. Let us work with dedication and generosity, without selfishness. Let us think about what each of us can contribute through our efforts to the service of society as a whole. Let us be aware of the value of unity in order to continue progressing. Let us address the needs of our fellow citizens promptly and effectively, promoting our rights and freedoms, based on equality, non-discrimination, and mutual respect, which lead to solidarity. The well-being of Spain and the Spanish people must be our greatest aspiration, the collective commitment that we must reaffirm every day. During these Christmas holidays, the Queen, the Prince and Princess of Asturias, and the Infantas join me in expressing our deepest affection and best wishes to all Spaniards, especially those who are far from our homeland, as well as to the many foreign residents in Spain. May 2008 be, in every way, a year filled with well-being and happiness for everyone! Good night.
paragraph_summary: The King reaffirms his commitment to Spain, highlighting the nation's progress, diversity, and unity ahead of the thirtieth anniversary of the Spanish Constitution.
reason: This is a concluding holiday message and a reaffirmation of the Crown's commitment to the nation.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on national progress, unity, and the celebration of a constitutional anniversary, which are affirmative and ceremonial themes rather than threats.

paragraph_text: Good evening! It brings me great joy to begin my remarks by expressing, from the bottom of my heart, my best wishes and most sincere congratulations to all Spaniards during this holiday season. At the end of this year, as is traditional, I would like to share a series of reflections and feelings about our development as a people and as a nation. The year 2008 was marked by significant events, and I would like to focus on three key areas: the thirtieth anniversary of the Constitution; the fight against terrorism; and the widespread financial and economic crisis we are experiencing.
paragraph_summary: The King reflects on the 2008 thirtieth anniversary of the Constitution, the fight against terrorism, and the ongoing financial and economic crisis.
reason: This is an introductory holiday greeting outlining the topics to be discussed.
crisis_reason: The paragraph explicitly identifies the financial and economic situation as a 'crisis' and frames terrorism as a threat that requires a fight, indicating an active, urgent danger.

paragraph_text: In that endeavor, you can always count on all my energy and on my firm commitment to continue serving with the greatest enthusiasm and dedication to Spain. To serve all our citizens, encouraging everything that unites us and allows us to progress together. A commitment and feelings that the Prince of Asturias shares fully with me and that have in him the best guarantee for the future. On this Christmas Eve and on the eve of the New Year, the Queen and my entire family join me in expressing to all Spaniards our greatest affection and best wishes, which we also dedicate to all those foreigners who live among us. From the heart, to all of you, a very happy 2009, and good night!
paragraph_summary: King Juan Carlos I expresses his commitment to serving Spain, highlights the Prince of Asturias's role, and offers holiday greetings to all residents.
reason: This is a concluding holiday greeting and well-wishes for the New Year.
crisis_reason: The paragraph is ceremonial and focuses on expressions of commitment, the role of the successor, and holiday greetings, which are explicitly excluded from the definition of a crisis.

paragraph_text: Good evening. On Christmas Eve, as I do every year, I address all of you to convey my best wishes for peace and happiness. At the end of this difficult and challenging year for everyone, I want to speak to you with honesty and realism, without shying away from the problems that plague our society. I want to do so with the confidence and optimism that inspire me, inspired by the virtues of the Spanish people, whom I love with all my heart and whom, over these thirty-six years of my reign, I have come to know so well and to admire with such pride.
paragraph_summary: The King addresses the Spanish people on Christmas Eve, acknowledging a challenging year while expressing confidence in the nation's virtues and his own pride.
reason: This is a seasonal greeting and introductory remark.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on reflection, national pride, and confidence in the country's virtues rather than framing specific challenges as urgent threats requiring immediate action or sacrifice.

paragraph_text: There are many messages I would like to convey to all sectors and groups that make up our society. Please know that you are all in my heart and in my thoughts. Tonight, I would like to express my sincere gratitude to so many Spaniards who, in recent months, have shown concern for my health, which I am now happily recovered. During this time, I have been able to appreciate, even more so, the rigor and acumen with which my son, the Prince of Asturias, accompanies me as Heir to the Crown in serving the Spanish people and Spain, its democracy, its rule of law, its freedoms, its unity and diversity, and the defense of its interests around the world. I reiterate my best wishes for this Christmas and for the year that is soon beginning. Let us stand together. Spain deserves it and needs it. Good night.
paragraph_summary: The King expresses gratitude for well-wishes regarding his health, commends the Prince of Asturias's service, and calls for national unity during the holiday season.
reason: This is a concluding message of gratitude and seasonal well-wishes.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on personal gratitude, ceremonial commendation, and a general call for unity, which are standard rhetorical elements of a holiday address rather than a framing of a specific threat or urgent danger.

paragraph_text: Good evening. We have begun a new year, and it can only get better than the last. 2020 was a difficult year. An uncertain year. The year of the pandemic. But it was also a year of shared experience, in a way we rarely see. Across generations, across the country, and among all the different people we are ‚Äì we have held each other's lives in our hands. We have simply never experienced anything like it before. New words have entered our vocabulary: contact tracing, infection rates, personal protective equipment. We have fumbled with face masks and stood in line for tests. We have discovered how much children miss school, and we were reminded of what it means to have a job. We have looked out of the window at an empty street and looked up at a clear blue sky, without a single contrail. We have seen the world in slow motion. As the disease raged, the last tourist went home, the last party was cancelled, and everything became so quiet that we could hear the beating of our hearts. We experienced humanity's ancient vulnerability and found a new strength. When life is reduced to its core ‚Äì to life and death ‚Äì everything becomes both more complicated and simpler. We will tell the story of the pandemic to future generations, just as so many of us were told about life during World War II. What we are experiencing now is historical.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the shared challenges, societal changes, and historical significance of the COVID-19 pandemic in Denmark during 2020.
reason: The paragraph focuses on the domestic impact of the pandemic on Danish society and daily life.
crisis_reason: The paragraph reflects on the pandemic as a past event and a historical milestone, focusing on the experience rather than an active, ongoing threat or a call for urgent action.

paragraph_text: * * * One hundred years ago, Denmark and the world were also hit by a pandemic: the "Spanish flu. " Someone who lived back then described it like this: "There are homes where the sick all lie, and no one has anyone to help them; they have to crawl out of their beds and help each other. " "There are no nurses, and hardly any doctors. " That was the writer Martin Andersen Nex√∏. He also described how "funeral coaches galloped through the streets to cope with the many deaths. " That clearly shows the difference between then and now. Back then, Denmark lacked what we have today: the welfare society that generations before us built. During the pandemic, we are fighting for those ‚Äì for you ‚Äì who laid the stones for the society that we all benefit from, and who stand at the forefront when illness demands its sacrifices. If anyone doubted that Denmark existed, as a community, as a nation, let them doubt no longer. My speech tonight is a tribute to Denmark, to all of you who together make up our beautiful country. 2020 is a year we will never forget. And yes, January and February will be difficult. But then spring will come. The beech trees will sprout. The birds will return. In the summer, we will have long, bright nights. 2021 will be better. Thank you to everyone in Denmark for your efforts. Happy New Year!
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on the COVID-19 pandemic by contrasting it with the 1918 flu, praising the Danish welfare state, and expressing optimism for 2021.
reason: The text serves as a tribute to Danish national identity and the welfare society.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on reflecting on a past event and expressing optimism for the future, which frames the situation as a recovery and a positive outlook rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: * * * We stand together by keeping our distance. Yet, we have grown closer. Throughout society, we have witnessed solidarity, helpfulness, and moving moments on a scale that we have not seen in Denmark before. At least, not in my lifetime. No one has come through this crisis without community. Whether it's the support packages for businesses, the colleague who took on an extra shift, the extended unemployment benefits, or the neighbor who went grocery shopping. Everyone has sacrificed something. Everyone has contributed. We are all in the same boat. We have had to prioritize the well-being of others, each and every one of us. Some feel that the price has been too high. I understand that. But the alternative was worse. It still is. In a time when it would have been easy to divide, we have chosen solidarity, and that is truly remarkable. 2020 will be recorded in history as a year that had so many heroes. In a very special way. Our heroism during the pandemic is also about what we *didn't* do. It's about not shaking hands, postponing parties. That kind of understated heroism suits us Danes well. We are not prone to grand gestures, but we know how to take responsibility and look after each other. It's through all those small actions that we achieve great things. Therefore, tonight, I can say, as it was and is: You, who are watching tonight ‚Äì you have saved lives! It's that simple. And so extraordinary. You may not know who you have saved. It could be a woman you passed on the street, who suffers from a chronic illness. By your behavior, you broke the chain of infection, preventing the disease from reaching her. I know it may not be a comfort to those of you who are grieving the loss of someone you loved tonight. A mother or father, a spouse, a brother or sister, a son or daughter, a dear friend. Every life lost is a tragedy. A story that ends, and must be carried on in our memories.
paragraph_summary: The Danish Prime Minister reflects on national solidarity, collective responsibility, and the sacrifices made by citizens to combat the COVID-19 pandemic during 2020.
reason: The paragraph discusses domestic solidarity and the collective response of Danish citizens to the crisis.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the past sacrifices and collective response to the pandemic, framing it as a period of recovery and reflection rather than an active, ongoing threat.

paragraph_text: I believe that we have now left behind the worst year. But we must expect that the most difficult months still lie ahead. January and February will test our resilience. The season is working against us. More people will get sick. The pressure on hospitals is already high, and even greater for the staff, to whom we already owe so much. The situation is serious. That is why we have extended the restrictions on large parts of society until mid-January. But regardless of the restrictions and closures that are introduced, no matter how many people are tested, there is one thing that is more important than anything else: our individual efforts. We know what works. When we become impatient ‚Äì and we will ‚Äì I hope we remember that the darkest hour is just before dawn. We can already glimpse the dawn on the horizon. Across the globe, 2020 was the year that people suddenly faced the same greatest challenge. The experiences we have gained, the dreams we have had, were more similar than ever before, across borders and continents. Now, we share a common hope in vaccines. It is truly remarkable what the world can achieve when everyone works together. Long live research and collaboration between the public and private sectors. Long live science. The vaccine is our path back to everyday life, to social gatherings, to hugs, to parties ‚Äì all the things we long for. But the vaccine is not a shortcut to lifting restrictions or changing our behavior. Not yet. Winter will be tough. But with each week that passes, more and more Danes will be vaccinated. Easter is on April 4th this year. By that time, the season will again be working in our favor. We will have made significant progress with vaccinations. I believe ‚Äì I hope ‚Äì that Easter will be our turning point. Not an end, but a turning point. Until then, we must hold onto everything we can do together.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister addresses the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, emphasizing the need for continued restrictions and individual effort until vaccinations facilitate a return to normalcy.
reason: It addresses domestic pandemic management while acknowledging the global nature of the challenge and international vaccine collaboration.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the pandemic as an active, ongoing threat that requires continued restrictions and individual vigilance, rather than focusing on recovery or a return to normalcy.

paragraph_text: Denmark is a pragmatic country. We find common solutions. A total of 14 tripartite agreements have been reached with trade unions and employers. Businesses have restructured production to make hand sanitizer. Public sector employees have solved problems under difficult conditions. The public and private sectors have together demonstrated remarkable innovation and creativity. There are so many people to thank. The Parliament and elected officials in both municipalities and regions have set aside everyday disagreements and worked for the common good. Of course, there have also been discussions and criticism. And there should be. But as Hal Koch so rightly said in 1945, democracy is much more than a form of government. It is a way of life. It is not the votes that represent the true strength of democracy. It is the conversations, and the willingness to find common solutions. We have reason to be proud of the strong and vibrant democracy we have. Agreements and collaboration have created results on an unprecedented scale throughout our society.
paragraph_summary: The speaker highlights how Danish collaboration between the public and private sectors and political consensus have strengthened the nation's democratic resilience and problem-solving capacity.
reason: The text highlights Danish political cooperation and the strength of the national democratic system.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on the positive outcomes of collaboration and consensus, framing them as strengths and achievements rather than as a response to an urgent threat or danger.

paragraph_text: Good evening. This year marks one hundred years since Southern Jutland was reunited with Denmark. The nation was healed. The people of Southern Jutland came home. We must celebrate this. Back then ‚Äì in the aftermath of the First World War ‚Äì our ancestors faced a crucial choice: should the people follow the country, or should the country follow the people? Denmark chose the latter. A referendum was held. Denmark was reunited, peacefully and democratically. There is now respect and understanding for both the German and Danish minorities, on both sides of the border. The reunification of 1920 tells us a great deal about the Danish people. When we make major decisions about our society and our future, we make wise choices, with lasting results. And we do so together.
paragraph_summary: The speech commemorates the centenary of the peaceful and democratic reunification of Southern Jutland with Denmark following the First World War.
reason: The paragraph discusses a historical event regarding Danish national reunification and internal border relations.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on a historical commemoration and a celebration of peaceful reunification, which are framed as achievements and traditions rather than active threats.

paragraph_text: Today, we are beginning a new year. We are also embarking on a new decade. Now, we must take the next major steps, and together, create a Denmark that is even more just. This concerns all of us, but it starts with our children. Tonight, I would like to talk about them. On this first day of the year, a little more than 100 children will be born here in Denmark. Congratulations to all those new parents. Some of you have probably already gone home. Others are still at the hospital. Perhaps the midwife or healthcare assistant has just brought a small Danish flag, a glass of fruit syrup, a slice of bread, and some cheese. It is a special day. Rarely has a slice of toasted bread tasted so good. A new life has begun. Our family background is very important to our lives and our future. When I try to understand my own values, my approach to being a mother, my relationship with religion and traditions, my political views, and even my eating habits, there is a great deal that stems from my upbringing in Aalborg. We shape our children. It is an important part of being a family. This is how our Danish culture is passed down from one generation to the next. Together, we have created a society built on trust, a welfare society where more and more people have opportunities that our parents did not have. We can be proud of that. Proud, but not complacent. Even though we are now in the year 2020, and even though most of the newborns will undoubtedly thrive, that is not the case for everyone. There is a pattern: it affects how well they perform in school, their working lives, and even how long they can expect to live. That is not fair. I believe in a society where effort is rewarded. Every individual has a fundamental responsibility for their own lives. But we cannot leave all responsibility to the individual, and certainly not to children. If a child's upbringing determines their future, the pattern remains too strong, and the sense of community too weak. A new decade. A new generation is being born and growing up. We must celebrate and acknowledge those who break the mold. But above all, we must stand together to break that mold.

reason: The focus is on domestic social issues, upbringing, and the goal of creating a more just Danish society.

paragraph_text: We have a particular responsibility to those children who are struggling the most: children who have experienced abuse, violence, and neglect. In Denmark, there are thousands of children who cannot live at home with their own mother and father, because their parents are unable to provide proper care. A few weeks ago, I visited some of those children here at Marienborg. We ate apple pastries and made Christmas decorations. We also took a walk in the garden. The children had a sparkle in their eyes, a sense of energy. We enjoyed ourselves. But we also talked about loneliness, bullying, and what it‚Äôs like when roles are reversed: when children become adults before their time, having to care for younger siblings, with an empty refrigerator. Other vulnerable children have told similar stories: feeling forced to see the parents who have hurt them, while simultaneously feeling guilty about not being at home. Who will take care of mom and dad? We can talk about this with a certain distance, in a way that makes it bearable for us adults. But I will not do that. I will say it as it is, without any sugarcoating. When society reacts too late, or not at all, and when we take too many considerations for the adults, it means that there are children in Denmark who are growing up with a lack of care and experiencing violence. We cannot defend that. We must not defend that. And those children who are already in foster care often tell us that they are doing well where they live now. But the reality is that we, as a society, are not always good enough at taking care of them. There can be many transitions: new foster families, more residential facilities, different caseworkers, changes of schools. Not just once, or twice, but many times. Try to imagine what that must be like: packing your school bag, showing up in a new class, again. Trying to learn faces and names. Wondering if you might make a friend? And being afraid that soon, you might be moved again. That is a heavy burden on young shoulders. Too heavy.
paragraph_summary: The speaker addresses the systemic failures and instability experienced by vulnerable children in Denmark who suffer from neglect, abuse, and frequent foster care transitions.
reason: The text addresses the domestic challenge of protecting vulnerable children within the Danish welfare system.

paragraph_text: My grandfather‚Äôs worldview was shaped by the First World War, by the deep wounds that war inflicted on Europe. My mother grew up during the Second World War. She was from Bornholm ‚Äì eight years old when Nex√∏ was bombed, while the rest of Denmark celebrated liberation. The sounds of falling bombs, the ruins, the fear. That followed her throughout her life. I myself grew up during the Cold War. I‚Äôve visited dissidents behind the Iron Curtain, witnessed Germany‚Äôs reunification. This year, in 2019, it‚Äôs been thirty years since the Berlin Wall fell, and hope for freedom was ignited across Europe. That‚Äôs the historical backdrop. But in recent years, it feels as though the fabric is being unravelled. The peaceful world we dreamed of is in turmoil. The picture is flickering and fragile. I worry about whether all that was achieved in the wake of the wars will be washed away again. The desire for peace, the hope for democracy, human rights, the will to cooperate, international communities that provide obligations and protection, built on integrity, created with the world wars still fresh in our memories. Can we be sure that this integrity and commitment will hold, when fewer people remember history ‚Äì other than as simply history? I‚Äôm not entirely sure.
paragraph_summary: The speaker reflects on European historical conflicts and expresses concern regarding the fragility of contemporary peace, democracy, and international cooperation.
reason: The paragraph reflects on European history, the Cold War, and the fragility of the international order.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames peace, democracy, and international cooperation as fragile and under threat, which constitutes a chronic threat to the integrity of the state and social order.

paragraph_text: Just before Christmas, two young women were brutally murdered during a vacation in Morocco. Danish Louisa Vesterager Jespersen was only 24 years old. Norwegian Maren Ueland was 28 years old. Our worst fears have been confirmed. These two innocent, defenseless women are victims of brutal, Islamist terrorists. My thoughts and my condolences are with the families of these young women. I can scarcely find the words to describe what they must be going through. We are all horrified, disgusted, and deeply saddened. But we must also react by standing together for what we believe in: the freedom and equality of all people. We must fight for our values, across political divides and across national borders. This isn't just about tough law enforcement, police work, and border controls. It also requires closer European cooperation, development assistance, diplomacy, and increased investment in our collective defense. We must stand together for our free societies.

crisis_reason: The paragraph frames a terrorist attack as a direct threat to security and democratic values, explicitly calling for urgent international action and unity in response to the danger.

paragraph_text: What does this changing world mean for Denmark? I am a child of the welfare state, the first in my family to graduate from university, just like so many others from my generation. I grew up in a Denmark characterized by a strong sense of community, a country with relatively small differences between the rich and the poor, a society where the distance between the cashier at the supermarket and the CEO was never so great that they couldn't meet in the swimming pool or at the parent-teacher meeting. Denmark is often highlighted as a counterpoint to the divisions seen elsewhere. In the United States, the gap between the rich and the poor is widening. In Great Britain, Brexit is creating chaos. In France, the "yellow vest" protesters are demonstrating against deteriorating living conditions. Against that backdrop, Denmark is a harmonious country. But self-praise doesn't get us very far. It might feel good in the moment, but it can blind us to our own shortcomings. We must be honest. The trends seen around the world ‚Äì where the great community is fraying ‚Äì are also present here at home. If too many people feel excluded or choose to remain outside, it will create fissures in that harmony.

paragraph_text: Good evening. Between Christmas and New Year, I‚Äôd been reflecting on the people I‚Äôve met over the past year. Two, in particular, stood out. Maisha, who is 19, and Farjana, who is 17. Two strong young women from Bangladesh. They visited me here at my office last autumn. They told me about their daily lives in one of the world‚Äôs poorest countries, and about their fight to end child marriage, violence, and discrimination against girls and women. Denmark supports that fight. So much of what we take for granted here at home isn‚Äôt a given elsewhere. The right to choose who you marry. The fact that women and men have the same rights and opportunities. Yes, even the idea that a prime minister and two young women from Bangladesh could solve the world‚Äôs problems over a cup of coffee. Maisha said about her visit to Denmark, "It was the best day of my life! " It‚Äôs humbling, and it makes you proud of our small country. Denmark isn't a perfect place, but we live in a lovely corner of the world, one of the best in the world. And I believe that‚Äôs linked to the fact that we have a great deal of freedom to live our lives as we choose, with respect for the community. We can talk to each other, even though we are different. We can pursue our own dreams, and at the same time, take responsibility for those who don‚Äôt have their own fulfilled. We have that balance between freedom and security.

paragraph_text: Here, at the beginning of the new year, things are going well in Denmark. Our goods are in demand abroad. There‚Äôs a strong interest in what we do, particularly in the area of green solutions. Many new jobs have been created. Danes who were unemployed just a few years ago now have jobs. Thousands who were previously on welfare are now earning their own salaries. I am convinced that having something to look forward to, being part of a community in the workplace, makes a difference. It makes a big difference. It brings a sense of well-being to everyday life, and hope for the future. We are now reaping the rewards of the difficult decisions we made after the financial crisis. Reforms to unemployment benefits, early retirement schemes, and welfare payments. Now, we need to ensure that everyone is included and that the good times continue. That‚Äôs why the government will resume negotiations this year on another reform, concerning taxes. So that those with the lowest incomes, but also Danes with average incomes, can have a little more breathing room in their family budgets, so that it pays better to work, and so that it pays better to save for retirement, and so that the economic recovery doesn doesn't falter because we are short of workers. We can do all of this while also being able to invest more in hospitals, elderly care, and the welfare system, which is also important for our everyday lives. Thanks to the responsible reforms of the past. Both lower taxes and a better welfare system. I support that balance.

reason: The paragraph discusses domestic economic reforms, employment, and welfare policies.
crisis_reason: The paragraph focuses on economic recovery, job growth, and routine policy reforms, which are framed as positive developments and governance rather than an urgent threat.

paragraph_text: I also want a geographical balance. Denmark must not be divided into areas of development and decline. That‚Äôs why we are moving government jobs across the country. The first phase of that process was launched two years ago, and the second phase will begin shortly. I know that not everyone agrees with this idea. I‚Äôve found that support often depends on where you live in the country. But I believe that it is beneficial for all of us if Denmark remains united.

reason: The text addresses the domestic policy of regional development and the distribution of government jobs within Denmark.

paragraph_text: Things are going well in Denmark in many ways. In fact, they‚Äôre going quite well. And that should give us a surplus, so that we can tackle the areas where things are not going so well. Tonight, I want to talk about one of the balances that I am seriously concerned is tipping, or already has tipped, in some places. I‚Äôm thinking about the children who are growing up in environments where it‚Äôs not the norm for parents to go to work, where money isn‚Äôt something you earn through employment, but something you receive from the government, or obtain through criminal activity. I‚Äôm talking about those housing areas where young people are forced to marry someone they don‚Äôt love, where women are considered less valuable than men, and where the values that those two young women from Bangladesh are fighting for, with Denmark‚Äôs support, are undermined ‚Äì right here in Denmark. Throughout the country, there are parallel societies. Many people with similar problems are clustered together. This creates a negative spiral, a counter-culture, where people don‚Äôt take responsibility, don‚Äôt participate, and don‚Äôt use the opportunities that we have in Denmark, but instead remain outside of it. There are gaps in the map of Denmark. This concerns me deeply. Because those areas also send people out onto the streets, where criminal gangs create insecurity. They reach into schools, where neglected children are on the edge. They drain the municipal budgets, where income is lower and expenses are higher than they need to be. And they spread into society, where Danish values like equality, freedom of thought, and tolerance are losing ground.
paragraph_summary: The Prime Minister expresses concern regarding the emergence of parallel societies in Denmark and their negative impact on social cohesion, crime, and democratic values.
reason: The focus is on domestic social problems, specifically parallel societies and integration issues within Denmark.
crisis_reason: The speaker frames the emergence of parallel societies as an urgent threat to social cohesion, crime levels, and democratic values, which constitutes a chronic issue presented as a serious danger.

paragraph_text: How did we get to this point? I am convinced that decades of lax immigration policies have contributed to this. We let too many people into Denmark that we weren‚Äôt able to integrate. We can‚Äôt change the past, but we can learn from it as we shape the future. That‚Äôs why I support a firm immigration policy. Sometimes, I meet people who believe that the government‚Äôs policies are too strict, and I understand them, to a certain extent. Because every refugee‚Äôs story is heartbreaking. Close to Europe, millions of people live in poverty and misery that we can scarcely comprehend. It touches our hearts, and we want to help. But we also need to use our heads and protect Denmark. That‚Äôs why we have put on the brakes with a number of measures here at home, and we are working together with the EU. The number of asylum seekers in Denmark is now the lowest it has been in years. And that‚Äôs why so many Syrian refugees, whom we have given shelter in recent years, should also return home to Syria as soon as conditions allow. I am proud that, relative to our size, Denmark is one of the countries that provides the most assistance in the world ‚Äì also in Syria and in neighboring countries. I am proud that we send our best men and women to fight against terrorism and unrest. Tonight, I want to send a greeting to all of Denmark‚Äôs deployed personnel. You are fighting for a better world, and you are helping to pave the way for refugees to return home and rebuild their countries. If we can equip Syrian refugees with the skills they need to take on that challenge, by allowing them to work and get an education while they are in Denmark, with the clear goal that they will eventually return home, then, in my opinion, that is the best help we can give them. We need to slow down the influx of refugees and migrants. We have done that. We need to get war refugees home as soon as conditions allow. And those with foreign backgrounds who will permanently remain in this country, of course, must take responsibility and contribute. The vast majority do. Tonight, I want to say to all of you with foreign backgrounds who, with determination, hard work, and courage, have embraced Denmark: You are welcome. You are doing well. Keep it up! But I know that many of you share my concerns about those who don‚Äôt want or aren‚Äôt able to do the same, those who abuse our hospitality, challenge our way of life, and undermine our tolerance. Ultimately, we risk losing the broad public support for Denmark to be an open country for those who can and want to: Skilled foreign workers, talented individuals from abroad, and those who have fallen in love with a Dane.

paragraph_text: There are so many success stories. Here's the English translation of the provided Danish text: "But it is precisely in the sunlight that shadows become sharper. And we must see it all. See reality as it is. Therefore, we must abandon the illusion that parallel societies and ghettos will simply disappear if we just give it time. They won't. When I visit places like Vollsmose, unfortunately, the sense of powerlessness and isolation is the same as when I was there last time. Perhaps even worse. I find that Danish rules, laws, and norms fall short in areas that do not share those values. Private schools, which are intended to enlighten, are instead being misused to achieve the opposite ‚Äì to obscure. Fundamental rights are being undermined by social control. The response from politicians has often been new regulations that apply to everyone. I have come to the realization that a far more targeted approach is needed. An approach that doesn't burden all of Denmark, but instead focuses on the areas where problems are greatest, and only there. First, we must stop patching up decades of failed policies. We need to set a new goal: to dismantle the ghettos entirely. In some places, by breaking up the concrete, demolishing buildings, and relocating residents to different areas. In other places, by carefully controlling who moves in. We need to close the gaps in the map of Denmark and recreate mixed neighborhoods where we interact with one another across different backgrounds."

paragraph_text: Second, we need to reach children early on. There are children who start school without being able to speak proper Danish. These children are already behind. Many of them never catch up to their peers. We, as adults, cannot accept that. For some children, the solution may be to start earlier in daycare. To move them out of the ghetto and into a Danish environment. And we must rigorously and effectively monitor the few schools where things are not working. We need to break the chain where generation after generation lives in parallel societies. A child has only one childhood. It must not be wasted.

paragraph_text: Good evening. In recent days, many of us Danes have been doing what we are world-famous for ‚Äì creating a cozy atmosphere. When my family gets together for that, we watch "Matador. " And we‚Äôre not the only ones. Even after 39 years and several reruns, the story from Korsb√¶k still brings together young and old. I think I‚Äôve watched it twenty times. "Matador" continues to resonate with us because, like any true classic, it doesn't just tell stories about yesterday, but also about today. "Matador" shows that progress creates opportunities, if we are ready to seize them. Those who cling to the old ways decline, like Damernes Magasin. Those who think differently thrive, like Agnes Jensen, the maid, who creates her own business. It was a great honor and pleasure when I hosted "Matador‚Äôs" author, Lise N√∏rgaard, last year. And there‚Äôs a special reason I‚Äôm mentioning her in my speech tonight. This year, Lise N√∏rgaard turns 100! Some say that after 100 years, everything is forgotten. But that‚Äôs not true. Lise N√∏rgaard remembers it for us. Therefore, we know that since the time "Matador" depicts, things have only moved in one direction: forward! We are richer. We live longer. We have greater freedom to do what we want. Today is better than the past. And what about the future? It can be even better!

paragraph_text: Denmark is a special country. And we live in close interaction with the rest of the world. We have a long tradition of that. Unfortunately, that tradition isn't always as noble as it could be. This year marks 100 years since Denmark sold the Danish West Indies to the United States, bringing an end to a horrific chapter in our history. Many of Copenhagen‚Äôs beautiful old houses and palaces were built with money earned from the toil and exploitation of people on the other side of the world. It is not a proud part of Danish history. It is shameful. And thankfully, it is in the past. Today, Danes are fighting against oppression and for freedom. And we can be proud of that.

paragraph_text: Three weeks ago, I visited Danish pilots, aircraft mechanics, and support personnel who are involved in our aircraft‚Äôs missions over Iraq and Syria. And I visited the Danish soldiers in Iraq, where their primary task is to train local security forces. Our deployed personnel are fantastic people. They are doing their part to push back against ISIS, a terrorist organization that has killed thousands of innocent people ‚Äì including children. It has displaced millions and was behind the cynical terrorist attacks in several European countries last year. We saw another horrific example of the reality of the terrorist threat on New Year‚Äôs Eve in Turkey. At the base in Iraq, I had a good conversation with two young men ‚Äì Frederik and Jens. We were sitting on the bunks. The accommodation was sparse. The conditions were cramped in a crowded tent housing ten people. Frederik and Jens talked about their demanding work. But throughout their stories, they radiated a sense of commitment to putting the community above themselves. I am convinced that that very quality is what makes Danish soldiers, perhaps, the best in the world. They are highly professional. But they are also people who can cooperate with other people. Trust. Respect. Cooperation. That‚Äôs very Danish. It might sound banal, but it‚Äôs unique. And that‚Äôs our strength in a new era.

paragraph_text: In the 1970s and 1980s, we watched *Matador* on television. Then came VHS. Then DVD. And today, we stream it all from the internet. But even though technology has changed, we still love to hear a good story. Neither robots nor computers change who we are as human beings. And that‚Äôs also my message to Victoria, one of many who responded to my call for good advice for this year‚Äôs New Year‚Äôs address. She wrote: ‚ÄúI would really like to hear a little about how young people shouldn‚Äôt be afraid of the future. ‚Äù My answer to Victoria and to all young people is this: Don‚Äôt listen to those who say that ‚Äúeverything was better in the old days‚Äù ‚Äì it wasn‚Äôt. Remember the past, but embrace the future. It‚Äôs yours! We must ensure that everyone becomes a winner in the future. Happy New Year!

paragraph_text: Robots are taking over the tough jobs. Supercomputers can make better diagnoses than doctors. Cars can drive more safely, and even drive themselves. It‚Äôs almost dizzying to think about. It can feel overwhelming, like trying to keep up, but it‚Äôs so difficult to see clearly. The only constant is change. Opportunities are growing. That‚Äôs good. But we risk people being left behind. That‚Äôs not good.

reason: Discusses the domestic impact of technological change on the Danish workforce.
crisis_reason: The paragraph frames the advancement of technology as a source of risk regarding social inequality, treating this as a significant threat to social cohesion that requires attention.

paragraph_text: This summer, I had two experiences that have stayed with me vividly. I‚Äôve talked about them before, and I‚Äôm going to share them again tonight, because I believe they show what we need to be aware of. My eldest son, Bergur, had just finished his bachelor's degree in International Business. To celebrate, he invited a group of his study friends to dinner at our home. I cooked and looked after the young people. The conversation flowed easily around the table ‚Äì all in perfect English. They talked about their dreams, the future, and life. It wasn't until quite late in the evening that I realized all the guests were Danish, with the exception of one young Canadian, who also spoke excellent Danish. None of those young people that evening were afraid of change. They were already moving forward at full speed, confident and hopeful, ready to embrace the world. Shortly after, I was cycling with another group of young people, as part of a project I started a few years ago for boys who hadn't really thrived in school, boys who had experienced setback after setback. The conversation flowed there too. One had gotten a good grade ‚Äì for the first time ever. Another hoped for an apprenticeship. These boys also have dreams, but they are often interrupted by almost nightmarish concerns: Do we have what it takes? Can we do it? Will our dreams come true? It struck me how different two groups of perfectly ordinary Danish young people could be. It reminded me how important it is that all children and young people get the absolute basics right: to be able to read, to count, so they can move forward and have a better life. Therefore, the government will launch a special initiative in the new year, focusing on schools where many students are struggling. Everyone needs hope for the future.

reason: Focuses on domestic education policy and the challenges facing Danish youth.
crisis_reason: The paragraph describes a policy initiative to address educational disparities as a routine governance matter rather than framing the situation as an urgent threat to the state or social order.

paragraph_text: If many feel that opportunities are few and only available to a select few, some may be tempted by easy solutions, by stopping progress, by halting new technology. But that‚Äôs simply not possible. We can‚Äôt keep jobs here by slowing down trade with other countries. And we can‚Äôt magically eliminate the refugee crisis or the threat of terrorism by withdrawing from European and international cooperation, which is needed to solve these problems. We can‚Äôt build walls against the world, because then we end up isolating ourselves. We must move forward into the future, take it into our own hands, shape it, and set goals for what we want to achieve. And I will set a high goal: we must make everyone winners of the future. That is my message tonight. We must both give those who are enthusiastic a boost and give those who are worried new courage. And in Denmark, we have a fantastic starting point to do just that. We have free education, a social safety net, a secure welfare system, and freedom, including gender equality. We have become richer and freer, generation after generation. And now, as before, we must find the Danish way forward. Every day, we must set higher goals than the day before, and work together to achieve them.

reason: Addresses the balance between international cooperation and domestic policy in managing global challenges like migration.

paragraph_text: We have a historic opportunity to do so right now, as we are finally emerging from the economic crisis. The government will give an extra bonus to those who, after years of unemployment, seize the opportunities for work that are currently better than they have been in 40 years. We must make the most of these good times. And here, we must learn from our experiences from the last time, when so many people were left without jobs. It‚Äôs been almost ten years. What happened then? A major economic crisis hit. Denmark became poorer. Unemployment rose. The housing market stalled. It ended up with me having to look you in the eye in a New Year‚Äôs address and announce a major tightening of after-work benefits, and later, a major unemployment benefit reform, with very little notice. It was necessary, but also harsh ‚Äì and we certainly don‚Äôt want that to happen again. We must make smart adjustments at the right time, so we avoid major upheavals with little warning. We must take responsibility for that. In solidarity with those people who risk being left behind if we don‚Äôt act. Therefore, we must act.

paragraph_text: First, we must invest in new skills and good jobs, and a secure welfare system. Most people will probably agree with that. Second, we need to find the money to invest. That‚Äôs where we might find less agreement. But you need the money before you can spend it. Anything else is irresponsible. If we use a little less money on student grants (SU), we can invest more in knowledge, education, and skills. If those of us who are able, stay in the workforce a little longer, we can pass on a richer Denmark to our children. If we spend less money on asylum centers in Denmark, we will have more money for welfare and to help refugees in neighboring areas. If we help more people transition from welfare to work, those who are working can receive a tax cut, so that it pays even better to work, and even more unemployed people will reach for the jobs that are currently filled by foreigners. We must act now. We can close our eyes to the challenges for a moment, but that won't solve anything. When we open them again, the problems are still there; in fact, they‚Äôve only grown larger. In the new year, the government will present proposals on how we can safely guide Denmark ‚Äì all of Denmark ‚Äì into the future. And we will continue the good collaboration between the government, the labor movement, and employers. A collaboration built on a strong, Danish tradition of taking responsibility together.
